docs.title: ⚙️ PART 2: SHELL ENHANCEMENT II – BACKGROUND JOBS & & (1 HOUR)
docs.text: Real shells allow commands to run in the background while you continue working. This is what & signifies.

docs.title: 2.1 Detecting &
docs.text: If the last token of the command line is &, we remove it and set a flag background = 1.

docs.title: 2.2 Running in Background
docs.text:
In the parent process, if background == 1, do not wait() for the child. Instead, print the child’s PID and add it to a job list.

typedef struct Job {
    pid_t pid;
    char *command;
    struct Job *next;
} Job;
Job *jobs = NULL;

When we spawn a background child:

pid_t pid = fork();
if (pid == 0) {
    // child setup and exec
} else {
    if (background) {
        add_job(&jobs, pid, line); // copy line
        printf("[%d] %d\n", job_id, pid);
        // do NOT wait
    } else {
        int status;
        waitpid(pid, &status, 0);
    }
}

docs.title: 2.3 Listing Jobs: jobs Builtin
docs.text: Iterate the list and print their PIDs, status, and command.

docs.title: 2.4 Bringing Jobs to Foreground: fg
docs.text:
fg %d needs to: - Find the job by id/pid. - Use tcsetpgrp() to give it terminal control (advanced); for now, just waitpid(pid, &status, 0) to wait for it.

Exercise: Implement fg by waiting for the specified PID.

CTF connection: In a reverse shell scenario, you might need to run a long‑running scan or brute‑forcer in the background while keeping an interactive session alive. Your shell’s job control gives you that.

docs.title: 🧟 PART 3: JOB CONTROL & REAPING ZOMBIES (SIGCHLD) (0.5 HOUR)
docs.text: When a background child finishes, it becomes a zombie until the parent wait()s. We can avoid zombies by handling SIGCHLD asynchronously.

docs.title: 3.1 SIGCHLD Handler
docs.text:
void sigchld_handler(int sig) {
    int saved_errno = errno;
    pid_t pid;
    int status;
    while ((pid = waitpid(-1, &status, WNOHANG)) > 0) {
        // find job in list and remove/update
        remove_job(pid);
    }
    errno = saved_errno;
}

Set this handler early in main (shell). This reaps children automatically, so no zombies accumulate.

docs.title: 3.2 Updating the Job Table
docs.text:
In the handler, mark the job as done, and optionally print a message [1] Done ls -lR.

Caution: Signal handlers must be minimal and async‑safe. We can’t call printf safely, so we just set a flag and let the main loop print messages.

Exercise: Integrate this into your shell and test with short background commands.

docs.title: 🏠 PART 4: ENVIRONMENT & CONFIGURATION FILES (0.5 HOUR)
docs.text: Every time a shell starts, it reads scripts like .bashrc. We’ll mimic that.

docs.title: 4.1 Reading a Startup File
docs.text:
On startup, attempt to open ~/.mysh_profile. Read it line‑by‑line and execute each as a normal command (but without prompting). This allows users to set aliases, export variables, etc.

void source_file(const char *path) {
    FILE *f = fopen(path, "r");
    if (!f) return;
    char *line = NULL;
    size_t len = 0;
    while (getline(&line, &len, f) != -1) {
        execute(line); // reuse your main command executor
    }
    free(line);
    fclose(f);
}

Exercise: Add a source builtin so users can reload config.

docs.title: 4.2 Saving History on Exit
docs.text: Use atexit() or explicit save when exit is called.

docs.title: 🐍 PART 5: GDB SCRIPTING WITH PYTHON – AUTOMATING ANALYSIS (1.5 HOURS)
docs.text: GDB’s Python API lets you write custom commands that introspect the inferior’s memory and registers, automate breakpoint handling, and build powerful reverse‑engineering assistants.

docs.title: 5.1 Enabling Python in GDB
docs.text: Most modern GDBs have Python. Check with gdb --configuration. Create a file mygdb.py and source it: source mygdb.py in GDB, or put commands in ~/.gdbinit.

docs.title: 5.2 Writing a Simple GDB Command in Python
docs.text:
import gdb

class PrintStackFrame(gdb.Command):
    """Print the stack frame around RSP for a given size."""
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__("stack_frame", gdb.COMMAND_USER)

    def invoke(self, arg, from_tty):
        size = 64
        try:
            size = int(arg)
        except:
            pass
        rsp = int(gdb.parse_and_eval("$rsp"))
        rip = int(gdb.parse_and_eval("$rip"))
        memory = gdb.selected_inferior().read_memory(rsp, size)
        print(f"   RSP = {rsp:#018x}  RIP = {rip:#018x}")
        for i in range(0, size, 8):
            qword = int.from_bytes(memory[i:i+8], 'little')
            print(f"   [{rsp+i:#018x}] {qword:#018x}")
        # Mark if RIP is inside
PrintStackFrame()

Usage: stack_frame 80 prints the top of stack.

Exercise: Write a GDB command pattern_offset that automates cyclic: given a crash value (like in $rip), runs cyclic -l in Python and prints offset.

docs.title: 5.3 Hooking Breakpoints
docs.text:
class BreakpointLogger(gdb.Breakpoint):
    def stop(self):
        print(f"Hit breakpoint at {self.location}")
        # Print registers
        for reg in ['rax','rbx','rcx','rdx','rsi','rdi']:
            val = gdb.parse_and_eval(f"${reg}")
            print(f"   {reg}: {val}")
        return False  # don't stop, continue (or True to stop)

Use it to trace function calls without stopping.

docs.title: 5.4 Automating Exploit Offset Discovery
docs.text:
With pwntools, you can run GDB headless and parse output. But a simple GDB script can do: - Load binary. - Set breakpoint at ret of vulnerable function. - Run with cyclic pattern. - Read $rip and compute offset. - Print result.

In‑session: Write a GDB script that sets a breakpoint on vulnerable+offset, runs with cyclic, catches the segfault, reads $rsp or $rip, and prints the offset. This becomes your “find_offset.py” GDB helper.

docs.title: 5.5 Dumping Heap Chunks
docs.text:
Create a command that reads the main_arena from libc and walks the bins, printing chunk sizes and flags. This is advanced but I’ll provide a skeleton.

CTF connection: In complex heap exploits, you often need to inspect bin state. GDB scripting automates that, turning hours of manual x/64gx into a single command.

docs.title: 🔍 PART 6: REVERSE ENGINEERING DEEP DIVE – KEYGEN CHALLENGE (1.5 HOURS)
docs.text: We’ll use Ghidra and Python to crack a simple licence‑key verifier and write a keygen.

docs.title: 6.1 The Challenge Binary
docs.text:
I’ll describe a binary we can create (or use a public one). The program takes a name and a serial, then checks if the serial matches a custom algorithm based on the name.

Example algorithm: serial = name[0]2 + name[1]3 + ... XOR 0x2A. We’ll implement it in C, compile without symbols, and use Ghidra to reverse.

Live walkthrough: 1. Load into Ghidra, find main, look for scanf or fgets calls. 2. Track the input into a function that performs the check. 3. The function loops over the name string, accumulates a sum, XORs a constant, and compares to user serial. 4. Identify the constant and the operation. Re‑implement the algorithm in Python to generate valid serials for any name.

CTF connection: This mirrors many CTF “keygen” or “reverse” challenges where you must produce a valid input based on reversing a binary.

docs.title: 6.2 Writing a Keygen in Python
docs.text:
def keygen(name):
    s = 0
    for i, ch in enumerate(name):
        s += ord(ch) * (i + 2)
    serial = s ^ 0x2A
    return serial

Exercise: After reversing, write the keygen, test it against the real binary, and capture the flag string if one is printed on success.

docs.title: 6.3 Using Unicorn for Emulation (Teaser)
docs.text: Unicorn is a CPU emulator that can run code snippets. Not today, but mention how you can emulate the check function to brute‑force quickly.

docs.title: 🧰 PART 7: GIT BASICS & PROJECT MANAGEMENT (0.5 HOUR)
docs.text: Your shell and tools deserve version control.

docs.title: 7.1 Setting Up a Repository
docs.text:
cd ~/myshell
git init
git config user.name "Your Name"
git config user.email "you@example.com"

docs.title: 7.2 Basic Workflow
docs.text:
git add myshell.c Makefile
git commit -m "Initial commit of my shell"
git log
git diff

docs.title: 7.3 Branching and Merging
docs.text:
git checkout -b feature-pipes
# ... make changes
git commit -a -m "Implement multi-pipe support"
git checkout master
git merge feature-pipes

docs.title: 7.4 Remote Repositories
docs.text:
Create a GitHub repo, push your code:

git remote add origin https://github.com/username/myshell.git
git push -u origin master

Why this matters: Your exploits and tools will be stored, versioned, and shareable. Collaborating on CTFs requires Git fluency.

docs.title: PS10‑1: Finish Your Shell with All Features
docs.text:
• Multi‑pipe support (e.g., ls | grep foo | wc -l). Build an n‑stage pipeline with careful descriptor handling (using arrays of pipe fds).
  • Background execution & with jobs and fg builtins.
  • Signal handling: Proper SIGINT behavior (shell ignores, child gets default). SIGCHLD reaper updates job list and prints notifications.
  • history and ! (repeat last command, e.g., !ls).
  • Redirection >, <, >>, and >& (redirect stderr).
  • File reader loop (source builtin) and startup config.
  • Bonus: Filename wildcard expansion (*) using glob().

docs.title: PS10‑2: GDB Automation Scripts
docs.text:
Create a Python GDB script auto_offset.py that: - Takes a vulnerable binary and input causing crash (from argv). - Automatically finds the offset to return address using the cyclic method. - Prints the offset and exits. Test it against the overflow program from Week 7.

Create another GDB command heap_walk that, given an address (or using the current arena), prints a summary of the first few free chunks in the small/large bins (using glibc symbols). This deepens your understanding of heap internals.

docs.title: PS10‑3: pwnable.kr – “input”
docs.text: This challenge requires you to send input through multiple channels (argv, stdin, file descriptor, environment, etc.) to pass a series of checks. Use your shell skills and pwntools. Write a detailed write‑up, explaining each stage and the solution script.

docs.title: PS10‑4: Reverse Engineering Keygen (Challenge)
docs.text:
I’ll provide a binary (or you can download an easy crackme from crackmes.one). Write a keygen in Python after reversing with Ghidra. Provide a full explanation of the algorithm and your keygen code.

Example: https://crackmes.one/crackme/... (choose one with a serial check).

docs.title: PS10‑5: OverTheWire Leviathan Levels 0‑2
docs.text: The Leviathan wargame begins with a setuid binary and a home directory with restricted permissions. Use your skills to find the password for the next level. Document each step.

docs.title: PS10‑6: Read “Advanced Programming in the UNIX Environment” (Stevens)
docs.text: Read Chapter 9 (Process Relationships) and Chapter 10 (Signals) in APUE. This will fill in gaps on process groups, sessions, and terminal handling. Use the knowledge to improve your shell’s job control.

docs.title: PS10‑7: Reflection
docs.text: In week10_reflection.md: 1. What was the most satisfying feature you added to your shell? Why? 2. How has GDB scripting changed your debugging/exploit workflow? Give a concrete example. 3. Describe the hardest part of the keygen challenge (e.g., understanding the algorithm, handling signed/unsigned, etc.). 4. Now that you have a fully functional shell, how might you modify it for post‑exploitation tasks (e.g., automatic flag exfiltration)?

docs.title: 🔥 DEEP DIVE WRAP‑UP
docs.text:
You now possess a personalized Unix shell that can run pipelines, manage jobs, and read your startup scripts. It’s the foundation of your future post‑exploitation agents. And you’ve unlocked the power of GDB Python scripting, turning the debugger from a manual tool into an automated exploit‑assist robot. You can reverse any binary, write keygens, and handle processes like a kernel hacker.

Next week, we begin networking—sockets, packet crafting, and your first network‑based CTF. Then we’ll dive into the OS itself, building your own kernel modules and exploring privilege escalation at the system‑call level.

Type make in your shell directory. Watch it build. Then run ./myshell and feel the power. The machine is yours.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: Networking is the bloodstream of CTFs. Every remote exploit, every command‑and‑control channel, every packet injection attack relies on understanding how machines talk to each other. This week you’ll stop treating the network as a black box and start crafting raw packets, building servers, and intercepting traffic like an attacker. By the end, you’ll have written a TCP stack client, a port scanner, and even spoofed ARP packets—the foundation of man‑in‑the‑middle attacks.

docs.title: ⏳ Session Overview (8‑Hour Immersive Block)
docs.text: Time (total) Section 0:00 – 1:30 The TCP/IP Stack & Packet Anatomy 1:30 – 3:30 Sockets in C: TCP Client/Server 3:30 – 4:30 Raw Sockets & Packet Crafting 4:30 – 5:00 Netcat, Nmap & Network Recon 5:00 – 6:00 Scapy: Python Packet Manipulation 6:00 – 7:00 Network Security Concepts (Sniffing, Spoofing, MITM) 7:00 – 8:00 Mini‑Project: Port Scanner & Problem Set Kickoff

docs.title: 🧱 PART 1: THE TCP/IP STACK & PACKET ANATOMY (1.5 HOURS)
docs.text: Before we code a single socket, you must understand the layered architecture that makes the internet possible.

docs.title: 1.1 The Four Layers
docs.text:
1. Link Layer (Ethernet, ARP, MAC addresses)
  2. Internet Layer (IP – addressing and routing)
  3. Transport Layer (TCP, UDP – ports, reliability)
  4. Application Layer (HTTP, DNS, FTP, etc.)

Each layer encapsulates the layer above it. Data moves down the stack on the sender and up on the receiver.

docs.title: 1.2 Ethernet Frame (Link Layer)
docs.text:
An Ethernet frame contains: - Destination MAC (6 bytes) - Source MAC (6 bytes) - EtherType (2 bytes, e.g., 0x0800 for IPv4) - Payload (IP packet) - CRC (4 bytes, hardware‑generated)

CTF relevance: ARP spoofing works by sending false ARP replies, poisoning the MAC‑IP mapping. You need to understand Ethernet frames to craft them.

docs.title: 1.3 IP Header (Internet Layer)
docs.text:
An IPv4 header is typically 20 bytes, containing: - Version (4), IHL, Type of Service, Total Length - Identification, Flags, Fragment Offset - TTL, Protocol (6=TCP, 17=UDP), Header Checksum - Source IP and Destination IP

Key fields for attacks: Source IP spoofing (for blind attacks or reflection), TTL manipulation, fragmentation attacks.

docs.title: 1.4 TCP Header (Transport Layer)
docs.text:
• Source Port, Destination Port (16‑bit each)
  • Sequence Number, Acknowledgment Number
  • Data Offset, Flags (SYN, ACK, FIN, RST, etc.), Window Size
  • Checksum, Urgent Pointer

The famous three‑way handshake: 1. Client → Server: SYN (seq = X) 2. Server → Client: SYN+ACK (seq = Y, ack = X+1) 3. Client → Server: ACK (seq = X+1, ack = Y+1)

CTF relevance: SYN flood attacks, TCP session hijacking (guessing sequence numbers), RST injection (breaking connections), and covert channels.

docs.title: 1.5 UDP and ICMP (Briefly)
docs.text:
• UDP: connectionless, no handshake, easy to spoof.
  • ICMP: control messages (ping, destination unreachable), used in tunneling and exfiltration.

docs.title: 1.6 Wireshark Dissection (Hands‑On)
docs.text:
Open Wireshark and capture a simple curl http://example.com. Walk through the captured packets: - Find the DNS query/response. - Locate the TCP three‑way handshake. - Follow the HTTP stream. - Inspect the raw bytes of headers.

Exercise: Capture your own traffic while performing a netcat connection. Identify each layer's headers.

docs.title: 🖥️ PART 2: SOCKETS IN C – TCP CLIENT/SERVER (2 HOURS)
docs.text: You’ve used Python sockets. Now we do it in C, where you control every byte.

docs.title: 2.1 Socket Creation (socket())
docs.text:
#include <sys/socket.h>
#include <netinet/in.h>
int sockfd = socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM, 0);

AF_INET = IPv4, SOCK_STREAM = TCP.

docs.title: 2.2 TCP Client
docs.text:
Steps: 1. socket() to create. 2. connect() to server (IP, port). 3. send() / recv() to communicate. 4. close().

struct sockaddr_in addr;
addr.sin_family = AF_INET;
addr.sin_port = htons(80);               // port in network byte order
inet_pton(AF_INET, "93.184.216.34", &addr.sin_addr); // IP string to binary

if (connect(sockfd, (struct sockaddr*)&addr, sizeof(addr)) < 0) {
    perror("connect"); exit(1);
}
char *request = "GET / HTTP/1.0\r\nHost: example.com\r\n\r\n";
send(sockfd, request, strlen(request), 0);
char buf[4096];
int n = recv(sockfd, buf, sizeof(buf)-1, 0);
buf[n] = '\0';
printf("%s\n", buf);

Code‑along: Write a client that downloads a webpage from any server. Change the port and experiment.

docs.title: 2.3 TCP Server
docs.text:
Steps: 1. socket() 2. bind() to a local address and port. 3. listen() for incoming connections. 4. accept() a client, creating a new socket for communication. 5. send()/recv().

int servfd = socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM, 0);
struct sockaddr_in serv_addr;
serv_addr.sin_family = AF_INET;
serv_addr.sin_addr.s_addr = INADDR_ANY;   // listen on all interfaces
serv_addr.sin_port = htons(9999);

bind(servfd, (struct sockaddr*)&serv_addr, sizeof(serv_addr));
listen(servfd, 5);
printf("Listening on port 9999...\n");

struct sockaddr_in client_addr;
socklen_t client_len = sizeof(client_addr);
int clientfd = accept(servfd, (struct sockaddr*)&client_addr, &client_len);

char buffer[1024];
int n = recv(clientfd, buffer, sizeof(buffer)-1, 0);
buffer[n] = '\0';
printf("Received: %s\n", buffer);
send(clientfd, "Hello from server\n", 18, 0);
close(clientfd);
close(servfd);

Exercise: Build an echo server that sends back everything the client sends until the client disconnects. Use a loop with recv until 0 bytes received.

docs.title: 2.4 A Tiny HTTP Server
docs.text:
Extend the echo server to parse the first line of the request (GET /path HTTP/1.0) and respond with a hard‑coded HTML page. This forces you to understand the HTTP protocol at the byte level, which is essential for web challenges.

CTF connection: Many CTFs present a custom protocol. Writing C servers trains you to read raw specifications and implement them, which is exactly what you do to communicate with a remote flag server.

docs.title: 🔧 PART 3: RAW SOCKETS & PACKET CRAFTING (1 HOUR)
docs.text: Raw sockets give you access to the IP layer and below, allowing you to craft arbitrary packets for spoofing and injection attacks.

docs.title: 3.1 Creating a Raw Socket
docs.text:
int sock = socket(AF_INET, SOCK_RAW, IPPROTO_TCP); // or IPPROTO_RAW

Requires root privileges (sudo). This allows you to set your own IP and TCP headers.

docs.title: 3.2 Crafting a SYN Packet
docs.text:
We’ll construct a TCP SYN packet to a target, with a spoofed source IP (for demonstration purposes only on a local, isolated network or with explicit permission).

  • Build an IP header (struct iphdr) and TCP header (struct tcphdr).
  • Use sendto() on the raw socket.
  • Learn about checksum calculation: required for IP and TCP headers (we can use a helper function).

Important: Linux requires the kernel to not overwrite headers; set socket option IP_HDRINCL.

Demo (simplified): Use nping or scapy first to understand, then attempt a raw socket in C for educational purposes. I’ll provide a safe skeleton.

int sock = socket(AF_INET, SOCK_RAW, IPPROTO_RAW);
if (sock < 0) { perror("raw socket"); exit(1); }
int on = 1;
setsockopt(sock, IPPROTO_IP, IP_HDRINCL, &on, sizeof(on));

// Build payload manually (IP + TCP headers)
char packet[4096];
struct iphdr *iph = (struct iphdr*)packet;
struct tcphdr *tcph = (struct tcphdr*)(packet + sizeof(struct iphdr));
// fill iph and tcph fields...
// sendto(sock, packet, total_len, ...);

We won’t go deep into header structs here, but the knowledge that it’s possible is crucial for later exploit development (e.g., crafting a specific packet to bypass firewall rules).

docs.title: 3.3 Packet Sniffing with Raw Sockets
docs.text:
You can also receive all packets by setting the socket to promiscuous mode. This is how tools like tcpdump start.

int sock = socket(AF_PACKET, SOCK_RAW, htons(ETH_P_ALL));
// recvfrom into a buffer, parse Ethernet/IP/TCP headers

Exercise: Write a simple sniffer that counts how many TCP, UDP, and ICMP packets arrive on eth0 over 10 seconds. This introduces you to low‑level monitoring.

CTF connection: Network forensics challenges often provide a pcap file; understanding how packets are structured helps write custom filters and extract hidden data.

docs.title: 📡 PART 4: NETCAT, NMAP & NETWORK RECON (0.5 HOUR)
4.1 Netcat – The TCP Swiss Army Knife
docs.text:
• nc -l -p 1234 — listen.
  • nc host 80 — connect and interact.
  • nc -e /bin/bash — bind a shell (used in some CTFs to catch flags).
  • nc -v host 1-1000 — port scan (though nmap is better).

Exercise: Use netcat to transfer a file between two terminals.

docs.title: 4.2 Nmap – The Network Mapper
docs.text:
nmap -sV 192.168.1.1  # service version detection
nmap -sS 192.168.1.0/24  # SYN scan (stealth)
nmap -O target      # OS fingerprinting

CTF connection: In many attack‑defense CTFs, you need to enumerate services on opponents’ machines quickly. Nmap scripting engine (NSE) can automate many probes.

docs.title: 🐍 PART 5: SCAPY – PYTHON PACKET MANIPULATION (1 HOUR)
docs.text: Scapy is the ultimate tool for packet crafting, sniffing, and protocol analysis—all in Python. It’s a CTF staple.

docs.title: 5.1 Basics: Creating and Sending a Packet
docs.text:
from scapy.all import *
# Craft a SYN packet
ip = IP(dst="192.168.1.1")
tcp = TCP(dport=80, flags="S")
pkt = ip/tcp
# Send and receive response
ans = sr1(pkt, timeout=2)
ans.show()

docs.title: 5.2 ARP Spoofing Demo
docs.text:
We can poison a target’s ARP table by sending unsolicited ARP replies:

arp = ARP(op=2, pdst="192.168.1.10", psrc="192.168.1.1", hwdst="ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff")
while True:
    send(arp, verbose=0)

(Only do this on your own isolated VM network.)

Exercise: Build a simple MiTM script that poisons ARP and then forwards captured packets using your Python knowledge. Not today, but later.

docs.title: 5.3 Sniffing with Scapy
docs.text:
def packet_handler(pkt):
    if IP in pkt:
        print(pkt[IP].src, "->", pkt[IP].dst, ":", pkt[IP].len)
sniff(iface="eth0", prn=packet_handler, count=10)

Scapy can also dissect entire protocols with pkt.show().

CTF connection: In many CTFs, you’ll get a pcap and must extract a flag hidden in a custom protocol. Scapy can parse it, or you can build a custom dissector.

docs.title: 6.1 Sniffing
docs.text:
• Passive: listen on a shared medium (hub, or mirrored port).
  • Active: ARP spoofing to force traffic through your machine.

docs.title: 6.2 Spoofing
docs.text:
• IP Spoofing: faking source IP (blind). Used in reflection attacks.
  • ARP Spoofing: pretending to be the gateway.
  • DNS Spoofing: responding with fake DNS answers.

docs.title: 6.3 Man‑in‑the‑Middle (MitM)
docs.text:
Intercept and modify traffic in transit. Tools: ettercap, mitmproxy, or custom Scapy scripts.

Ethical note: Only practice in a controlled lab environment. Never attack networks you don’t own.

docs.title: 6.4 Common CTF Challenges
docs.text:
• Packet analysis (pcap): Find a flag in a TCP stream, extract an image from HTTP, reconstruct a file from fragments.
  • Protocol reverse engineering: A server sends a strange binary protocol; you must implement a client to get the flag.
  • Network pivoting: Compromise a machine and use it to scan/attack internal networks.

Exercise: Use Wireshark to open a pcap from a CTF (e.g., from picoCTF “shark on wire” series). Practice filtering: http, tcp.port==8080.

docs.title: 🔨 MINI‑PROJECT: PORT SCANNER IN C AND PYTHON (1 HOUR)
7.1 Requirements
docs.text:
• Scan a range of ports on a given IP.
  • TCP connect scan (try connect(); if it succeeds, port open).
  • Timeout for responsiveness.
  • Concurrent scanning (optional: using threads or select).

docs.title: 7.2 Python Version (Rapid Prototyping)
docs.text:
import socket, sys

def scan_port(host, port):
    try:
        s = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
        s.settimeout(0.2)
        s.connect((host, port))
        s.close()
        return True
    except:
        return False

if __name__ == '__main__':
    host = sys.argv[1]
    start, end = map(int, sys.argv[2:4])
    for port in range(start, end+1):
        if scan_port(host, port):
            print(f"Port {port} open")

Optimization: Use ThreadPoolExecutor or asyncio to speed it up.

docs.title: 7.3 C Version (From Scratch)
docs.text:
Re‑implement in C, using fork() to create child processes that each scan a subset of ports. This combines networking and process management from Week 9.

Exercise: Add service fingerprinting: after connecting, read any banner sent, print it.

docs.title: 7.4 Extend to a Simple Recon Tool
docs.text:
• Resolve hostname to IP with getaddrinfo.
  • Add a --syn flag that uses raw sockets (advanced).

docs.title: PS11‑1: Network Programming in C
docs.text:
• TCP Echo Server/Client: Build a chat between two terminals using your echo server.
  • HTTP Client: Write a C program that sends an HTTP GET request to a host given on command line and prints the response headers and body. Handle chunks properly.
  • Multiplexed Server: Modify the echo server to handle up to 5 clients simultaneously using fork() or select(). Test with multiple netcat connections.

docs.title: PS11‑2: Packet Crafting with Scapy
docs.text:
• Write a Scapy script that sends a SYN scan to 127.0.0.1 ports 1‑1024 and prints open ports (based on SYN‑ACK response).
  • Craft an ICMP echo request (ping) with a custom payload containing your flag string, and capture it with Wireshark to verify.

docs.title: PS11‑3: OverTheWire Bandit 28‑33
docs.text: Finish Bandit. These levels involve git, more SSH, and some scripting. Document each.

docs.title: PS11‑4: picoCTF Forensics & Network
docs.text: Solve at least 5 challenges under “Forensics” (e.g., “shark on wire 1”, “extensions”, “WhitePages”) and “Network” categories. Use Wireshark, Python, and your network knowledge.

docs.title: PS11‑5: Reading – Kurose & Ross Chapter 3 (Transport Layer)
docs.text: Read up to section 3.4 (principles of reliable data transfer). Focus on stop‑and‑wait, go‑back‑N, and TCP’s mechanisms. This will make the Stanford CS144 sequence numbers labs much clearer.

docs.title: PS11‑6: Watch Stanford CS144 Lecture 1
docs.text: Watch the first lecture of Stanford CS144 (Introduction to Computer Networking). Take notes on the Internet architecture and the end‑to‑end principle.

docs.title: PS11‑7: Reflection
docs.text: In week11_reflection.md: 1. What surprised you about how low‑level network communication really is? 2. Describe a time when your port scanner gave a false negative/positive. How did you adjust? 3. How can raw socket knowledge be misused? What ethical boundaries do you now understand better? 4. How does understanding the TCP handshake help you later in exploiting network‑based services?

docs.title: 🔥 DEEP DIVE WRAP‑UP
docs.text:
You’ve dismantled the network stack. You can create a socket, listen for connections, send crafted packets, and sniff traffic. You’ve used the tools of network reconnaissance (nmap, netcat), and built your own in C and Python. This is the foundation for everything from web exploitation to kernel network driver attacks.

Next week, we’ll enter the OS phase—reading xv6 source code and diving into operating system internals. The ability to spawn processes, handle files, and work with sockets will be crucial as you begin to understand system calls and kernel‑level privilege escalation.

Open Wireshark, capture your own traffic, and marvel at the choreography of SYN, ACK, and data. You’re now the operator at every layer.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: You’ve built a Python toolkit, a C shell, a network stack, and exploit primitives. Now it’s time to fuse everything into one cohesive, unstoppable skillset. This week is your capstone: you’ll simulate a full CTF — attacking your own vulnerable services, defending them, and analyzing forensic artifacts. You’ll unlock the basics of memory forensics and file carving, refresh essential cryptography, and polish your shell into a work of art. By the end, you won’t just be ready for Phase 2; you’ll be a competitor.

docs.title: ⏳ Session Overview (8‑Hour Immersive Block)
docs.text: Time (total) Section 0:00 – 3:00 Mini‑CTF Simulation: Attack & Defend 3:00 – 4:00 Forensics: File Carving & Memory Analysis 4:00 – 5:00 Cryptography Refresher & Real‑World Crypto 5:00 – 6:30 Shell Final Polish: Readline, History, Completion 6:30 – 7:00 Git Cleanup, Documentation & Repo Release 7:00 – 7:30 Phase 2 Roadmap & Next Steps 7:30 – 8:00 Final Reflection & Retrospective

docs.title: 🛡️ PART 1: MINI‑CTF SIMULATION – ATTACK & DEFEND (3 HOURS)
docs.text: This is the capstone event of Phase 1. You will set up three custom vulnerable services on your local VM, exploit them, capture flags, and then patch the vulnerabilities. You’ll practice the complete attack‑defense cycle.

docs.title: 1.1 Setup the Battlefield
docs.text:
Create a directory ~/mini_ctf with subdirectories for each challenge. Also create a flags/ directory to store the flag files that your services will read.

mkdir -p ~/mini_ctf/{stack_chall,format_chall,heap_chall,exploits,flags}
echo "CTF{st4ck_0v3rfl0w_m4ster}" > ~/mini_ctf/flags/stack_flag.txt
echo "CTF{f0rm4t_str1ng_l34k}" > ~/mini_ctf/flags/format_flag.txt
echo "CTF{h34p_c0rrupt10n_w1n}" > ~/mini_ctf/flags/heap_flag.txt

docs.title: 1.2 Challenge 1 – Stack Buffer Overflow (Vulnerable Service)
docs.text:
Write a simple TCP server in C that reads a message and echoes it back — but using gets() instead of fgets().

Vulnerable code skeleton:

#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <sys/socket.h>
#include <netinet/in.h>

void handle_client(int clientfd) {
    char buffer[64];
    char flag_buf[128];
    FILE *f = fopen("flags/stack_flag.txt", "r");
    if (f) {
        fgets(flag_buf, sizeof(flag_buf), f);
        fclose(f);
    }
    // Read client data — VULNERABLE
    write(clientfd, "Enter name: ", 12);
    gets(buffer);  // deliberate overflow
    write(clientfd, "Hello, ", 7);
    write(clientfd, buffer, strlen(buffer));
    write(clientfd, "\n", 1);
    close(clientfd);
}

int main() {
    int servfd = socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM, 0);
    struct sockaddr_in addr = {.sin_family=AF_INET, .sin_port=htons(8888), .sin_addr.s_addr=INADDR_ANY};
    bind(servfd, (struct sockaddr*)&addr, sizeof(addr));
    listen(servfd, 5);
    while (1) {
        int clientfd = accept(servfd, NULL, NULL);
        if (fork() == 0) {
            close(servfd);
            handle_client(clientfd);
            exit(0);
        }
        close(clientfd);
    }
}

Compile without protections: gcc -fno-stack-protector -z execstack -no-pie -o stack_svc stack_svc.c

Your mission: Write a pwntools exploit that overflows the buffer, overwrites the return address, and executes shellcode to read and send back stack_flag.txt. Then patch the source to use fgets and recompile.

docs.title: 1.3 Challenge 2 – Format String Vulnerability
docs.text:
Create a similar service that uses printf(user_input) directly.

Vulnerable code: recv(clientfd, buffer, sizeof(buffer)-1, 0); printf(buffer);

Put a secret variable on the stack containing a pointer to the flag. The flag itself is loaded into a global buffer.

Your mission: Use format string %x to leak the secret pointer, then use %s to read the flag at that address. Alternatively, use %n to overwrite a function pointer (like the return address or a GOT entry) to redirect execution to a print_flag function that you add. Write the exploit. Then patch to printf("%s", buffer).

docs.title: 1.4 Challenge 3 – Heap Overflow / Use‑After‑Free
docs.text:
Create a service that manages a simple note‑taking system: - CREATE <size> <data> — allocates memory. - READ <id> — prints note. - DELETE <id> — frees note (but doesn't remove the pointer — UAF). - EDIT <id> <data> — overwrites note with same size.

The flag is read into a note at startup and hidden.

Your mission: Exploit the use‑after‑free to read or overwrite an existing note and leak the flag. Then patch to NULL the pointer after free.

docs.title: 1.5 Defense Phase
docs.text:
After exploiting, patch all three sources, recompile, and verify the exploits no longer work. Write a short report on each vulnerability, its impact, and the mitigation. This report is your first security advisory.

CTF connection: In attack‑defense CTFs, you need to patch your own services while exploiting others. Speed matters. This simulation teaches the flow.

docs.title: 🔍 PART 2: FORENSICS – FILE CARVING & MEMORY ANALYSIS (1 HOUR)
docs.text: Forensics challenges are common in CTFs. You’ll learn to recover deleted files from disk images and extract secrets from memory dumps.

docs.title: 2.1 File Carving Basics
docs.text:
• strings: extract ASCII strings from any binary.
  • binwalk: find embedded files inside firmware or disk images.
  • foremost/scalpel: carve files based on headers/footers (e.g., JPEG, PNG).

Hands‑on: Download a simple CTF forensics challenge (e.g., “shark on wire” or a known dump) or create your own:

dd if=/dev/zero of=test.img bs=1M count=10
mkfs.ext4 test.img
mkdir mnt && sudo mount test.img mnt
echo "CTF{f1le_c4rv1ng_r0cks}" > mnt/secret.txt
sudo umount mnt
# Now delete the file by overwriting the inode (complicated) or just provide the image

Exercise: Use strings and grep to find the flag. Then use foremost to recover the file.

docs.title: 2.2 Memory Dump Analysis (Volatility Framework)
docs.text:
Install Volatility3 (pip install volatility3). Download a sample memory dump (e.g., from a CTF like “memlapse” or publicly available test dumps).

Commands to know: - vol -f mem.raw windows.info or linux.info - vol -f mem.raw linux.pslist — list processes. - vol -f mem.raw linux.filescan — find open file handles. - vol -f mem.raw linux.dumpfile --pid <PID> --virtaddr <addr> — dump a file from memory.

Exercise: In a Linux memory dump, find the bash process, dump its command history, and extract a flag that was passed as an environment variable.

CTF connection: Memory forensics is often the only way to retrieve encryption keys, injected shellcode, or hidden malware from a live system.

docs.title: 2.3 Network Traffic Forensics
docs.text:
Revisit the pcap from Week 11. Use Wireshark to follow TCP streams, extract objects (HTTP files), and identify suspicious patterns. Use tcpflow or foremost on the pcap.

Hands‑on: Given a pcap, extract a ZIP file transferred over HTTP, then crack its password using the dictionary from Phase 1.

docs.title: 🔐 PART 3: CRYPTOGRAPHY REFRESHER & REAL‑WORLD CRYPTO (1 HOUR)
docs.text: You’ve done Caesar and XOR. Now we touch modern symmetric encryption modes and padding oracle attacks — a classic CTF crypto vulnerability.

docs.title: 3.1 XOR and One‑Time Pads
docs.text: Quick recall: XOR with a repeating key is breakable via frequency analysis (Vigenère on bytes). Write a Python function break_repeating_key_xor(ciphertext) using Hamming distance to guess key size, then single‑byte XOR on each transposed block.

docs.title: 3.2 AES Modes – ECB vs CBC
docs.text:
• ECB (Electronic Codebook): identical plaintext blocks produce identical ciphertext blocks. You can detect patterns (e.g., Tux penguin image encrypted with ECB still shows outlines). In CTFs, ECB allows block‑swapping and byte‑by‑te‑byte decryption through oracle attacks.
  • CBC (Cipher Block Chaining): more secure, but vulnerable to padding oracle if error messages differ.

docs.title: 3.3 Padding Oracle Attack (Concept)
docs.text:
When a server decrypts CBC‑encrypted data and returns a padding error versus a generic error, you can decrypt the whole ciphertext without the key. This is a classic crypto challenge.

Hands‑on: Use CryptoHack’s “Padding Oracle” challenge or set up a local server with Python’s cryptography library. Write a script that performs the attack byte by byte. We’ll do a simplified walkthrough:

  1. Given an IV and ciphertext, you control a modified ciphertext sent to the server.
  2. The server decrypts and checks PKCS#7 padding; if invalid, it tells you “bad padding”.
  3. You brute‑force the last byte of the intermediate state until padding becomes valid (0x01), then move backwards.

Exercise: Solve the “Padding Oracle” challenge from CryptoHack.

docs.title: 3.4 RSA Basics (Optional Glimpse)
docs.text:
• Public key (n,e), private key (d). Encryption: c = m^e mod n.
  • Common CTF pitfalls: small e (3) with short messages, common modulus, Wiener’s attack on small d, etc.
  • We won’t dive deep, but introduce the concepts for Phase 2 crypto specialisation.

CTF connection: Crypto challenges range from simple rot13 to complex lattice attacks. This refresher puts you in the game.

docs.title: 💻 PART 4: SHELL FINAL POLISH – READLINE, HISTORY, COMPLETION (1.5 HOURS)
docs.text: Your shell is functional. Now we make it a pleasure to use, adding GNU Readline support for line editing, history, and tab completion.

docs.title: 4.1 Integrating Readline
docs.text:
Replace your fgets/getline with readline:

#include <readline/readline.h>
#include <readline/history.h>

char *line = readline("mysh $ ");
if (!line) break; // EOF
if (*line) {
    add_history(line);  // automatically saved/loaded if you want
}
execute(line);
free(line);

Compile with -lreadline.

Benefits: - Line editing (left/right, delete). - Persistent history if you set history_file and call read_history/write_history. - Tab completion via custom completion function.

docs.title: 4.2 Custom Tab Completion
docs.text:
Write a completion generator that suggests builtin names and filenames. This uses the GNU Readline callback mechanism.

Simplified:

char *completion_generator(const char *text, int state) {
    static int i, len;
    static char **list;
    if (!state) {
        i = 0;
        len = strlen(text);
        // Build list of possible completions (builtins + binaries in PATH)
        list = build_completion_list(text);
    }
    while (list[i]) {
        if (strncmp(list[i], text, len) == 0)
            return strdup(list[i]);
        i++;
    }
    return NULL; // no more matches
}

Register it with rl_attempted_completion_function = custom_completion;.

Exercise: Implement completion for filenames only, using opendir/readdir. Then add builtin completion.

docs.title: 4.3 Environment and Prompt
docs.text: Make the prompt dynamic: [user@host cwd]$. Use getenv, getcwd, gethostname.

docs.title: 4.4 Startup Script and Aliases
docs.text:
Your ~/.mysh_profile can now define functions and aliases. Implement simple alias substitution: alias ll='ls -l'.

Exercise: Add a builtin alias that manages a dictionary of aliases and expands them before parsing.

CTF connection: This fully‑featured shell becomes your post‑exploitation agent. You can add builtins like download, upload, exec for easy remote control.

docs.title: 📦 PART 5: GIT CLEANUP, DOCUMENTATION & REPO RELEASE (0.5 HOUR)
docs.text: You’ve built a mountain of code. Now package it for the world (or your future self).

docs.title: 5.1 Repository Structure
docs.text:
~/myshell/          – your Unix shell
~/ctf_tools/        – Python library
~/mini_ctf/         – challenges and exploits
~/writeups/         – weekly reflections

docs.title: 5.2 Writing a Good README
docs.text: Each project gets a README.md: - What it does. - How to build and run. - Examples. - License (MIT).

docs.title: 5.3 Creating a Release
docs.text: Commit all final changes, tag a version (v1.0), and push to GitHub. This builds a public portfolio.

docs.title: 5.4 Write‑Up Collection
docs.text: Collect your weekly reflection files and problem set solutions into a single document. This is your personal CTF learning journal.

docs.title: 🗺️ PART 6: PHASE 2 ROADMAP & NEXT STEPS (0.5 HOUR)
docs.text: You’ve completed Phase 1 — the bedrock. Over the 12 weeks, you’ve gone from “Hello World” to building a shell, writing exploits, reversing binaries, and crafting network packets. This is equivalent to the first 1–2 years of a top CS degree focused on security.

docs.title: 6.1 What Awaits in Phase 2
docs.text:
• CMU 15‑213 (ICS) full labs: Attack Lab (deeper ROP), Malloc Lab (implement a real allocator, exploit it), Proxy Lab.
  • MIT 6.828 (xv6): Implement system calls, copy‑on‑write fork, file systems, network drivers. Exploit your kernel modules.
  • Stanford CS144: Build a complete TCP stack in C++. Real understanding of sequence numbers, retransmission, congestion control.
  • Applied Security Courses: Stanford CS155, MIT 6.858, CMU 18‑330 — deep dives into web, network, system, and advanced binary exploitation.
  • Specialization: Choose your track — pwn, web, RE, crypto.

docs.title: 6.2 Immediate Next Steps
docs.text:
• Set up the CMU ICS environment (download the labs from CS:APP website).
  • Start reading CS:APP Ch 3 thoroughly — you already have the assembly background.
  • Begin the Bomb Lab Phase 2 if you haven’t. Use your improved GDB scripting.

You are ready. The skills you’ve built aren’t fragile; they’re hammered into muscle memory through hundreds of hours of deliberate practice.

docs.title: PS12‑1: Complete the Mini‑CTF
docs.text:
• Exploit all three challenges (stack, format, heap) and capture the flags.
  • Write the patches and verify them.
  • Write a security advisory report (2‑3 pages) describing the vulnerabilities, exploitation steps, and patches. Include code snippets.

docs.title: PS12‑2: Forensics Deep Dive
docs.text:
• Solve the “Shark on Wire” series (1 and 2) from picoCTF if you haven’t.
  • Download a memory dump from a public CTF (e.g., “memlapse” from picoCTF) and use Volatility to find the flag.
  • Create your own forensics challenge: hide a flag in a PNG file using echo "CTF{stego}" >> image.png, then recover it with strings.

docs.title: PS12‑3: Crypto Challenge – Padding Oracle
docs.text:
• Complete the CryptoHack “Padding Oracle” challenge (under Symmetric Ciphers).
  • Write your own padding oracle server in Python (using Flask or just TCP) and attack it.

docs.title: PS12‑4: Shell Final Touches
docs.text:
• Integrate Readline, tab completion, history file, and dynamic prompt.
  • Add alias and unalias builtins.
  • Write a ~/.mysh_profile that sets up useful aliases.
  • Push your final shell to GitHub with a polished README.

docs.title: PS12‑5: pwnable.kr “random”, “input2”, “leg”
docs.text: Continue the pwnable.kr journey. “random” is a classic, “input2” deepens the multi‑vector input challenge, “leg” involves ARM assembly. Use everything you’ve learned.

docs.title: PS12‑6: Start CS:APP Chapter 3 Deep Read
docs.text: Spend 2 hours reading CS:APP Chapter 3 (Machine‑Level Representation) up to 3.7. You’ll find it much easier now with your assembly and GDB background.

docs.title: PS12‑7: Final Retrospective
docs.text: Write a comprehensive week12_final_reflection.md covering: 1. What are the top 5 skills you gained over these 12 weeks? 2. Which week was the hardest? Why? 3. Describe a bug you spent hours on and how you solved it. 4. How has your mental model of computers changed? 5. What are you most excited to learn in Phase 2? 6. Advice for your past self starting Week 1.

docs.title: 🏁 THE END OF THE BEGINNING
docs.text:
You’ve completed an extraordinary journey. Twelve weeks ago, you were wondering how to set up a CLI. Now you write shellcode, reverse engineer binaries, forge packets, and automate exploits. You’ve built a custom shell, a CTF toolkit, and a full capstone competition. You’ve absorbed the curriculum of the world’s best universities and made it your own.

Phase 2 will take you even deeper — into kernel internals, advanced ROP, hardware attacks, and competitive CTF dominance. But for now, breathe. Take a snapshot of your VM. Save everything. You’ve earned a moment of pride.

The machine will be waiting when you’re ready. Go break something—and then fix it.

docs.title: Week 1: Welcome to the Terminal – Linux & Command‑Line Foundations
Part I – The Big Picture: Why Linux & Why the Command Line?
1.1 What is an Operating System?
docs.text:
An operating system (OS) is the software that manages all the hardware and software on a computer. It’s the bridge between you and the machine. Common consumer OSs are Windows, macOS, and Linux.

In cybersecurity, Linux is the de facto standard. Almost all servers run Linux, most security tools are built for Linux first, and the command‑line interface (CLI) is far more powerful and scriptable than a graphical one. During this entire course you will live in the Linux terminal.

docs.title: 1.2 The Command‑Line Interface (CLI)
docs.text:
Instead of clicking icons, you type textual commands. This might feel primitive, but it’s actually much faster and more precise once you learn it. The program that reads your commands is called a shell; the most common one is Bash (Bourne Again Shell). When you open a terminal, you’re looking at a shell prompt.

Example prompt:

user@hostname:~$
  • user – your username.
  • hostname – the computer’s name.
  • ~ – your current directory (the tilde means your home directory).
  • $ – you’re running as a normal user (a # means root, the superuser).

docs.title: 1.3 Why Linux for This Course?
docs.text:
• Free and open source.
  • Every programming language and hacking tool works seamlessly.
  • Mastering Linux CLI gives you total control over the system.
  • Over 90% of cloud and web servers run Linux – the very targets you’ll later be exploiting.

Our Week 1 goal: install a Linux virtual machine, learn to move around and manipulate files using only the terminal, and complete the first few levels of the Bandit wargame as live practice.

docs.title: Part II – Setting Up Your Lab: VirtualBox & Ubuntu
2.1 Virtualisation Concepts
docs.text:
A virtual machine (VM) is a simulated computer that runs inside your real physical computer. The host is your actual OS (Windows/macOS), and the guest is the Linux system we will install. The software that manages VMs is called a hypervisor; we’ll use VirtualBox, which is free and works on all major platforms.

We will install Ubuntu Desktop, the most beginner‑friendly Linux distribution. Even though the desktop has a graphical interface, we will do almost everything in the terminal.

docs.title: 2.2.1 Download VirtualBox
docs.text: Go to virtualbox.org and download the installer for your host OS (Windows/macOS/Linux). Install it with default options.

docs.title: 2.2.2 Download Ubuntu ISO
docs.text: Visit ubuntu.com/download/desktop. Download the latest LTS (Long Term Support) version (e.g., 24.04 LTS). The .iso file is about 5 GB.

docs.title: 2.2.3 Create a New Virtual Machine
docs.text:
1. Open VirtualBox, click New.
  2. Name: WebExploitLab, Type: Linux, Version: Ubuntu (64-bit).
  3. Memory: assign at least 2048 MB (2 GB). If your host has 8 GB RAM, give 4096 MB for better performance. Do not exceed half your total RAM.
  4. Hard disk: choose “Create a virtual hard disk now”. Use VDI (VirtualBox Disk Image), dynamically allocated. Size: 25 GB minimum (the disk grows as needed, it won’t use 25 GB immediately).
  5. Click Create.

docs.title: 2.2.4 Attach the Ubuntu ISO
docs.text: Select your new VM, click Settings → Storage. Under the “Controller: IDE” there’s an empty optical drive (shows “Empty”). Click it, then on the right side click the little disk icon and choose “Choose a disk file…”. Select the Ubuntu ISO you downloaded. Click OK.

docs.title: 2.2.5 Start the VM and Install Ubuntu
docs.text:
1. Click Start.
  2. The VM boots from the ISO. Choose “Try or Install Ubuntu”.
  3. Select your language, then “Install Ubuntu”.
  4. Keyboard layout: usually “English (US)”.
  5. Updates and other software: choose “Normal installation”. Check “Download updates while installing Ubuntu” and “Install third‑party software…”.
  6. Installation type: choose “Erase disk and install Ubuntu” (it only erases the virtual disk, not your host, so it’s safe).
  7. Time zone: select yours.
  8. Create your account: Name: student, username: student, password: choose a simple one you’ll remember (this is a lab). Important: select “Log in automatically” for convenience.
  9. Wait for installation to complete. Then click “Restart Now”. When it prompts you to “Please remove the installation medium, then press ENTER”, just press Enter; the virtual optical disk is automatically removed.

After reboot, you should see the Ubuntu desktop. Congratulations, you have a Linux machine!

docs.title: 2.3 First Boot & Updates
docs.text:
Open the terminal: press Ctrl+Alt+T. A black window with white text appears – this is your new home.

Update the system’s package list and upgrade all software:

sudo apt update
sudo apt upgrade -y

You’ll be prompted for your password; type it (nothing shows on screen) and press Enter. Wait a few minutes. This ensures all tools are current.

docs.title: Part III – The Linux Filesystem and Basic Navigation
3.1 The Tree Structure
docs.text:
Linux uses a single tree, starting at the root directory /. Everything – hard drives, USB sticks, folders – is under /. Your personal files live in /home/student (your home directory), abbreviated as ~.

Key directories: - /home – user home folders. - /etc – system configuration files. - /var – variable data like logs. - /tmp – temporary files, wiped on reboot. - /usr – user‑installed software.

docs.title: 3.2 The Command Prompt
docs.text:
After opening the terminal, you see:

student@WebExploitLab:~$

This teaches you three things: who you are, where you are, and that you’re a normal user.

docs.title: pwd – Print Working Directory
docs.text:
Shows your current location.

$ pwd
/home/student

docs.title: ls – List files and directories
docs.text:
$ ls
Desktop  Documents  Downloads  Music  Pictures  Videos

Common options: - ls -l : long listing (permissions, owner, size, date). - ls -a : show all files, including hidden ones (names starting with .). - ls -la : combination.

Try:

$ ls -la ~

You’ll see entries like ., .., .bashrc, .profile etc. The . is the current directory, .. is the parent directory.

docs.title: cd – Change Directory
docs.text: bash cd /etc # absolute path (starts from /) cd .. # go up one level cd ~ # go home cd - # go to previous directory cd # alone, goes home Practice: bash cd /var/log pwd ls cd ~ pwd

docs.title: 3.4 Help! The man Command
docs.text:
Linux has built‑in manuals. To learn about any command, type `man <command>`. For example:
```bash
man ls
```
Navigate with arrow keys, `q` quits. Read the description and some options. You don’t need to memorise everything; `man` is always there.

---

docs.title: 4.1 cat – Concatenate and Display Files
docs.text:
Displays the contents of a file on the screen.
```bash
cat /etc/hostname
```
Useful for quick peeks. Later we’ll use it to read password files.

docs.title: 4.2 echo – Print a Line of Text
docs.text:
```bash
echo "Hello, hacker!"
```
Often used with redirection to create small files.

docs.title: 4.3 Redirection > and >>
docs.text:
- `>` : overwrites a file with output.
- `>>` : appends to a file.

Example:
```bash
echo "My first file" > myfile.txt
cat myfile.txt
echo "Adding a line" >> myfile.txt
cat myfile.txt
```

docs.title: 4.4 mkdir – Make Directories
docs.text:
```bash
mkdir myfolder
mkdir -p parent/child/grandchild    # creates intermediate folders
```

docs.title: 4.5 rm – Remove Files and Directories (Be Careful!)
docs.text:
```bash
rm myfile.txt           # delete a file
rm -r myfolder          # delete a folder and its contents recursively
rm -rf dangerous/       # force delete without asking – can be catastrophic if used incorrectly
```
**Golden rule:** there is no trash can in the terminal; deleted is deleted. Double‑check before pressing Enter.

docs.title: 4.6 cp – Copy
docs.text:
```bash
cp sourcefile destinationfile
cp -r sourcedir/ destdir/       # copy directories recursively
```

docs.title: 4.7 mv – Move or Rename
docs.text:
```bash
mv oldname newname      # rename
mv file.txt ~/Documents # move
```

docs.title: 4.8 Text Editors: nano
docs.text:
While `vim` is powerful, `nano` is simple for beginners.
```bash
nano mytext.txt
```
Type something, then `Ctrl+X` to exit, `Y` to save, `Enter` to confirm filename.

---

docs.title: Part V – Hands‑On Practice: OverTheWire Bandit
docs.text: Bandit is a free online wargame that teaches Linux through escalating challenges. You connect via SSH (Secure Shell) to their server and find passwords to advance.

docs.title: 5.1 SSH Basics
docs.text:
SSH allows secure remote login. The command is:
```
ssh username@hostname -p port
```
By default port is 22; Bandit often uses 2220 (check the level page). The first time you connect, it asks to verify the host key – type `yes`.

docs.title: 5.2 Bandit Level 0 → Level 1
docs.text:
**Goal:** The password for level 1 is stored in a file called `readme` in the home directory. Log into the server and read it.

**Connection info** (from [overthewire.org/bandit](https://overthewire.org/wargames/bandit/bandit0.html)):
- Host: `bandit.labs.overthewire.org`
- Port: `2220`
- Username: `bandit0`
- Password: `bandit0`

Open your local terminal and run:
```bash
ssh bandit0@bandit.labs.overthewire.org -p 2220
```
Enter the password `bandit0` (no output while typing). After logging in, you see a banner and a prompt.

Now do:
```bash
ls
```
You’ll see one file: `readme`.
```bash
cat readme
```
You get a string like `NH2SXQwcBdpmTEzi3bvBHMM9H66vVXjL`. That’s the password for level 1.

Write it down (or better, in a notes file on your VM). To log out, type `exit`.

**Test the password:** Connect to bandit1:
```bash
ssh bandit1@bandit.labs.overthewire.org -p 2220
```
Password is the one you just retrieved. If you succeed, move on.

**Why this matters:** You used a remote terminal, `ls`, `cat` to retrieve sensitive data – a very real scenario in web exploitation where you might read server files after gaining shell access.

docs.title: 5.3 Bandit Level 1 → Level 2
docs.text:
**Goal:** Password in a file named `-` in the home directory.

The dash `-` is a special character: many commands interpret it as standard input/output. If you try `cat -`, the shell expects input, not a file. Solutions:
- Specify the path explicitly: `cat ./-` (current directory relative path).
- Use redirection: `cat < -`.
- Use `cat ./-`.

Connect as bandit1:
```bash
ssh bandit1@bandit.labs.overthewire.org -p 2220
password: (from level0)
```
Inside:
```bash
ls
```
Sees `-`.
```bash
cat ./- 
```
Outputs something like `rRGizSaX8Mk1RTb1CNQoXTcYZWU6lgzi`. That’s level2 password. Exit and record.

docs.title: 5.4 Bandit Level 2 → Level 3
docs.text:
**Goal:** Password in a file called `spaces in this filename` in the home directory.

Filenames with spaces can be handled by:
- Tab completion: type `cat sp` then press `Tab`; the shell auto‑escapes it.
- Quote the name: `cat "spaces in this filename"`.
- Escape with backslash: `cat spaces\ in\ this\ filename`.

Connect as bandit2 (password from level1). `ls` shows `spaces in this filename`.
```bash
cat "spaces in this filename"
```
Retrieve next password.

docs.title: 5.5 Bandit Level 3 → Level 4
docs.text:
**Goal:** Password in a hidden file in the `inhere` directory.

Hidden files start with `.`. Use `ls -a` to see them.

Connect as bandit3.
```bash
ls
```
Sees `inhere`.
```bash
cd inhere
ls
```
Nothing? Because there’s a hidden file.
```bash
ls -a
```
You see `...` and a file named `.hidden`.
```bash
cat .hidden
```
Get password for level4.

docs.title: 5.6 Bandit Level 4 → Level 5
docs.text:
**Goal:** Password in the only human‑readable file in the `inhere` directory. Inside you’ll find 10 files (`-file00` to `-file09`). Some contain binary data, one contains the password as a text string.

First, connect as bandit4.
```bash
ls
cd inhere
ls
```
You see `-file00` ... `-file09`. These start with `-`, so command like `cat -file00` won’t work; use `./-file00`.

We need to find which file is human‑readable (text). Use the `file` command to check file types:
```bash
file ./*
         ```
         You’ll see output like:
         ```
         ./-file00: data
         ./-file01: data
         ./-file02: ASCII text
         ...
         ```
         Only one will show `ASCII text` (or `Unicode text`). That file contains the password.

         Another method: use the `strings` command to print readable strings, or use `grep` to skip binary files. But `file` is simplest.

         ```bash
         file ./*
         ```
         Identify the text file (say `-file07`). Then:
         ```bash
         cat ./-file07
         ```
         And you’re through.

         **Logout** from the server (`exit`). Now you’ve completed Bandit levels 0‑4, demonstrating core terminal skills.

         ---

docs.title: Part VI – Week 1 Wrap‑Up and Verification
6.1 Milestone Checklist
docs.text:
Test yourself without notes: - [ ] Start the VM, open a terminal, run sudo apt update. - [ ] Navigate from / to /home/student/Documents and back to ~ using only cd. - [ ] List all files (including hidden) in your home directory. - [ ] Create a directory named Week1 and inside it a file notes.txt containing “I love Linux”. - [ ] Copy the file to notes_backup.txt, then delete the backup. - [ ] Rename notes.txt to command_notes.txt. - [ ] Connect to Bandit level 4, read the password, and explain why the - in filenames is tricky. - [ ] Predict what cat ./- does and why.

If you stumble, re‑do the relevant section. Mastery at this stage is all about repetition.

docs.title: 6.2 Pro Tip: Keep a Personal Notes Repository
docs.text: Create a directory called ~/cybernotes and inside a file week1.md. Write a quick summary of what you learned, every command with a brief example. This becomes a powerful reference.

docs.title: 6.3 What’s Next?
docs.text:
Next week (Week 2) we will build on this foundation: - File permissions, chmod, users/groups. - More advanced commands: grep, find, pipes and filters. - Bandit levels 5‑10.

But first, ensure you can navigate and manipulate files without a graphical interface – that is the true milestone of Week 1.

This concludes the comprehensive Week 1 deep dive. Everything from setting up your virtual environment to solving real‑world Linux puzzles is now at your fingertips. When you’re confident, type exit one last time and prepare for Week 2. I’ll be here when you’re ready to move on.

docs.title: Week 2: Power User Essentials – Permissions, Pipes, Grep, Find, and Bandit 5–10
docs.text:
This week you’ll move from a terminal tourist to a terminal resident. We will cover Linux file permissions and ownership, how to change them (chmod, chown), how to read them, then we’ll dive into the pipe operator and powerful text filtering with grep, plus the find command to search for files by almost any attribute. You will then apply all of this by completing Bandit levels 5 through 10, each designed to force you to use these new skills.

By the end of this week you should be able to comfortably locate any file on a system based on its name, size, permissions, owner, or content, and chain small commands together to solve problems. That’s the heart of a power user.

docs.title: Part I – The Linux Permission System
1.1 Why Permissions Matter
docs.text: Linux is a multi‑user system. The kernel must decide who can read, write, or execute every single file and directory. In web exploitation, misconfigured file permissions often allow an attacker to read sensitive configuration files (e.g., database credentials) or even execute arbitrary code (if a file is world‑writable and executable). Understanding permissions is therefore non‑negotiable.

docs.title: 1.2 Reading Permissions with ls -l
docs.text:
When you run ls -l, each line looks like this:

-rw-r--r-- 1 student student 1234 Jan 1 12:00 myfile.txt

Breakdown: - The first character is the file type: - - = regular file - d = directory - l = symbolic link - The next nine characters are three sets of permission triads: - First triad (rw-): user (owner) permissions - Second triad (r--): group permissions - Third triad (r--): others (world) permissions - Each triad can be r (read), w (write), x (execute), or - (absent).

In the example: - Owner student can read and write, but not execute. - Members of the group student can only read. - Everyone else can only read.

For a directory, the permissions are interpreted differently: - r – allows listing the directory’s contents (e.g., ls). - w – allows creating, deleting, or renaming files inside (even if the file itself is not writable). - x – allows entering the directory (cd) and accessing files inside if you know their names.

Commonly, directories need x to be usable.

docs.title: 1.3 Octal and Symbolic Notation
docs.text:
Permissions can be described in two ways:

1. Symbolic – using letters: u (user/owner), g (group), o (others), a (all). Operators: + (add), - (remove), = (set exactly). Examples: - chmod u+x script.sh – add execute for owner. - chmod go-w file – remove write for group and others.

2. Octal (numeric) – each permission has a value: - r = 4 - w = 2 - x = 1

Sum them for each triad. So rwx = 7, rw- = 6, r-- = 4, etc. Three digits for owner, group, others: - chmod 755 file → owner: rwx (7), group: r-x (5), others: r-x (5) - chmod 644 file → owner: rw- (6), group: r-- (4), others: r-- (4)

Octal is faster and often used in documentation.

docs.title: 1.4 Changing Permissions with chmod
docs.text:
Experiment on your VM:

cd ~
touch testfile
ls -l testfile           # default: -rw-rw-r-- (or 664) on Ubuntu

Now make it executable only by owner:

chmod 744 testfile
ls -l testfile           # -rwxr--r--

Remove write from group and others using symbolic:

chmod go-w testfile
ls -l testfile           # -rwxr-xr--? wait, we removed w, so it becomes r-x? Actually if it was 744 (rwxr--r--), remove w from go: g had r--, o had r--, so no effect. Let's try with a different starting point.

Better: change to 644, then add execute for user:

chmod 644 testfile
chmod u+x testfile
ls -l                    # -rwxr--r--

Practice a few combinations until the output of ls -l makes instant sense.

docs.title: 1.5 Ownership: chown and chgrp
docs.text:
• chown changes file owner (and optionally group): sudo chown newowner file
  • chgrp changes group: chgrp newgroup file

Example:

sudo chown root testfile          # change owner to root (requires sudo)
ls -l testfile                    # -rwxr--r-- 1 root student ...
sudo chown student:adm testfile   # owner:student, group:adm

Because we are in a learning environment, you can use sudo with caution. Notice that only root can change a file’s owner; for group, the owner can change it to a group they are a member of.

docs.title: 1.6 Special Permission Bits (Brief Intro)
docs.text:
Beyond the basic 9 bits, there are three special ones you’ll eventually see: - setuid (4###): execute file as the file owner (e.g., /usr/bin/passwd). - setgid (2###): execute as the group, or on directories, new files inherit the group. - sticky bit (1###): on directories, only the file’s owner can delete it (like /tmp).

These are represented by a fourth octal digit at the front, or by s/t in ls -l (e.g., rwsr-xr-x). Not vital this week, but be aware they exist.

docs.title: 2.1 Who Are You?
docs.text:
• whoami – prints your username.
  • id – shows your uid, gid, and all group memberships.

Example:

$ id
uid=1000(student) gid=1000(student) groups=1000(student),4(adm),27(sudo),...

This tells you that you belong to sudo group, which gives you the power to run administrative commands if you use sudo.

docs.title: 2.2 User Accounts Files
docs.text:
• /etc/passwd – user account info (one line per user): username:x:UID:GID:comment:home:shell
  • /etc/shadow – hashed passwords (only readable by root).
  • /etc/group – group definitions.

Try:

cat /etc/passwd

Look for student and root. Note the UID 0 for root.

docs.title: 2.3 sudo and Privilege Escalation
docs.text:
sudo allows a permitted user to run a command as another user (default root). You’ll need it to install packages or modify system files. The first user you created during Ubuntu installation is automatically in the sudo group.

Use it carefully; sudo rm -rf / destroys your system.

docs.title: 2.4 Switching Users: su
docs.text:
To become another user in the shell:

su username

You’ll need that user’s password. To become root:

sudo su -

(The - starts a login shell, which sets the environment correctly.) However, for our labs, sudo before individual commands is safer.

docs.title: Part III – The Power Tools: Pipes, grep, and find
docs.text: Now we combine small, specialised commands to do complex things. This is the Unix philosophy.

docs.title: 3.1 Standard Streams and Redirection (Revisited)
docs.text:
Every command has three default streams: - stdin (0) – input. - stdout (1) – normal output. - stderr (2) – error messages.

You already used > and >> to redirect stdout to a file. To redirect stderr:

command 2> error.log

To redirect both:

command > output.log 2>&1

docs.title: 3.2 Pipes |
docs.text:
The pipe takes the stdout of one command and feeds it as stdin to the next. This is the chain that builds powerful one‑liners.

Example:

ls -la /etc | less

Now you can scroll through the list.

docs.title: 3.3 grep – Global Regular Expression Print
docs.text:
grep searches text for patterns. It’s indispensable.

Basic usage:

grep "pattern" filename

But its true strength comes from reading from stdin via pipes.

Common options: - -i : case insensitive. - -v : invert match (show lines not containing pattern). - -r or -R : recursive search in directories. - -n : show line numbers. - -l : list only filenames containing the match. - -E : extended regular expressions (so you can use +, ?, | etc.). Alternatively use egrep.

Examples:

# Find lines containing 'error' in syslog (if exists)
grep -i error /var/log/syslog

# List all files in /etc that contain the string '127.0.0.1'
grep -rl '127.0.0.1' /etc

# Count how many times 'student' appears in /etc/passwd
grep -c 'student' /etc/passwd

docs.title: 3.4 Regular Expressions Crash Course
docs.text:
grep uses basic regular expressions by default. Key symbols: - . – any single character. - ^ – start of line. - $ – end of line. - * – zero or more of the preceding character/group. - [abc] – any one of a, b, c. - [^abc] – any one not a, b, c. - \ – escape special chars.

In extended mode (-E): - + – one or more. - ? – zero or one. - | – OR. - () – grouping.

For example, to match lines starting with a digit:

grep '^[0-9]' file

We’ll use these in Bandit levels.

docs.title: 3.5 find – The Ultimate File Finder
docs.text:
find searches the filesystem by almost any attribute. Its syntax is:

find [path...] [expression]

If you omit path, it defaults to the current directory. The expression is composed of options, tests, and actions.

Most common tests: - -name pattern – filename (supports globbing *, ?, etc.). Use -iname for case‑insensitive. - -type f (file), -type d (directory), -type l (link). - -size +10k (greater than 10 kilobytes), -size -5M, -size 100c (100 bytes exactly). - -user name – owned by user. - -group name – owned by group. - -perm mode – permissions exactly (e.g., -perm 644). Prefix with - for “at least these bits set” (e.g., -perm -u+x). - -mtime n – modified n*24 hours ago (+n greater, -n less). - -newer file – newer than reference file.

Actions (default is -print if none given): - -print – print filename (default). - -exec command {} \; – execute command on each file, {} is placeholder. The \; ends the command. - -delete – delete matching files (use with caution!).

Examples:

# Find all .txt files in home
find ~ -name "*.txt"

# Find files larger than 1 MB
find /var/log -type f -size +1M

# Find files with 777 permissions
find / -perm 777 2>/dev/null   # 2>/dev/null suppresses permission denied errors

# Find and delete all .tmp files in /tmp older than 7 days
find /tmp -name "*.tmp" -mtime +7 -delete

Important: find often generates “Permission denied” errors when scanning directories you can’t read. Always redirect stderr to /dev/null for cleaner output:

find / -name "shadow" 2>/dev/null

docs.title: 3.6 Combining find with xargs or -exec
docs.text:
Sometimes you want to process the files found. xargs takes stdin and constructs command lines:

find . -name "*.log" -print0 | xargs -0 rm

The -print0 and -0 handle filenames with spaces safely. But -exec is cleaner for simple tasks.

docs.title: Part IV – Bandit Levels 5–10 Intensive Walkthrough
docs.text: Now you’ll apply every new command. Each Bandit level is a puzzle that demands you to use the tools above. Connect via SSH as before.

docs.title: 4.1 Bandit Level 5 → Level 6
docs.text:
Goal: Password is stored in a file somewhere under the inhere directory and has all of the following properties: - human-readable - 1033 bytes in size - not executable

So we need to find a file of exactly 1033 bytes that is not executable. The human‑readable condition is a hint to skip binary; we can use find to locate 1033‑byte files and then inspect them. Also “not executable” means the executable bit is not set for anyone. We can enforce with ! -executable (if find supports it) or check permissions manually. But simplest: find all files of size 1033c, then file them.

Connect with ssh bandit5@bandit.labs.overthewire.org -p 2220, password from level4.

Once in:

ls
cd inhere
ls

You see many directories: maybehere00 through maybehere19. The password file could be in any of them.

First, let’s locate files of exactly 1033 bytes:

find . -type f -size 1033c

All files matching will be listed. Usually there’s only one. (Note: sometimes you get multiple, but the non‑executable condition selects the right one.)

Now check which ones are not executable. You can do ls -l or use find with ! -perm -111 (at least one execute bit). -perm -111 means any file with any execute bit set. We want the opposite:

find . -type f -size 1033c ! -perm -111

This should return exactly one file, say ./maybehere07/.file2. Read it with cat and retrieve the password.

Alternatively, manually cat the output of first find. If multiple, look for the one that is ASCII. But the permissions method is more precise.

docs.title: 4.2 Bandit Level 6 → Level 7
docs.text:
Goal: The password is stored somewhere on the server (not necessarily under inhere) and has all of the following properties: - owned by user bandit7 - owned by group bandit6 - 33 bytes in size

Now we need to search the entire filesystem. Connect as bandit6.

ssh bandit6@bandit.labs.overthewire.org -p 2220

Run:

find / -user bandit7 -group bandit6 -size 33c 2>/dev/null
  • -user bandit7 – owned by user bandit7.
  • -group bandit6 – group bandit6.
  • -size 33c – exactly 33 bytes.
  • 2>/dev/null – discard all the “Permission denied” noise.

You’ll likely get one result: /var/lib/dpkg/info/bandit7.password or similar. cat it and grab the password.

If you don’t specify the root path /, find only searches current directory, so you must specify /. This is a common CTF pattern.

docs.title: 4.3 Bandit Level 7 → Level 8
docs.text:
Goal: The password for the next level is stored in the file data.txt next to the word millionth.

We need to search for a specific string in a file. Connect as bandit7.

ssh bandit7@bandit.labs.overthewire.org -p 2220
ls
cat data.txt | grep millionth

Or more directly:

grep millionth data.txt

The output is something like: millionth TESKZC0XvTetK0S9xNwm25STk5iWrBvP. That’s the password.

docs.title: 4.4 Bandit Level 8 → Level 9
docs.text:
Goal: Password is stored in data.txt and is the only line of text that occurs only once.

So we need to find the unique line. sort | uniq -u is the classic approach. uniq works on sorted input; -u prints only lines that are unique.

Connect as bandit8:

ssh bandit8@bandit.labs.overthewire.org -p 2220

sort data.txt | uniq -u

That single line is the password.

docs.title: 4.5 Bandit Level 9 → Level 10
docs.text:
Goal: Password is one of the few human-readable strings in data.txt, preceded by several ‘=’ characters.

data.txt is largely binary data. We can use strings to extract readable sequences, then grep for lines containing === (the hint “preceded by several ‘=’ characters” means the password is near a block of equals).

Connect as bandit9:

ssh bandit9@bandit.labs.overthewire.org -p 2220

strings data.txt | grep '==='

You’ll see a few lines, one of which is something like:

========== the*passwordishiddenwith===== G7w8LIi6J3kTb8A7j9LgrywtEUlyyp6s

Or a string that clearly looks like a password. Copy it.

docs.title: 4.6 Bandit Level 10 → Level 11 (End of this Week’s Bandit Progression)
docs.text:
Goal: Password is stored in data.txt, which contains base64 encoded data.

Base64 is a common encoding used in web and CTF challenges. The base64 command can decode it.

Connect as bandit10:

ssh bandit10@bandit.labs.overthewire.org -p 2220

cat data.txt          # showing base64 encoded string
base64 -d data.txt    # decode; if command is called base64, use base64 -d. Some systems use 'base64 --decode'

The output: “The password is …”, with the password.

Alternative: cat data.txt | base64 -d.

This wraps up Bandit 5‑10. You’ve now practiced find with size, permissions, user/group, grep, sort | uniq, strings, and base64 decoding – all essential for real enumeration and CTF solving.

docs.title: Part V – Week 2 Extra Resource and Consolidation
5.1 Recommended External Material
docs.text:
The schedule mentions “Watch Linux basics videos from FreeCodeCamp (free YouTube ‘Linux for Beginners’)”. This is a full‑length course; you don’t have to watch it all, but if you have time, it reinforces what you’re learning. Since you’re already doing hands‑on, I recommend watching the sections that cover: - Permissions (chmod, chown) - grep and find (the video likely has a dedicated segment) - Piping and redirection

The channel’s “Linux for Ethical Hackers” or “Linux Crash Course” might also be helpful. But the Bandit labs are the best practice.

docs.title: 5.2 Milestone Verification
docs.text:
You should now be able to: - Look at an ls -l output and instantly describe who can do what. - Change permissions to restrict or allow access. - Find any file on the system by name, size, owner, or permission. - Use grep to filter output and files. - Chain commands with |. - Solve Bandit 0‑10 without assistance.

Try this challenge on your own VM without the internet: 1. Create a directory ~/week2lab with three subdirectories dir1, dir2, dir3. 2. In dir1, create a file secret.txt containing “the eagle flies at midnight” and set permissions so only you can read and write it, nobody else can read, write, or execute (chmod 600). 3. In dir2, create a file -findme (with leading dash) and make it executable by everyone, then list directory to see permissions via ls -l (use ./-findme to run it after you make it executable; put #!/bin/bash\necho "found" in it). 4. In dir3, create 50 files named file1…file50 with random contents. Make exactly one file 1033 bytes (you can use truncate -s 1033 fileX or fallocate). Then use find to locate the 1033‑byte file. 5. Use a pipe to list all files in /etc that end with .conf, then count them (hint: ls /etc/*.conf | wc -l). 6. Search the entire VM for any file named hosts (should be /etc/hosts) using find, suppressing errors.

If you can do all that without peeking, congratulations – you’re now terminal‑fluent enough to continue.

docs.title: Part VI – Cheat Sheet for Week 2
docs.text: Command / Syntax Meaning ls -l long listing with permissions chmod 755 file set rwxr-xr-x chmod u+x file add execute to owner chmod go-w file remove write from group and others chown user file change owner chgrp group file change group whoami / id user identity grep "pattern" file search pattern in file grep -rni "pattern" dir recursive, case insensitive, line numbers command | grep "p" filter command output find /path -name "*.txt" find by name find / -type f -size 100c find 100‑byte files find / -user bob -group staff 2>/dev/null find by owner/group, suppress errors sort file | uniq -u show unique lines only strings binaryfile print readable strings base64 -d file decode base64 > / >> redirect output (truncate/append) 2>/dev/null discard stderr command1 | command2 pipe stdout of 1 to stdin of 2 xargs build commands from stdin

docs.title: Part VII – What’s Next?
docs.text:
Next week (Week 3) will be all about scripting and process control. You’ll learn: - Bash scripting fundamentals: variables, conditionals, loops. - Job control (background, foreground, ps, kill). - And you’ll tackle Bandit levels 11‑15.

But for now, make sure you’ve internalised the Week 2 content – especially find and grep, because they’ll be used every single day in CTFs. Practice the Bandit levels multiple times until you can solve them with your eyes closed.

When you’re ready to move on, I’ll take you deep into scripting and process management. But remember: real hackers don’t rush, they master the foundations.

docs.title: Week 3: Scripting & Process Control – From Command‑Line User to Automator
docs.text: This week transforms you from a person who types commands into someone who writes programs (shell scripts) to tell the computer exactly what to do, and who can control running processes like a sysadmin. By Friday, you will have authored a real backup script, mastered job control, and applied your new text‑processing skills to crack five more Bandit levels. The theme is automation and control.

docs.title: 1.1 What Is a Shell Script?
docs.text:
A shell script is simply a plain‑text file containing a sequence of commands that the shell can execute one after the other. Instead of typing them interactively every time, you save them, make the file executable, and run it. This is the heart of automation.

The default shell on Ubuntu is Bash. A script can be as simple as:

#!/bin/bash
echo "Hello, world!"

The first line #!/bin/bash is called the shebang (or hash‑bang). It tells the operating system which interpreter to use to run the script. Without it, the script may still run if you execute it with bash script.sh, but making it executable and relying on the shebang is the standard.

docs.title: 1.2 Your First Script
docs.text:
Open your terminal. Create your scripts in a dedicated folder:

mkdir ~/scripts
cd ~/scripts
nano hello.sh

Inside nano, type:

#!/bin/bash
# This is a comment. Comments start with #.
echo "Hello, hacker!"
echo "Current date and time: $(date)"

Save (Ctrl+O, Enter) and exit (Ctrl+X). Now make the script executable:

chmod +x hello.sh

Run it with:

./hello.sh

You should see two lines printed. The $(date) is a command substitution that captures the output of the date command and inserts it into the string. You can also use backticks: `date` but $() is preferred because it nests cleanly.

docs.title: 1.3 Variables
docs.text:
Variables store data. In Bash, you assign a value without spaces around the =:

name="Ada"
age=25
echo "Hello, $name. You are $age years old."
  • Use $variable or ${variable} to access the value. The curly braces are needed when the variable is followed immediately by other text: echo "${name}ist" would print “Adaist”.
  • Variable names are case‑sensitive, by convention UPPERCASE for environment variables and lowercase for script‑local ones.

Quoting rules: - Double quotes " allow variable and command substitution. - Single quotes ' treat everything literally. - Example: bash echo "Today is $USER" # Today is student echo 'Today is $USER' # Today is $USER
